COMPLETE SERIES · 16 PARTS
PART 01 / 13

Web Development Foundations

Everything that happens before you write a single line of React or Express. Understanding these fundamentals is what separates developers who debug in 10 minutes from those who spend 10 hours. These are also the most common interview topics.

HTTP DNS CORS Async/Await JSON TCP/IP Status Codes Localhost
1.1

What is Web Development?

📖 DEFINITION
📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Web Development is the discipline of building applications that are accessed through a web browser or network. It is divided into three primary domains: Frontend (client-side — what users see and interact with), Backend (server-side — business logic, data processing, authentication), and Database (persistent storage of structured data). A developer who works across all three domains is called a Full-Stack Developer.

💡 ANALOGY
💡 REAL-WORLD COMPARISON

Think of a restaurant. The Frontend is everything the customer experiences — the menu, the décor, the plates, how the staff presents your food. The Backend is the kitchen — the chefs following recipes, managing orders, applying business rules (no substitutions, happy hour pricing). The Database is the pantry and inventory — raw ingredients stored, tracked, and retrieved on demand. A full-stack developer built the entire restaurant from scratch.

⚙️ HOW THE THREE LAYERS WORK TOGETHER

In FitoGlobe, when a user clicks "Log Workout", here is exactly what happens across all three layers:

▸ Full-Stack Request Flow — FitoGlobe "Log Workout"
👤 User
localhost:5173
──▶
⚛️ React Frontend
WorkoutPage.jsx
──▶
🖥️ Express Backend
localhost:5000
──▶
🗄️ PostgreSQL
via Prisma ORM
◀────────────────── RESPONSE FLOWS BACK ──────────────────▶
FRONTEND DOES:
• Renders HTML/CSS
• Handles user events
• Manages UI state
• Sends HTTP requests
• Displays response data
BACKEND DOES:
• Validates input data
• Checks JWT auth token
• Runs business logic
• Queries the database
• Returns JSON response
DATABASE DOES:
• Stores workout rows
• Enforces constraints
• Handles relations
• Indexes for speed
• ACID transactions
  1. 1
    User Interaction (Frontend) User fills a workout form in React and clicks "Save". React captures the onClick event and calls a function that prepares the data.
  2. 2
    HTTP Request Sent The frontend uses axios to send a POST request to http://localhost:5000/api/workouts with the workout data as a JSON body, plus a JWT token in the Authorization header.
  3. 3
    Backend Receives Request Express receives the request, runs it through middleware (CORS check → JSON parsing → JWT verification), then hands it to the workout controller.
  4. 4
    Database Operation The controller uses Prisma to run prisma.workout.create(), which generates and executes a SQL INSERT statement in PostgreSQL.
  5. 5
    Response Returns PostgreSQL confirms the insert → Prisma returns the saved object → Express sends a 201 Created JSON response → React receives it and updates the UI state → The workout appears on screen.
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Web Development Basics
What is the difference between Frontend, Backend, and Full-Stack development?
Frontend development focuses on everything a user directly sees and interacts with in a browser — the layout, styling, animations, buttons, and forms. Technologies include HTML, CSS, and JavaScript frameworks like React. Backend development handles server-side logic: processing requests, authenticating users, applying business rules, and communicating with databases. Technologies include Node.js, Express, Python/Django, and Java/Spring. Full-Stack development encompasses both, meaning the developer can build an entire feature end-to-end — from designing the UI in React to writing the API in Express to designing the database schema in PostgreSQL. In FitoGlobe, for example, building the "Log Workout" feature required frontend work (React form), backend work (Express route + controller), and database work (Prisma schema + query).
Why do we separate the frontend and backend into different servers instead of having one combined server?
Separation of concerns is the core reason — each server does one job well. The frontend server (Vite on localhost:5173) serves static files and handles UI rendering. The backend server (Express on localhost:5000) handles data and business logic. This separation enables independent scaling — if thousands of users are browsing but only a few are saving data, you can scale the frontend without touching the backend. It also enables team separation — frontend and backend developers work independently without stepping on each other's code. Additionally, the same backend API can serve multiple clients — a React web app, a mobile app, and a third-party service — without any changes. In production, FitoGlobe deploys the frontend to Vercel (optimized for static assets and CDN delivery) and the backend to Railway (optimized for long-running Node.js processes).
What happens between a user clicking a button and seeing new data on screen? Walk me through every step.
When a user clicks "Save Workout" in FitoGlobe, the following chain of events occurs: (1) The browser's JavaScript event listener fires the onClick handler. (2) The React component calls the workoutService.create() function which uses Axios to construct an HTTP POST request with the workout data as a JSON body and the JWT token in the Authorization header. (3) The browser sends TCP packets containing this HTTP request across the network to the backend server. (4) Express receives the request and passes it through the middleware chain — CORS headers are checked, the JSON body is parsed, and the JWT token is verified. (5) The request reaches the createWorkout controller function. (6) Prisma ORM constructs and executes a SQL INSERT statement against PostgreSQL. (7) PostgreSQL stores the row and returns the newly created record. (8) Prisma returns a JavaScript object. (9) The controller sends a 201 Created HTTP response with the workout JSON. (10) Axios on the frontend receives the response, the .then() or await resolves, React's setState updates the workouts array, and React re-renders the list — the new workout appears on screen.
1.2

How the Internet Works

📖 DEFINITION
📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

The Internet is a global network of interconnected computers that communicate using a standardized suite of protocols called TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol). Data is broken into small units called packets, transmitted independently through various network paths, and reassembled at the destination. The Domain Name System (DNS) acts as the internet's phonebook, translating human-readable domain names (e.g. fitoglobe.com) into IP addresses that computers use to locate each other.

💡 ANALOGY
💡 POSTAL SYSTEM ANALOGY

The internet works like an international postal system. When you send a large document, the post office breaks it into multiple envelopes (packets), each labeled with a sender and destination address. Each envelope travels independently — some go through Delhi, some through Mumbai — and they're reassembled in order when they all arrive. Your domain name (fitoglobe.com) is like a person's name. The DNS system is like looking up their actual street address in a phonebook so you know exactly where to send the letter.

⚙️ HOW IT WORKS — DNS Resolution + TCP/IP Step by Step

When you type fitoglobe.com in your browser and press Enter, here is every single thing that happens:

▸ DNS Resolution Process
Browser Cache
Check local
DNS cache first
→
OS Resolver
Check /etc/hosts
& OS cache
→
ISP DNS Server
Your ISP's
resolver
↓ if not found ↓
Root DNS Server
13 root servers
worldwide
→
TLD Server (.com)
Handles all
.com domains
↓
Authoritative NS
Returns actual
IP address
Browser gets IP → 185.199.108.153
Now initiates TCP connection to this IP on port 443 (HTTPS)
  1. 1
    Browser Cache Check Browser checks if it already resolved fitoglobe.com recently. DNS records have a TTL (Time To Live) — if not expired, it uses the cached IP instantly.
  2. 2
    OS Cache & Hosts File If browser cache misses, the OS checks its own DNS cache, then the /etc/hosts file (Windows: C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts). This is why adding 127.0.0.1 fitoglobe.local to your hosts file makes it resolve locally.
  3. 3
    ISP Recursive Resolver Your ISP's DNS server receives the query and begins the recursive lookup process on your behalf.
  4. 4
    Root Nameservers The resolver asks one of the 13 root nameservers: "Where are the .com servers?" The root server responds with the address of the .com TLD (Top-Level Domain) server.
  5. 5
    TLD Nameserver The .com TLD server is asked: "Who is authoritative for fitoglobe.com?" It responds with the address of the domain's authoritative nameserver (usually the hosting provider, e.g. Vercel's DNS).
  6. 6
    Authoritative Nameserver Returns IP Vercel's authoritative DNS returns the actual IP address: e.g. 76.76.21.21. This is cached with the TTL value.
  7. 7
    TCP Handshake Browser initiates a TCP connection to that IP using a 3-way handshake: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. This establishes a reliable connection before any data is sent.
  8. 8
    TLS Handshake (HTTPS) After TCP is established, a TLS handshake occurs: certificates are exchanged, encryption algorithms are negotiated, and a shared encryption key is established. All subsequent data is encrypted.
  9. 9
    HTTP Request & Response Browser sends the HTTP GET request. Server responds with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files. Browser parses and renders the page.

What Are Packets?

When your browser sends an HTTP request or receives a large response (like FitoGlobe's React bundle), the data is broken into packets — small chunks typically 1,500 bytes each. Each packet contains:

▸ Anatomy of a Network Packet
HEADER
Source IP
Dest IP
Packet #
Checksum
PAYLOAD (actual data)
HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: application/json {"workouts": [...], "user": {...}}
TRAILER
Error
Check
CRC
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Internet & DNS
What happens when you type a URL in the browser and press Enter?
This is one of the most famous interview questions in software engineering. The process begins with the browser checking its local DNS cache for the domain's IP address. If not found, it queries the OS cache and the local hosts file. If still unresolved, it contacts the ISP's recursive DNS resolver, which performs a hierarchical lookup through root nameservers → TLD nameservers → authoritative nameservers until the IP address is obtained. Once the browser has the IP, it initiates a TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) to establish a reliable connection. For HTTPS sites (which includes FitoGlobe in production), a TLS handshake follows to negotiate encryption. Finally, the browser sends an HTTP GET request, receives the HTML response, parses it, and begins fetching additional resources (CSS, JavaScript, images) to render the complete page.
What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is a connection-oriented protocol that guarantees reliable, ordered delivery of packets. Before any data is sent, a connection is established via the three-way handshake. If packets are lost, TCP retransmits them. This makes it ideal for web requests, API calls, file transfers — situations where data integrity is critical. FitoGlobe's API calls use TCP. UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is connectionless — it fires packets without establishing a connection or guaranteeing delivery. This makes it faster but unreliable. UDP is used in situations where speed matters more than perfection: live video streaming, online gaming, DNS queries, and video calls. A dropped frame in a video call is acceptable; a dropped authentication packet is not.
What is an IP address and why does it matter for web development?
An IP address is a unique numerical identifier assigned to every device on a network, used to locate and communicate with that device. IPv4 addresses are 32-bit numbers written as four octets (e.g. 192.168.1.1), while IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses to accommodate the vastly larger number of internet-connected devices. For web developers, IP addresses matter in several ways: 127.0.0.1 (loopback address) is your own machine — when you run FitoGlobe locally on localhost:5000, your backend is listening on this IP. In production, your domain's A record points to the IP address of your server (e.g. Railway's server IP). Load balancers distribute traffic across multiple IP addresses. Rate limiting is often implemented per-IP. Understanding IP addresses also helps with debugging CORS, proxies, and network configuration.
1.3

Localhost & Ports

📖 DEFINITION
📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Localhost (resolved to IP address 127.0.0.1) is a loopback network interface — a virtual network that routes back to the same machine. It allows a computer to communicate with itself using standard network protocols, without any external network traffic. A port is a 16-bit number (0–65535) that identifies a specific process or service on a host machine. Ports allow a single IP address to run multiple network services simultaneously — your React dev server, Express backend, and PostgreSQL database all run on the same machine but on different ports.

💡 ANALOGY
💡 APARTMENT BUILDING ANALOGY

Your computer (IP 127.0.0.1) is like an apartment building. The IP address is the building's street address — it identifies the building. The port number is the apartment number — it identifies which specific resident (service) inside the building you want to reach. Port 5173 is your React frontend's apartment. Port 5000 is your Express backend's apartment. Port 5432 is PostgreSQL's apartment. Everyone shares the same address, but the apartment number directs the visitor to the right door.

⚙️ HOW LOCALHOST WORKS IN FITOGLOBE
▸ FitoGlobe Local Development — Port Layout
:5173
Vite Dev Server
Serves React app
Hot Module Replacement
Proxies /api → :5000
Frontend
:5000
Express Server
Handles API routes
Auth middleware
Database queries
Backend
:5432
PostgreSQL
Default Postgres port
Prisma connects here
Stores all app data
Database

Why Port 5173 and 5000?

These port numbers are not random — they have specific reasons:

PortServiceWhy This NumberWho Assigned It
80HTTPIANA assigned standard HTTP portIANA (reserved)
443HTTPSIANA assigned standard HTTPS portIANA (reserved)
5432PostgreSQLDefault PostgreSQL portPostgreSQL team
27017MongoDBDefault MongoDB portMongoDB team
3000React/NodeHistorical convention for dev serversCommunity convention
5173ViteVite's chosen default (5173 = "VITE" on phone keypad)Vite team
5000Express (FitoGlobe)User-chosen, avoids conflicts below 1024You (in .env)
⚠️ IMPORTANT

Ports 0–1023 are "well-known ports" reserved by the OS. Running a server on port 80 or 443 locally requires admin/root privileges. Always use ports above 1024 for development servers. If you get EADDRINUSE: address already in use :::5000, another process is already using that port. Run lsof -i :5000 (Mac/Linux) or netstat -ano | findstr :5000 (Windows) to find and kill it.

💻 CODE EXAMPLE
backend/server.js — Port Configuration
JS
1// Load environment variables from .env file
2require('dotenv').config();
3
4// Read PORT from environment or default to 5000
5const PORT = process.env.PORT || 5000;
6
7// Tell Express to listen on all network interfaces (0.0.0.0)
8// '0.0.0.0' means: accept connections from any IP, not just localhost
9// This is important for Railway/Render deployment
10app.listen(PORT, '0.0.0.0', () => {
11 console.log(`Server running on port ${PORT}`); // "Server running on port 5000"
12});
frontend/vite.config.js — Proxy (solves CORS in dev)
JS
1export default defineConfig({
2 plugins: [react()],
3 server: {
4 port: 5173, // Run Vite on this port (default)
5 proxy: {
6 // Any request starting with /api goes to the backend
7 '/api': {
8 target: 'http://localhost:5000', // Forward to Express server
9 changeOrigin: true, // Rewrite the host header to match target
10 // Now fetch('/api/workouts') hits localhost:5000/api/workouts
11 // This bypasses CORS because it's server-to-server, not browser-to-server
12 }
13 }
14 }
15});
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Localhost & Ports
What is the difference between localhost, 127.0.0.1, and 0.0.0.0?
These three addresses serve distinct purposes. localhost is a hostname that the OS resolves to a loopback address — typically 127.0.0.1 for IPv4 or ::1 for IPv6. It always refers to your own machine. 127.0.0.1 is the specific IPv4 loopback address — network traffic sent to it never leaves the machine and is looped back internally. When you run Express locally, it binds to 127.0.0.1 by default, accepting only local connections. 0.0.0.0 is a special address meaning "bind to all available network interfaces." When Railway runs your Express server, it starts it on 0.0.0.0, meaning it accepts connections from any IP — from the internet. This is why in FitoGlobe's production config, we use app.listen(PORT, '0.0.0.0') rather than the localhost-only default.
Why does React run on port 5173 and Express on port 5000? Why can't they share a port?
A port can only be bound to one process at a time on a given IP address. If both React's Vite dev server and Express tried to listen on port 3000, the second one to start would get an EADDRINUSE error. They need separate ports because they are completely different processes with different responsibilities. Vite serves static files and handles Hot Module Replacement for the frontend. Express handles API requests, authentication, and database queries for the backend. By giving them different ports, both can run simultaneously during development. In production, this separation is maintained differently — the React app is built into static files hosted on Vercel (port 443 in the cloud), while Express runs on Railway (also port 443, different domain). The browser calls both but distinguishes them by domain name.
1.4

HTTP Protocol

📖 DEFINITION
📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is a stateless, application-layer protocol that defines the rules for communication between a client (typically a browser or API consumer) and a server. HTTP operates on a request-response model: the client sends a request containing a method, URL, headers, and optional body; the server processes it and responds with a status code, headers, and optional body. HTTPS (HTTP Secure) is HTTP transmitted over a TLS/SSL-encrypted connection, ensuring data confidentiality and integrity.

💡 ANALOGY
💡 WAITER ANALOGY

HTTP is like a formal protocol between a customer (client) and a restaurant (server). You must follow the protocol: choose a specific request type (GET = "I want to see the menu", POST = "I want to order this", PUT = "Change my entire order", PATCH = "Just change the drink", DELETE = "Cancel my order"). You attach a table number (URL) and a note about what format you understand (headers: "Accept: application/json"). The waiter (HTTP) delivers your request to the kitchen (server) and brings back the response (your food, or an error message if the item is unavailable).

⚙️ HTTP REQUEST/RESPONSE ANATOMY
▸ Complete HTTP Request — POST /api/workouts (FitoGlobe)
REQUEST →
POST /api/workouts HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost:5000
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGci...
Accept: application/json
{
  "name": "Chest Day",
  "exercises": [...],
  "duration": 60
}
🟢 Request Line: Method + Path + Protocol
🔵 Headers: Metadata about the request
🟡 Body: Actual data (POST/PUT/PATCH only)
← RESPONSE
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Content-Type: application/json
X-Request-ID: abc123
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
{
  "id": "clx8k2...",
  "name": "Chest Day",
  "createdAt": "2024-01-15"
}
🟢 Status Line: Protocol + Code + Message
🔵 Headers: Metadata about the response
🟡 Body: Returned data (JSON for APIs)

HTTP Methods — Deep Dive

MethodPurposeHas Body?Idempotent?FitoGlobe Example
GET Retrieve data — NEVER modify data with GET No Yes GET /api/workouts — Fetch all workouts
POST Create a new resource Yes No POST /api/workouts — Create new workout
PUT Replace an entire resource (full update) Yes Yes PUT /api/workouts/:id — Replace workout completely
PATCH Partial update — only send changed fields Yes Yes PATCH /api/user/profile — Update just the username
DELETE Remove a resource No Yes DELETE /api/workouts/:id — Delete a workout
ℹ️ WHAT IS IDEMPOTENT?

An idempotent operation produces the same result whether you do it once or 100 times. DELETE /workouts/123 is idempotent — the workout is deleted whether you call it once or ten times. POST /workouts is NOT idempotent — calling it 10 times creates 10 duplicate workouts. This distinction matters for retry logic: if a GET, PUT, DELETE, or PATCH request times out, it's safe to retry. If a POST times out, retrying might create duplicates.

💻 CODE EXAMPLE
backend/routes/workout.routes.js — All HTTP Methods
JS
1const router = require('express').Router(); // Create a mini Express app for routing
2const { protect } = require('../middleware/auth'); // JWT auth middleware
3const ctrl = require('../controllers/workout.controller');
4
5// GET /api/workouts — retrieve all workouts for logged-in user
6// protect middleware runs first: verifies JWT, sets req.user
7router.get('/', protect, ctrl.getWorkouts);
8
9// GET /api/workouts/:id — retrieve ONE workout by its ID
10// :id is a URL parameter, accessible as req.params.id
11router.get('/:id', protect, ctrl.getWorkoutById);
12
13// POST /api/workouts — create a new workout
14// req.body will contain the workout data from the frontend
15router.post('/', protect, ctrl.createWorkout);
16
17// PATCH /api/workouts/:id — update specific fields of a workout
18// Only send the fields you want to change, not the whole object
19router.patch('/:id', protect, ctrl.updateWorkout);
20
21// DELETE /api/workouts/:id — permanently remove a workout
22router.delete('/:id', protect, ctrl.deleteWorkout);
23
24module.exports = router; // Export so server.js can use it
❌ COMMON MISTAKES
  • Using GET to modify data (e.g., GET /api/deleteUser/123)

    GET requests should NEVER have side effects. Browsers, CDNs, and caching layers treat GET as safe and may cache or pre-fetch it — inadvertently triggering your delete. Always use DELETE for deletions, POST for creates.

    Fix

    Map actions to correct methods: read → GET, create → POST, update → PATCH/PUT, delete → DELETE.

  • Sending sensitive data in the URL query string

    URLs are logged in server logs, browser history, and Referer headers. Never put passwords, tokens, or sensitive data in GET parameters like /login?password=abc123.

    Fix

    Send sensitive data in the request body (POST/PUT) or headers (Authorization). These are not logged by default and are encrypted in HTTPS.

  • Confusing PUT and PATCH — using PUT for partial updates

    PUT replaces the entire resource. If you PUT {"name": "Chest Day"}, all other fields (duration, exercises) get deleted/nulled. PATCH is for partial updates.

    Fix

    Use PATCH when you only want to update specific fields. Use PUT only when you're replacing the entire resource object.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — HTTP Protocol
What does it mean that HTTP is "stateless"?
HTTP being stateless means that each request-response pair is completely independent. The server retains no memory of previous requests. If you make 100 API calls, the server treats each one as if it has never seen you before. This is a fundamental design choice that makes HTTP servers highly scalable — they don't need to maintain session state per user, so any server instance can handle any request. However, real applications need state (you want to stay logged in). This problem is solved at the application layer through tokens (JWT) or sessions (stored server-side). In FitoGlobe, every single API request includes the JWT token in the Authorization header — because HTTP itself doesn't remember your login, you must prove your identity on every single request.
What is the difference between HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3?
HTTP/1.1 (1997) established the standard: persistent connections, chunked transfer, pipelining. However it suffers from "head-of-line blocking" — if one request stalls, all queued requests behind it wait. HTTP/2 (2015) introduced multiplexing — multiple requests can be in-flight simultaneously over a single connection. It also introduced header compression (HPACK) and server push. This significantly improved performance, especially for loading large JavaScript apps like React. HTTP/3 (2022) replaced TCP with QUIC (a UDP-based protocol), eliminating TCP-level head-of-line blocking and improving performance on unreliable networks. Most modern CDNs (Cloudflare, Vercel's edge network) support HTTP/3. When you deploy FitoGlobe to Vercel, your users automatically benefit from HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 without any code changes.
What are HTTP headers and why are they important?
HTTP headers are key-value pairs that carry metadata about the request or response — they are separate from the actual data (body). They tell both sides how to interpret and handle the communication. In FitoGlobe, critical headers include: Content-Type: application/json which tells Express to parse the body as JSON (without this, req.body is undefined). Authorization: Bearer [JWT] carries the authentication token checked by the protect middleware. Access-Control-Allow-Origin controls CORS behavior. Cache-Control tells browsers and CDNs how long to cache a response. Without the correct headers, even a perfectly formatted request will fail — Express won't parse the JSON body without Content-Type: application/json, and the auth middleware will reject requests without a valid Authorization header.
1.5

HTTP Status Codes

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

HTTP Status Codes are three-digit numeric codes included in every HTTP response that indicate the outcome of the request. They are grouped into five classes by their first digit: 1xx (Informational), 2xx (Success), 3xx (Redirection), 4xx (Client Error — the client did something wrong), and 5xx (Server Error — the server failed to fulfill a valid request).

2xx — Success Codes

200
OK
Standard success response. Used for GET requests that return data, and PUT/PATCH that update data. In FitoGlobe: GET /api/workouts returns 200 with the workouts array.
201
Created
Resource was successfully created. Use this for POST requests. In FitoGlobe: POST /api/workouts returns 201 with the newly created workout object.
204
No Content
Success but no response body. Perfect for DELETE operations. In FitoGlobe: DELETE /api/workouts/:id returns 204 — deletion confirmed, nothing to return.

4xx — Client Error Codes

400
Bad Request
The request was malformed or invalid. Validation failed. In FitoGlobe: sending a workout without a required "name" field returns 400 with a validation error message.
401
Unauthorized
Authentication required. No valid token provided. In FitoGlobe: accessing /api/workouts without a JWT token in the Authorization header returns 401. NOT "you don't have permission" — that's 403.
403
Forbidden
Authenticated but not authorized. In FitoGlobe: User A trying to delete User B's workout — the token is valid but this action is not permitted.
404
Not Found
The requested resource doesn't exist. In FitoGlobe: GET /api/workouts/nonexistent-id returns 404 when no workout matches that ID.
409
Conflict
Request conflicts with current state. In FitoGlobe: trying to register with an email that already exists in the database returns 409 Conflict.
422
Unprocessable Entity
Request is well-formed but semantically invalid. Like 400 but more specific — the syntax is correct, but the data fails business logic validation.
429
Too Many Requests
Rate limit exceeded. In FitoGlobe AI features: if a user calls the AI endpoint too many times per minute, return 429 to prevent API cost abuse.

5xx — Server Error Codes

500
Internal Server Error
Generic server error — something crashed on the server. In FitoGlobe: an unhandled exception in a controller (e.g., Prisma throws an unexpected error) results in 500. Never expose the actual error message to clients.
502
Bad Gateway
The upstream server returned an invalid response. Common when Railway's backend is starting up after a cold start and the reverse proxy can't reach it yet.
503
Service Unavailable
Server is temporarily unavailable (overloaded, deploying, maintenance). Should include a Retry-After header. Common during deployments.
💻 CODE EXAMPLE
backend/controllers/workout.controller.js — Proper Status Codes
JS
1const getWorkoutById = async (req, res) => {
2 try {
3 // req.params.id is the :id from the URL /api/workouts/:id
4 const workout = await prisma.workout.findUnique({
5 where: { id: req.params.id }
6 });
7
8 // If Prisma returns null, the record doesn't exist → 404
9 if (!workout) {
10 return res.status(404).json({ message: 'Workout not found' });
11 }
12
13 // Check ownership: does this workout belong to the requesting user?
14 // req.user was set by the JWT protect middleware
15 if (workout.userId !== req.user.id) {
16 return res.status(403).json({ message: 'Not authorized' }); // 403 not 401!
17 }
18
19 // All checks passed — return the workout with 200 OK
20 res.status(200).json({ data: workout });
21
22 } catch (error) {
23 // Database error, unexpected crash → 500 Internal Server Error
24 // NEVER send error.message to client — it may expose internals!
25 console.error(error); // Log full error server-side
26 res.status(500).json({ message: 'Server error' }); // Generic message to client
27 }
28};
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — HTTP Status Codes
What is the difference between 401 and 403?
This is a very common interview question and a frequent point of confusion. 401 Unauthorized means the request lacks valid authentication credentials — the server doesn't know who you are. Despite the name "Unauthorized," it actually means "Unauthenticated." In FitoGlobe, if you try to access /api/workouts without including a JWT token in the Authorization header, you get 401 because the server cannot identify you. 403 Forbidden means the server knows exactly who you are (authentication succeeded) but you do not have permission to perform the requested action. In FitoGlobe, if User A (properly authenticated with a valid JWT) tries to delete User B's workout, the server returns 403 — your identity is confirmed, but this action is not permitted for your account. Remember: 401 = "Who are you?", 403 = "I know who you are, but you can't do this."
Why should we never send the actual server error (stack trace) to the client in a 500 response?
Exposing stack traces or internal error details to clients is a serious security vulnerability. A stack trace reveals your file structure (e.g., /home/ubuntu/fitoglobe/backend/controllers/workout.controller.js:42), library versions (attackers research known vulnerabilities), database schema details (table names, column names), and sometimes even sensitive configuration values. An attacker who receives detailed error messages can use this information to craft more targeted attacks. The correct pattern, as used in FitoGlobe's error handler, is to: (1) log the complete error server-side using console.error or a logging service like Sentry, (2) send a generic, non-revealing message to the client like { "message": "Internal server error" }, and (3) include a request ID that the user can provide when reporting the issue, which you can use to look up the actual error in your logs.
1.6

JSON — JavaScript Object Notation

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight, text-based data interchange format that is both human-readable and machine-parseable. It is derived from JavaScript object literal syntax but is language-independent — virtually every programming language can parse and generate JSON. JSON is the de facto standard for data exchange in REST APIs, replacing XML due to its conciseness and simpler structure. In FitoGlobe, all data transmitted between the React frontend and the Express backend is serialized as JSON.

💡 COMMON LANGUAGE ANALOGY

JSON is like a universal translator between systems. Your React frontend speaks JavaScript. Your Express backend speaks JavaScript. Your PostgreSQL database speaks SQL. Your mobile app (if you build one) might speak Swift or Kotlin. JSON is the common language they all understand — a structured text format that any of them can read and write, regardless of the underlying technology.

JSON Data Types

All Valid JSON Data Types — FitoGlobe User Example
JSON
1{
2 "id": "clx8k2abc123", // String — always double quotes, never single
3 "age": 24, // Number (integer) — no quotes
4 "weight": 72.5, // Number (float) — no quotes
5 "isPremium": true, // Boolean — lowercase true/false (not "true")
6 "profilePhoto": null, // Null — represents intentional absence of value
7 "goals": [ // Array — ordered list, mixed types allowed
8 "lose weight",
9 "build muscle"
10 ],
11 "preferences": { // Object — nested key-value pairs
12 "theme": "dark",
13 "language": "en",
14 "units": "metric"
15 }
16 // INVALID in JSON: undefined, functions, Date objects, comments(!)
17}

JSON.stringify() vs JSON.parse()

These two functions are the gatekeepers between JavaScript objects and JSON strings. Understanding them is critical because most bugs involving JSON happen here.

JSON.stringify vs JSON.parse — With Common Mistakes
JS
1// ---- JSON.stringify: JavaScript Object → JSON String ----
2const workoutObj = { name: 'Chest Day', duration: 60, sets: [3, 4] };
3
4// Convert JS object to JSON string (for HTTP request body or localStorage)
5const jsonString = JSON.stringify(workoutObj);
6// Result: '{"name":"Chest Day","duration":60,"sets":[3,4]}'
7// Note: it's now a STRING — you can't do workoutObj.name on jsonString
8
9// ---- JSON.parse: JSON String → JavaScript Object ----
10// Convert JSON string back to a live JavaScript object
11const parsedObj = JSON.parse(jsonString);
12console.log(parsedObj.name); // "Chest Day" — now accessible as object
13
14// ---- COMMON MISTAKE: double-stringifying ----
15const doubleStringified = JSON.stringify(JSON.stringify(workoutObj));
16// Result: '"{\\"name\\":\\"Chest Day\\"...}"' — escaped JSON string inside a string
17// On the backend: req.body will be a string, not an object. Nightmare to debug!
18
19// ---- COMMON MISTAKE: not handling JSON.parse errors ----
20try {
21 // JSON.parse throws SyntaxError if the string is not valid JSON
22 const data = JSON.parse('this is not JSON'); // Will throw!
23} catch (e) {
24 console.error('Invalid JSON:', e.message); // Always wrap in try/catch
25}
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — JSON
Why is JSON preferred over XML for REST APIs?
JSON is preferred over XML for several practical reasons. First, verbosity: XML requires opening and closing tags for every field — <name>Chest Day</name> — while JSON is "name": "Chest Day". A typical API response in XML can be 3-5x the size of the equivalent JSON. Second, native JavaScript integration: JSON maps directly to JavaScript objects and arrays, requiring no special parsing library. JSON.parse() is built into every JavaScript engine. Third, readability: JSON is visually cleaner and easier for both humans and machines to process. Fourth, data types: JSON has native support for numbers, booleans, null, arrays, and objects, while everything in XML is a string by default, requiring type annotations. The main advantage XML retains is in document-centric use cases and namespacing, which is why XML is still used in SOAP web services and configuration files like Maven's pom.xml.
What are the limitations of JSON? What can't it represent?
JSON has several important limitations that developers must work around. No Date type: JSON has no built-in date or time type. Dates must be serialized as strings (ISO 8601 format: "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z") and parsed back to Date objects on the receiving end. This is why you see createdAt: "2024-01-15..." in FitoGlobe's API responses. No undefined: undefined is a JavaScript concept that doesn't exist in JSON. If you JSON.stringify({a: undefined}), the key is simply dropped from the output. No functions: Functions cannot be serialized. No circular references: If object A references object B which references object A, JSON.stringify throws a TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON. No comments: Standard JSON does not support comments (JSONC is a non-standard extension that adds this). Number precision: JSON numbers use IEEE 754 floating-point, meaning very large integers (above Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER) lose precision — important for database IDs that are large integers.
1.7

CORS — Cross-Origin Resource Sharing

🔥 WHY THIS MATTERS

CORS errors are the #1 most confusing error for beginners building full-stack apps. Every developer who has built a separate frontend and backend has encountered: Access to XMLHttpRequest has been blocked by CORS policy. Understanding WHY this happens (not just how to fix it) will save you hours of debugging.

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser-based security mechanism that controls how web pages can request resources from a different origin than the one that served the page. An origin is defined as the combination of protocol + domain + port. Two URLs are considered different origins if ANY of these three components differ. CORS is enforced exclusively by the browser — the request still reaches the server, but the browser blocks the JavaScript from accessing the response unless the server explicitly permits it.

💡 BORDER CONTROL ANALOGY

Imagine two countries: localhost:5173 (Frontend Country) and localhost:5000 (Backend Country). By default, the border is closed — the frontend country's citizens (your JavaScript code) cannot receive goods (data) from the backend country. CORS is the visa system. The backend country (your Express server) issues visas by sending special headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin) saying: "Citizens from localhost:5173 are allowed in." Without this visa, your React code gets the CORS error — not because the request failed, but because the browser acts as border control and refuses to give your JavaScript the response it received.

⚙️ HOW CORS WORKS INTERNALLY
▸ CORS Flow — Simple Request vs Preflight Request
✓ SIMPLE REQUEST (GET)
1. Browser sends GET request
↓
2. Server processes it
↓
3. Server responds WITH ACAO header
↓
4. Browser checks ACAO header ✓
↓
5. JS receives response ✓
⚡ PREFLIGHT REQUEST (POST/PUT/DELETE)
1. Browser sends OPTIONS request first
↓
2. Server responds with allowed methods
↓
3. Browser sends actual request
↓
4. Server processes + responds
↓
5. JS receives response ✓
✗ WHAT HAPPENS WITHOUT CORS HEADERS:
1. Browser sends request → Server receives and processes it ← SERVER STILL RUNS YOUR CODE!
2. Server responds → Browser checks for Access-Control-Allow-Origin header
3. Header is missing → Browser throws CORS error in console
4. Your JavaScript NEVER sees the response — blocked at browser level
5. The server-side operation (DB write, etc.) may have already happened!
⚠️ CRITICAL INSIGHT

CORS is a browser security feature, not a server feature. If you call your API using Postman, curl, or from another server — CORS is never checked. CORS only applies to browser-based JavaScript making cross-origin requests. This is why your API "works in Postman but fails in the browser."

💻 CODE EXAMPLE
backend/server.js — Complete CORS Configuration
JS
1const cors = require('cors'); // npm install cors
2
3// ❌ WRONG — Never do this in production!
4// This allows ANY website to call your API — security nightmare
5app.use(cors()); // Allows all origins (*)
6
7// ✅ CORRECT — Explicit allowed origins
8const allowedOrigins = [
9 'http://localhost:5173', // Vite dev server
10 'https://fitoglobe.vercel.app' // Production frontend
11];
12
13app.use(cors({
14 // origin function runs for every request — checks if origin is allowed
15 origin: (origin, callback) => {
16 // Allow requests with no origin (Postman, curl, same-origin)
17 if (!origin) return callback(null, true);
18 // Check if the request origin is in our allowed list
19 if (allowedOrigins.includes(origin)) {
20 callback(null, true); // Allow — adds ACAO header to response
21 } else {
22 callback(new Error('Not allowed by CORS')); // Reject
23 }
24 },
25 methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE'], // Allowed HTTP methods
26 allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization'], // Headers frontend can send
27 credentials: true, // Allow cookies to be sent cross-origin (for refresh tokens)
28}));
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — CORS
What is CORS and why does it exist?
CORS is a security mechanism implemented by browsers that restricts how JavaScript running on one origin can interact with resources from a different origin. It exists because of the Same-Origin Policy — a foundational browser security rule that prevents malicious websites from reading sensitive data from other origins. Without it, a malicious website could use your logged-in browser session to steal your banking data or your FitoGlobe workout history by making API requests on your behalf. An origin is the combination of protocol, domain, and port — http://localhost:5173 and http://localhost:5000 are different origins even though they're on the same machine, because the port differs. CORS solves the problem of how to safely RELAX the Same-Origin Policy for legitimate cross-origin requests (like your React frontend calling your Express API) while keeping the security protection against malicious sites.
What is a preflight request and why does the browser send it?
A preflight request is an automatic OPTIONS HTTP request that the browser sends before certain cross-origin requests to ask the server: "Do you allow this type of request from this origin?" The browser triggers a preflight when the request uses non-simple methods (POST, PUT, DELETE), or non-simple headers (like Authorization or Content-Type: application/json). The browser sends the OPTIONS request with Access-Control-Request-Method and Access-Control-Request-Headers headers. The server must respond with matching Access-Control-Allow- headers. If the preflight fails (server doesn't respond correctly), the actual request is never sent. This is why in FitoGlobe you might see two network requests in DevTools for one API call — the OPTIONS preflight followed by the actual POST. The preflight response is cached by the browser based on the Access-Control-Max-Age header, so it doesn't happen on every request.
If CORS is a browser feature, does disabling CORS on the server create a security risk?
Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * (allowing all origins) does create security risks, depending on what your API does. For a completely public API with no authentication (like a public weather API), allowing all origins is acceptable. However, for FitoGlobe — which uses authentication and user-specific data — allowing all origins means any website could make authenticated requests on behalf of your users. This enables Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)-like attacks. The correct approach is to whitelist only your specific trusted origins. Additionally, credentials: true (which allows cookies to be sent) is incompatible with Allow-Origin: * — browsers refuse this combination entirely and you'll get another CORS error. You must specify the exact allowed origins when credentials are involved.
1.8

Async/Await & Promises

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

A Promise is an object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation. It exists in one of three states: pending (operation in progress), fulfilled (operation completed successfully), or rejected (operation failed). Async/Await is syntactic sugar built on top of Promises — the async keyword declares that a function returns a Promise, and the await keyword pauses execution within that function until the awaited Promise resolves, allowing asynchronous code to be written in a linear, synchronous-looking style.

💡 RESTAURANT ORDER TICKET ANALOGY

When you order food at a restaurant, the waiter gives you a ticket (a Promise). You don't stand frozen at the counter waiting — you go sit down, talk to friends, drink water (other code runs). When your food is ready, the kitchen calls your ticket number (Promise resolves) and you pick it up. await is like deciding "I'll wait right here at the counter until my order is ready before I do anything else" — but only YOU wait, not the entire restaurant.

⚙️ HOW THE EVENT LOOP WORKS

Understanding async/await requires understanding JavaScript's Event Loop — the mechanism that allows a single-threaded language to handle asynchronous operations.

▸ JavaScript Event Loop — How Async Works
CALL STACK
Executes synchronous code
LIFO (Last In First Out)
One thing at a time

main()
createWorkout()
await prisma.create()
WEB APIs / NODE APIS
Handle async operations
outside JS thread

setTimeout()
fetch() / HTTP req
DB queries
File system I/O
CALLBACK QUEUE
Completed async callbacks
wait here

resolve(workoutData)
→ waiting for stack
  to be empty
↓ EVENT LOOP: When Call Stack is empty → moves callback from Queue to Stack ↓
💻 CALLBACK HELL → PROMISES → ASYNC/AWAIT Evolution
Evolution of Async Code — Three Approaches
JS
1// ============================================
2// ❌ APPROACH 1: Callback Hell (Old Way - avoid)
3// Each async operation nests inside the previous one
4// Very hard to read, debug, and error handle
5loginUser(email, password, (err, user) => {
6 if (err) return handleError(err); // Error handling in every callback
7 getWorkouts(user.id, (err2, workouts) => {
8 if (err2) return handleError(err2);
9 getProgress(user.id, (err3, progress) => {
10 if (err3) return handleError(err3);
11 // ... keep nesting deeper and deeper — "Pyramid of Doom"
12 });
13 });
14});
15
16// ============================================
17// ✅ APPROACH 2: Promises (.then chain)
18// Better — flat chain, but .then() chains get messy with complex logic
19loginUser(email, password)
20 .then((user) => getWorkouts(user.id)) // user available here
21 .then((workouts) => getProgress(user.id)) // wait — user not in scope here!
22 .catch((err) => handleError(err)); // One catch handles all errors
23
24// ============================================
25// ✅✅ APPROACH 3: Async/Await (Modern — use this)
26// Reads like synchronous code, all variables accessible in same scope
27const loadDashboard = async () => {
28 try {
29 // await pauses THIS function until loginUser resolves
30 // Other code in the event loop continues running while we wait
31 const user = await loginUser(email, password);
32 const workouts = await getWorkouts(user.id); // user is accessible here!
33 const progress = await getProgress(user.id);
34 return { user, workouts, progress }; // All data assembled cleanly
35 } catch (error) {
36 // One catch block handles ALL errors from any await above
37 console.error('Dashboard load failed:', error);
38 throw error; // Re-throw for the caller to handle
39 }
40};
41
42// ---- PARALLEL EXECUTION — don't await sequentially when independent ----
43// ❌ SLOW: Each request waits for the previous one (300ms total = 3x 100ms)
44const workouts = await getWorkouts(userId);
45const diet = await getDiet(userId);
46const progress = await getProgress(userId);
47
48// ✅ FAST: All three requests fire simultaneously (100ms total)
49const [workouts, diet, progress] = await Promise.all([
50 getWorkouts(userId), // starts immediately
51 getDiet(userId), // starts immediately (not waiting for workouts)
52 getProgress(userId) // starts immediately (not waiting for diet)
53]); // Waits for ALL to complete before proceeding
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Async/Await & Promises
What is the JavaScript Event Loop and how does it enable async operations?
JavaScript is a single-threaded language — it can only execute one piece of code at a time on a single call stack. The Event Loop is the mechanism that allows JavaScript to perform non-blocking operations despite this. When you call an async operation like await prisma.workout.findMany(), JavaScript hands that operation off to the Node.js runtime (which uses C++ threads via libuv). The current function is paused and removed from the call stack, allowing other code to run. When the database query completes, a callback is placed in the task queue. The Event Loop continuously checks: "Is the call stack empty? If yes, take the next callback from the queue and push it onto the stack." This is why you can handle thousands of simultaneous HTTP requests in Node.js with a single thread — while waiting for database responses, the thread handles other requests instead of blocking.
What is the difference between Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, and Promise.race?
These are three different strategies for handling multiple Promises concurrently. Promise.all() runs all promises in parallel and resolves when ALL succeed. If ANY promise rejects, the entire Promise.all immediately rejects — a "fail fast" behavior. Use this when all results are required (loading dashboard data where all three API calls must succeed). Promise.allSettled() also runs all in parallel but ALWAYS waits for all to complete, regardless of success or failure. It returns an array of objects each with a status ("fulfilled" or "rejected") and a value or reason. Use this when you want all results even if some fail (loading optional analytics data — partial results are acceptable). Promise.race() resolves or rejects as soon as the FIRST promise settles. Use this for timeout patterns: race an operation against a setTimeout promise, and if the timeout wins, cancel the operation.
What happens if you forget to await an async function in Express?
Forgetting await is one of the most common bugs in Express controllers. If you write const workout = prisma.workout.create(...) without await, workout will be a Promise object, not the actual workout data. When you then do res.json({ data: workout }), you'll send a serialized Promise (which becomes {}) to the client — confusing and hard to debug. More critically, if the database operation throws an error, your try/catch block won't catch it because the Promise rejection is unhandled. In older Node.js versions, unhandled Promise rejections could silently crash the entire application. In modern Node.js (v15+), they crash the process with an exit code of 1. The fix is always to write const workout = await prisma.workout.create(...) inside an async function wrapped in a try/catch.
1.9

Synchronous vs Asynchronous

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Synchronous (blocking) execution means operations execute sequentially — each operation must complete before the next one begins. The program is "blocked" during the wait period. Asynchronous (non-blocking) execution means an operation is initiated and control immediately returns to the caller. The result is handled later via a callback, Promise, or async/await when the operation completes. Node.js's performance advantage over traditional synchronous servers (like Apache's threaded model) comes entirely from its asynchronous, event-driven architecture.

⚙️ VISUAL TIMELINE COMPARISON
▸ Handling 3 Requests — Sync vs Async Timeline
❌ SYNCHRONOUS (blocking) — 900ms total
Thread 1
Request 1 (300ms)
Request 2 (300ms)
Request 3 (300ms)
User 2 and 3 must WAIT for User 1 to finish. Total time = 300+300+300 = 900ms
✓ ASYNCHRONOUS (non-blocking) — 300ms total
Event Loop
R1
R2
R3
← waiting for DB responses (all 3 running in parallel) →
DB Queries
All 3 DB queries run simultaneously
Responses
R1✓
R2✓
R3✓
All 3 requests handled concurrently. Total time ≈ 300ms (just the longest single operation)

Real FitoGlobe Examples

OperationSync or Async?Why
prisma.workout.findMany() ASYNC Network I/O to PostgreSQL — takes time, should not block
bcrypt.hash(password) ASYNC CPU-intensive — bcrypt is intentionally slow; async prevents blocking the event loop
jwt.verify(token, secret) SYNC Pure computation — very fast, doesn't need async
JSON.parse(body) SYNC In-memory operation — instantaneous, no I/O
axios.get('/api/workouts') ASYNC HTTP network request — must await or use .then()
nodemailer.sendMail() ASYNC Network I/O to email SMTP server
❌ COMMON MISTAKES
  • Awaiting things that don't need to be awaited sequentially

    The most common performance mistake: developers write await before every async operation even when they are independent, causing them to run one-by-one instead of in parallel. Loading a FitoGlobe dashboard that needs workouts + diet + progress data would take 3x longer if each is awaited sequentially.

    Fix

    Use Promise.all([...]). when operations are independent. Only use sequential await when the second operation depends on the result of the first.

  • Using fs.readFileSync() or other sync APIs inside Express route handlers

    Synchronous file/network operations block the entire Node.js event loop — not just the current request. If 1,000 users are connected and one request triggers fs.readFileSync('bigfile.json'), ALL 1,000 users are frozen until that file is read.

    Fix

    Always use async versions: await fs.promises.readFile(), await prisma.workout.findMany(), etc. The *Sync variants are only acceptable in startup scripts that run once before the server begins accepting requests.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Sync vs Async
Why is Node.js good for I/O-heavy applications but not CPU-heavy ones?
Node.js's single-threaded, event-driven architecture makes it excellent for I/O-bound applications — web servers, APIs, real-time apps, file servers. When Node.js awaits a database query or HTTP request, the thread is freed to handle other requests. Thousands of concurrent connections can be managed because most of the time is spent waiting for I/O, not computing. FitoGlobe's Express backend is a perfect use case. However, Node.js is poor for CPU-intensive tasks — image processing, video encoding, heavy cryptography, machine learning inference. A CPU-intensive operation runs on the single JavaScript thread and blocks the event loop entirely. While one request is computing, ALL other requests queue up waiting. Solutions for CPU-heavy work in Node.js include Worker Threads (for parallelizing JavaScript), child processes, or offloading to specialized microservices written in Python or Go.
What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism, and how does Node.js achieve concurrency without parallelism?
Parallelism means multiple operations executing simultaneously on multiple CPU cores at the exact same moment. Concurrency means multiple operations are in progress at the same time, but they take turns using the CPU — not truly simultaneous. Node.js achieves concurrency through its event loop: while one operation is waiting for I/O (database response, file read, HTTP response), the thread switches to executing other operations. The I/O itself actually runs in parallel in the background via libuv's thread pool and OS-level async I/O — but the JavaScript code executes on a single thread. This is sometimes called "cooperative concurrency." The result for a web server like FitoGlobe is effectively similar to parallel processing for I/O-bound tasks, with far less memory overhead than maintaining separate threads per connection.
✓ PART 1 COMPLETE
Foundations Covered

You now understand the complete foundation of how web applications communicate, how data flows, and how JavaScript handles async operations.

Web Dev Triangle ✓ DNS + TCP/IP ✓ HTTP Methods ✓ Status Codes ✓ JSON ✓ CORS ✓ Async/Await ✓ Event Loop ✓
MERGE INSTRUCTION: This is Part 1. It contains the full HTML structure (<html> through </html>). Parts 2-13 will contain only content sections to paste inside the existing structure before </main>.
PART 02 / 13

Project Structure

Why does FitoGlobe look the way it does? Every folder, every file, every config — nothing is arbitrary. Understanding the why behind your project structure is what lets you navigate any codebase confidently and make architectural decisions in interviews.

Folder Architecture Environment Variables package.json Monolith vs Microservices Vite vs CRA node_modules .gitignore Semver
2.1

Why Separate Frontend and Backend?

📖 DEFINITION
📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Separation of Concerns (SoC) is a software design principle that states each module or layer of an application should be responsible for a single, well-defined concern. In a full-stack application, this means the frontend is exclusively responsible for presentation and user interaction, while the backend exclusively handles data processing, business logic, and persistence. Running them as distinct processes (on different ports or different cloud services) enforces this separation at the infrastructure level, not just at the code level.

💡 ANALOGY
💡 DEPARTMENT STORE ANALOGY

A department store (full application) has a showroom floor (frontend) and a warehouse + accounts office (backend + database). Customers only see and interact with the showroom. The warehouse staff handle inventory, pricing rules, and restocking — customers never go there. If the showroom needs a redesign, you refurbish it without touching the warehouse. If the warehouse needs a new inventory system, the showroom floor is untouched. They communicate via a standard interface — the checkout counter (the API). This is exactly how FitoGlobe works: Vercel hosts the showroom, Railway hosts the warehouse.

⚙️ THE REAL BENEFITS — NOT JUST THEORY
▸ Separation Benefits — What It Actually Unlocks
WITHOUT SEPARATION (Monolith mixing UI+Logic)
  • Deploy entire app for a CSS change
  • Frontend and backend teams conflict on same files
  • Can't build a mobile app without duplicating logic
  • Scale the entire app even if only one part is stressed
  • Technology locked — changing backend means changing frontend
  • Frontend bugs can crash the whole server
WITH SEPARATION (FitoGlobe Architecture)
  • Deploy frontend to Vercel independently, instantly
  • Teams work in parallel without conflicts
  • Add a React Native app — same API, zero backend changes
  • Scale only the service under load
  • Swap React for Vue without touching Express
  • Frontend error returns JSON, doesn't crash backend

The API Contract — The Bridge Between Both Worlds

When frontend and backend are separate, they communicate exclusively through a defined API contract — a formal agreement about what URLs exist, what data to send, and what to expect back. In FitoGlobe, this contract is:

🏋️ FITOGLOBE API CONTRACT EXAMPLE

Both teams agree: To create a workout, send a POST to /api/workouts with {"name": string, "duration": number, "exercises": array} in the body, with a valid JWT in the Authorization header. Expect 201 Created with the created workout object, or 400 with validation errors.

Result: The React developer can mock this endpoint during development. The Express developer can build and test the endpoint independently. They only need to integrate at the end — and as long as both followed the contract, it works.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Separation of Concerns
Why run the frontend on port 5173 and backend on 5000 instead of serving everything from one Express server?
Running them separately provides several concrete development advantages. First, Hot Module Replacement (HMR): Vite's dev server provides instant browser updates when you change React code — a capability Express cannot provide. If you served the React build from Express, you'd need to rebuild the entire app on every change. Second, independent restarts: changing a backend file should not require restarting the frontend, and vice versa. Third, production parity: in production, the frontend will live on Vercel's global CDN and the backend on Railway — having them separate in development mirrors this setup. Fourth, build optimization: Vite handles code splitting, tree shaking, and asset optimization for the frontend, which is separate from Express's job of handling API requests. In production, you don't run vite dev at all — you run vite build which outputs static files, and those files go to Vercel's CDN.
What does it mean for a frontend to be "decoupled" from the backend?
Decoupling means the frontend has no knowledge of the backend's internal implementation — it only knows about the API contract (URLs, request format, response format). The React components in FitoGlobe don't know whether the backend uses PostgreSQL or MongoDB, whether it's running on Node.js or Python, or whether it's deployed on Railway or AWS. They only know: "Send a POST to /api/workouts with this data and I'll get back a workout object." This decoupling means you could completely rewrite the Express backend in Python/FastAPI, switch from PostgreSQL to MongoDB, and the React frontend would require zero changes as long as the API contract is preserved. The same API could simultaneously serve a React web app, a React Native mobile app, and a third-party developer's integration — all from the same backend code.
2.2

Monolith vs Microservices vs Serverless

📖 FORMAL DEFINITIONS

A Monolith is an application where all functionality — authentication, workout tracking, nutrition, AI features, notifications — is deployed as a single, unified process. A Microservices architecture breaks the application into small, independently deployable services, each responsible for one business domain, communicating via HTTP APIs or message queues. Serverless (FaaS — Function as a Service) deploys individual functions that execute on demand, with no persistent server process — the cloud provider manages scaling, and you pay only for actual execution time.

💡 CITY PLANNING ANALOGY

A monolith is like a single giant building with everything inside — gym, restaurant, office, apartments. Easy to manage, but if the gym floods, the whole building is affected, and you can't add more restaurant space without rebuilding the whole structure. Microservices are like a city — separate buildings for each purpose. The gym can be expanded without touching the restaurant. If the restaurant burns down, the gym still runs. But now you need roads (APIs) connecting them, traffic lights (service discovery), and a city planner (orchestration). Serverless is like food trucks — you deploy them exactly when and where needed, they serve customers, then disappear. No maintaining a permanent building, but can't handle sustained heavy traffic.

▸ Architecture Comparison — FitoGlobe Context
🏢 MONOLITH
server.js
├── /auth routes
├── /workouts routes
├── /nutrition routes
├── /ai routes
└── DB connection
✓ Simple to build & debug
✓ One deployment
✓ No network latency between features
✗ One bug can crash all features
✗ Scale entire app for one bottleneck
👈 FitoGlobe uses this
🏙️ MICROSERVICES
auth-service:3001
workout-service:3002
nutrition-service:3003
ai-service:3004
api-gateway:80
✓ Scale each service independently
✓ Different tech per service
✓ Fault isolation
✗ Complex to build & deploy
✗ Network overhead between services
For large teams (10+ devs)
⚡ SERVERLESS
/api/auth → function()
/api/workouts → function()
/api/ai → function()
[spins up on request]
[disappears after]
✓ Pay per execution
✓ Auto-scales to zero
✗ Cold starts (1-3s delay)
✗ No persistent connections
✗ Hard to use with Prisma/WebSockets
Vercel/Netlify functions
ℹ️ WHICH SHOULD YOU USE?

Start with a Monolith. Always. Companies like Shopify, Stack Overflow, and Basecamp ran monoliths for years at massive scale. Microservices add enormous operational complexity — you need Docker, Kubernetes, service discovery, distributed tracing, and more. The correct progression is: Build a monolith → identify actual bottlenecks through real traffic → extract only the services that need independent scaling. FitoGlobe is at stage 1, which is exactly right for a solo developer or small team.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Architecture Patterns
What is a cold start in serverless, and how does it affect user experience?
A cold start occurs when a serverless function is invoked after being idle — the cloud provider must spin up a new execution environment, load the Node.js runtime, load your function code, and initialize any connections before the function can process the request. This can add 500ms to 3 seconds of latency to the first request after a period of inactivity. Subsequent requests reuse the "warm" container and respond in normal time. For FitoGlobe, a cold start on the AI endpoint would mean a user waits 2-3 seconds before even getting a response. This is why FitoGlobe uses Railway (a persistent Node.js process) rather than serverless for the backend — the server is always warm and ready. Cold starts are particularly severe for functions that use database ORMs like Prisma, because Prisma's client initialization and database connection pool setup add significant time to the cold start.
When would you actually choose microservices over a monolith?
Microservices become genuinely justified when specific conditions are met, not just because they sound modern. The key triggers are: organizational scale — when you have 5+ independent teams working on the same codebase and deployment conflicts become a daily problem; vastly different scaling requirements — for example, an AI service that needs GPUs while the auth service needs minimal resources; different release cycles — when one part of the system needs to deploy 20 times a day while another deploys monthly; and different technology requirements — when the AI service genuinely needs Python/PyTorch and the API needs Node.js. For FitoGlobe, none of these conditions apply. It is a solo developer project where microservices would add months of operational overhead for zero user-facing benefit. The industry consensus has shifted back toward "microservices are not a default choice" — they're a solution to specific organizational and technical scaling problems.
2.3

FitoGlobe Complete Folder Structure

ℹ️ HOW TO READ THIS SECTION

Every single file and folder is explained — what it is, why it exists, and what would break if you deleted it. Understanding your own project structure deeply is what separates a developer who built a project from one who understands it.

The Top-Level Layout

fitoglobe/ ← Root workspace (not served anywhere) ├── frontend/ ← Everything React sees and serves ├── backend/ ← Everything Express handles ├── .gitignore ← Tells Git what NOT to track └── README.md ← Project documentation

Frontend Folder — Full Breakdown

frontend/ ├── public/ ← Static files served as-is (no processing) │ ├── favicon.ico ← Browser tab icon │ └── robots.txt ← Instructions for search engine crawlers │ ├── src/ ← All source code lives here (Vite processes this) │ ├── main.jsx ← App entry point — mounts React into index.html │ ├── App.jsx ← Root component — routing, global state, layout │ │ │ ├── components/ ← Reusable UI pieces (Button, Modal, Card...) │ │ ├── Navbar.jsx ← Top navigation bar │ │ ├── Sidebar.jsx ← Side navigation │ │ ├── WorkoutCard.jsx ← Single workout display card │ │ └── LoadingSpinner.jsx ← Reusable loading indicator │ │ │ ├── pages/ ← Full page components (one per route) │ │ ├── HomePage.jsx ← Dashboard / landing after login │ │ ├── WorkoutPage.jsx ← Workout logging and history │ │ ├── DietPage.jsx ← Nutrition tracking │ │ ├── ProgressPage.jsx ← Charts and progress tracking │ │ ├── ProfilePage.jsx ← User profile settings │ │ ├── AICoachPage.jsx ← AI chatbot and recommendations │ │ ├── LoginPage.jsx ← Login form │ │ └── OnboardingPage.jsx ← New user setup flow │ │ │ ├── services/ ← API call functions (all backend communication) │ │ ├── api.js ← Axios instance with base URL + interceptors │ │ ├── auth.service.js ← login, register, logout, refresh │ │ ├── workout.service.js ← CRUD for workouts │ │ ├── diet.service.js ← CRUD for diet entries │ │ ├── ai.service.js ← AI chatbot API calls │ │ └── user.service.js ← Profile update, preferences │ │ │ ├── hooks/ ← Custom React hooks (reusable stateful logic) │ │ ├── useAuth.js ← Access auth context anywhere │ │ ├── useWorkouts.js ← Workout data fetching + state │ │ └── useLocalStorage.js ← Sync state to localStorage │ │ │ ├── context/ ← React Context providers (global state) │ │ └── AuthContext.jsx ← User session state available app-wide │ │ │ ├── utils/ ← Pure helper functions (no React, no API) │ │ ├── formatDate.js ← Date formatting helpers │ │ └── calculateBMI.js ← Health calculation formulas │ │ │ └── assets/ ← Images, fonts, icons (processed by Vite) │ └── logo.svg ← App logo │ ├── index.html ← The ONE HTML file — React mounts here ├── vite.config.js ← Vite build config, proxy, plugins ├── tailwind.config.js ← TailwindCSS customization ├── postcss.config.js ← PostCSS (Tailwind needs this) ├── .eslintrc.cjs ← Code quality rules ├── .env ← Local secrets (NEVER commit this) ├── .env.example ← Template showing what .env needs (safe to commit) ├── package.json ← Dependencies + scripts manifest └── package-lock.json ← Exact versions lockfile (always commit this)

Backend Folder — Full Breakdown

backend/ ├── server.js ← App entry point — Express setup, middleware, listen │ ├── routes/ ← URL path definitions — maps paths to controllers │ ├── auth.routes.js ← /api/auth/login, /api/auth/register, /api/auth/refresh │ ├── workout.routes.js ← /api/workouts CRUD │ ├── diet.routes.js ← /api/diet CRUD │ ├── ai.routes.js ← /api/ai/chat, /api/ai/recommend │ └── user.routes.js ← /api/user/profile, /api/user/settings │ ├── controllers/ ← Business logic — what happens when a route is hit │ ├── auth.controller.js ← hashPassword, generateJWT, verifyCredentials │ ├── workout.controller.js← getWorkouts, createWorkout, updateWorkout, deleteWorkout │ ├── diet.controller.js ← getDietEntries, logMeal, deleteMeal │ ├── ai.controller.js ← callGroqAPI, parseAIResponse, saveRecommendation │ └── user.controller.js ← getProfile, updateProfile, deleteAccount │ ├── middleware/ ← Functions that run BEFORE controllers │ ├── auth.middleware.js ← Verifies JWT — sets req.user on every protected route │ ├── error.middleware.js← Centralized error handler (4 args: err,req,res,next) │ ├── validate.middleware.js← Input validation using Zod/Joi │ └── rateLimit.middleware.js← Prevents abuse of AI endpoints │ ├── config/ ← Configuration and connection setup │ ├── db.js ← Prisma client singleton instance │ └── passport.js ← Google OAuth strategy setup │ ├── prisma/ ← Database layer — Prisma-specific files │ ├── schema.prisma ← Database schema — source of truth for DB structure │ ├── migrations/ ← History of all database changes (auto-generated) │ └── seed.js ← Populates DB with sample data for development │ ├── services/ ← External service integrations │ ├── email.service.js ← Nodemailer — send password reset / welcome emails │ └── ai.service.js ← Groq/Claude API client and prompt templates │ ├── utils/ ← Pure helper functions (no Express, no DB) │ ├── generateToken.js ← JWT creation helper │ └── apiError.js ← Custom error class with statusCode │ ├── .env ← DB URL, JWT secret, API keys (NEVER commit) ├── .env.example ← Shows required env vars (safe to commit) ├── package.json └── package-lock.json
🏋️ WHY THIS EXACT STRUCTURE?
routes/ vs controllers/
Separation of Routing vs Logic
Routes only define WHAT URL maps to WHAT function. Controllers define WHAT HAPPENS. This way, if you add rate limiting to all workout routes, you change one line in the route file — not in every controller function.
middleware/
Cross-Cutting Concerns
Auth checking, validation, rate limiting — these concerns apply to MANY routes. Putting them in middleware means you write the logic once and apply it anywhere with one word: router.get('/', protect, ctrl.get).
services/ (frontend)
API Communication Layer
All axios.get/post/put/delete calls live in service files — NEVER directly in components. If the backend API URL changes, you update one service file, not 20 components.
config/db.js
Singleton Pattern
Prisma client is instantiated ONCE and exported. Every controller imports from config/db.js. Without this, you'd create hundreds of database connections — one per request — crashing PostgreSQL.
context/ (frontend)
Global State
The logged-in user's data needs to be accessible everywhere — Navbar, WorkoutPage, AICoachPage. Context avoids "props drilling" this data through every component in the tree.
prisma/schema.prisma
Single Source of Truth
The database schema is defined ONCE in schema.prisma. Prisma generates TypeScript types, migrations, and a query client from this single file. Change the schema → run migration → types update automatically.
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Folder Structure
What is the MVC pattern and how does FitoGlobe implement it?
MVC (Model-View-Controller) is an architectural pattern that separates an application into three interconnected components. The Model represents the data structure and database logic — in FitoGlobe, this is the Prisma schema (schema.prisma) which defines the data models (User, Workout, DietEntry) and the Prisma client queries in controllers. The View is the presentation layer — in a traditional server-side app this is HTML templates, but in FitoGlobe's API-driven architecture, the view is the React frontend (the JSON responses from the API are the "view data" that React renders). The Controller is the middle layer that receives requests, calls the model for data, and returns a response — in FitoGlobe these are the files in backend/controllers/. The routes in backend/routes/ act as the URL dispatcher that hands requests to the correct controller. This separation means business logic (controllers) can be tested independently of the database schema (models) and the UI (views).
Why put all API calls in a services/ folder instead of directly in React components?
Centralizing API calls in service files is a principle called the Service Layer pattern, and it provides concrete maintainability benefits. First, single point of change: if the backend API changes from /api/workouts to /api/v2/workouts, you update one line in workout.service.js, not in every component that fetches workouts. Second, reusability: the WorkoutPage, the Dashboard widget, and the ProgressPage might all need to fetch workouts — they all call workoutService.getAll() instead of duplicating the axios call. Third, testability: you can mock workoutService in component tests without mocking the entire network. Fourth, consistent error handling: the axios interceptor in api.js handles 401 responses (token expired → redirect to login) in one place, not in every component. Without this pattern, changing the API structure requires hunting through every component that makes API calls — a maintenance nightmare.
2.4

Environment Variables

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Environment variables are key-value pairs stored outside of the application's source code in the operating system's environment or a configuration file (.env). They allow the same application code to behave differently in different environments (development, testing, production) without modifying source code. They are the standard mechanism for storing secrets — database credentials, API keys, JWT secrets — that must not be committed to version control.

💡 RECIPE ANALOGY

A recipe book (your code) says "add 1 cup of [SWEETENER]." The actual sweetener — sugar, honey, stevia — is an environment variable. The recipe is the same everywhere, but on the production kitchen (Railway) you use sugar, and in the test kitchen (local) you use stevia. The recipe book never actually contains the ingredient — it references a variable name. This is why your code says process.env.DATABASE_URL — it references the variable by name without hardcoding the actual connection string.

What Goes in Environment Variables?

VariableDev Value ExampleProd ValueWhy Env Var?
DATABASE_URL postgresql://postgres:pass@localhost:5432/fitoglobe Railway's URL Different DB per environment; credentials are secret
JWT_SECRET dev-secret-change-me 64-char random string Compromising this means forging any user's token
GROQ_API_KEY gsk_abc123... Same key, more credits Anyone with this key can spend your Groq quota
NODE_ENV development production Controls error verbosity, logging levels, optimizations
FRONTEND_URL http://localhost:5173 https://fitoglobe.vercel.app CORS allowed origin changes per environment
PORT 5000 Set by Railway automatically Railway assigns a dynamic port — you must respect it
💻 CODE EXAMPLE
backend/.env — Complete FitoGlobe Configuration
ENV
1# =============================================
2# SERVER CONFIG
3# =============================================
4NODE_ENV=development # Switches app behavior (logging, errors, etc.)
5PORT=5000 # Backend listens on this port locally
6
7# =============================================
8# DATABASE — PostgreSQL via Prisma
9# =============================================
10# Format: postgresql://USER:PASSWORD@HOST:PORT/DATABASE
11DATABASE_URL="postgresql://postgres:Fitoglobe123@localhost:5432/fitoglobe_dev"
12
13# =============================================
14# JWT AUTHENTICATION
15# =============================================
16JWT_SECRET="your-64-char-random-secret-here-change-before-production"
17JWT_EXPIRE="7d" # Access token expires after 7 days
18JWT_REFRESH_SECRET="different-secret-for-refresh-tokens"
19JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRE="30d" # Refresh token lasts 30 days
20
21# =============================================
22# GROQ AI API
23# =============================================
24GROQ_API_KEY="gsk_abc123yourkeyhere" # Get from console.groq.com
25
26# =============================================
27# CORS — Allowed origins
28# =============================================
29FRONTEND_URL="http://localhost:5173" # In production: https://fitoglobe.vercel.app
backend/server.js — How dotenv Loads Variables
JS
1// MUST be the very first line — before any other require()
2// dotenv reads .env file and adds each key to process.env
3require('dotenv').config();
4
5// Now process.env.DATABASE_URL is available everywhere
6// process.env is a global Node.js object — no import needed
7console.log(process.env.DATABASE_URL); // "postgresql://postgres:..."
8console.log(process.env.NODE_ENV); // "development"
9
10// ---- VITE: Frontend env vars need VITE_ prefix ----
11// frontend/.env
12// VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:5000
13// VITE_APP_NAME=FitoGlobe
14// Access in React with: import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL
15console.log(import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL); // React/Vite specific
16
17// ❌ WRONG — Hardcoded secrets (never do this)
18const secret = "myHardcodedSecret123"; // Committed to GitHub forever!
19
20// ✅ CORRECT — From environment
21const secret = process.env.JWT_SECRET; // From .env (not committed)
22
23// ✅ BEST PRACTICE — Validate env vars at startup
24const requiredEnvVars = ['DATABASE_URL', 'JWT_SECRET', 'GROQ_API_KEY'];
25requiredEnvVars.forEach((varName) => {
26 if (!process.env[varName]) {
27 // Crash immediately with clear message rather than failing mysteriously later
28 throw new Error(`Missing required env var: ${varName}`);
29 }
30});
❌ COMMON MISTAKES
  • Committing the .env file to GitHub

    This is a critical security incident. Once secrets are on GitHub (even if you delete them later), they are in Git's history forever and must be considered compromised. GitHub automatically scans for leaked secrets and notifies providers. Attackers scan GitHub for leaked API keys and can drain your Groq credits, access your database, or forge authentication tokens within minutes of exposure.

    Fix
