25// ============================================
26// STEP 3: Create Express application instance
27// ============================================
28const app = express(); // Creates a new Express app — just a function with properties
29
30// ============================================
31// STEP 4: Global middleware — ORDER IS CRITICAL
32// These run for EVERY request before any route handler
33// ============================================
34
35// Security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, etc.)
36app.use(helmet());
37
38// CORS — must come BEFORE routes so OPTIONS preflight requests are handled
39app.use(cors({
40 origin: process.env.FRONTEND_URL,
41 credentials: true,
42 methods: ['GET','POST','PUT','PATCH','DELETE']
43}));
44
45// Body parser — must come BEFORE routes so req.body is populated
46// Parses JSON bodies: Content-Type: application/json → req.body = JS object
47app.use(express.json({ limit: '10mb' })); // Limit prevents huge payload attacks
48app.use(express.urlencoded({ extended: true })); // Parses form submissions
49
50// Global rate limiter — max 100 requests per 15 minutes per IP
51// Prevents brute force attacks and API abuse
52const globalLimiter = rateLimit({
53 windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000, // 15 minutes in milliseconds
54 max: 100, // Max requests per window per IP
55 message: { error: 'Too many requests, please try again later' }
56});
57app.use('/api', globalLimiter); // Apply only to API routes, not health checks
58
59// Passport initialization (must be after body parser for OAuth callbacks)
60app.use(passport.initialize());
61
62// ============================================
63// STEP 5: Mount route handlers
64// '/api/auth' prefix means all routes in authRoutes are under /api/auth/...
65// ============================================
66app.use('/api/auth', authRoutes);
67app.use('/api/workouts', workoutRoutes);
68app.use('/api/diet', dietRoutes);
69app.use('/api/ai', aiRoutes);
70app.use('/api/user', userRoutes);
71
72// Health check — Railway pings this to verify the server is alive
73app.get('/health', (req, res) => res.json({ status: 'ok', timestamp: new Date() }));
74
75// 404 handler — catches any request that didn't match a route above
76app.use((req, res) => res.status(404).json({ error: `Route ${req.path} not found` }));
77
78// ============================================
79// STEP 6: Error handling middleware — MUST BE LAST
80// 4 parameters (err, req, res, next) tell Express this is an error handler
81// ============================================
82app.use(errorHandler);
83
84// ============================================
85// STEP 7: Start HTTP server
86// ============================================
87const PORT = process.env.PORT || 5000;
88app.listen(PORT, '0.0.0.0', () => {
89 console.log(`🚀 FitoGlobe API running on port ${PORT}`);
90 console.log(`📊 Environment: ${process.env.NODE_ENV}`);
91});
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — server.js
Why must express.json() middleware be registered before route handlers?
Express middleware executes in the exact order it is registered with app.use(). When a POST request arrives carrying a JSON body, Express reads the raw byte stream but does not automatically parse it. express.json() is a middleware that intercepts the request, reads the raw body stream, parses it as JSON, and attaches the resulting object to req.body. If you register routes before express.json(), those routes handle the request before parsing happens, meaning req.body is undefined. Your controller code that does const { name, duration } = req.body will destructure undefined — resulting in both values being undefined, which creates subtle bugs that may not throw errors immediately. The fix is always: global middleware first, routes second.
What is the difference between app.use() and app.get()/app.post()?
app.use(path, handler) mounts middleware for ALL HTTP methods. It also performs prefix matching — app.use('/api', router) matches any request whose path starts with /api, including /api/workouts, /api/auth/login, etc. app.get(path, handler) matches only GET requests at the exact path. Path matching is exact (not prefix), so app.get('/api') only matches /api, not /api/workouts. In FitoGlobe's server.js, app.use('/api/workouts', workoutRoutes) uses app.use because the router inside workoutRoutes defines sub-paths like / and /:id. The combined paths become /api/workouts/ and /api/workouts/:id. Using app.get('/api/workouts', workoutRoutes) would not work because the prefix matching wouldn't apply correctly.
4.2

Express — How It Works Internally

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Express.js is a minimal, unopinionated web application framework for Node.js. At its core, Express wraps Node.js's built-in http.createServer() with a layer that provides: a routing system (mapping URL patterns and HTTP methods to handler functions), a middleware stack (a linear sequence of functions each receiving the request and response objects), and a collection of helper methods for building HTTP responses. The express() call returns a function — the application handler — that Node.js's HTTP server passes every incoming request to.

💡 ASSEMBLY LINE ANALOGY

Express is like a factory assembly line. Every car (HTTP request) that enters the factory (server) moves through a series of stations (middleware) in sequence. Station 1 adds the doors (CORS headers). Station 2 installs the engine (parses JSON body). Station 3 checks the VIN (verifies JWT). Station 4 does the final assembly (controller logic). If a car fails inspection at any station, it gets redirected to the defect ramp (error handler). The line keeps moving — cars don't wait for each other (async Node.js).

▸ Express Internals — What Happens When express() Is Called
WHAT express() ACTUALLY RETURNS
1// Simplified Express source
2function express() {
3 // app is just a function!
4 const app = function(req, res) {
5 app.handle(req, res); // run middleware stack
6 };
7 app._router = new Router(); // empty middleware list
8 app.use = (path, fn) => {
9 app._router.use(path, fn); // push to stack
10 };
11 return app;
12}
HOW NODE.JS HTTP SERVER USES IT
1// app.listen() is shorthand for:
2http.createServer(app).listen(PORT);
3
4// Every TCP connection that Node.js
5// receives on PORT calls app(req, res)
6// which runs app._router.handle()
7// which walks the middleware stack
8// until a handler sends a response
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Express Internals
What is Express and what problem does it solve over raw Node.js http module?
Node.js's built-in http module gives you a very low-level API — you receive a request object and must manually: parse the URL path, check the HTTP method, read the request body byte by byte, set response headers, and handle all routing logic yourself. For a production API with 20+ routes, this becomes thousands of lines of repetitive, error-prone imperative code. Express abstracts all of this with: routing — app.get('/workouts/:id', handler) automatically matches paths and extracts URL parameters; middleware — a composable pipeline of functions that transform the request/response; body parsing — express.json() handles reading and parsing the request body stream; and response helpers — res.json(), res.status(), res.redirect() simplify sending responses. Express is intentionally minimal — it provides these essentials and leaves everything else (database connections, authentication, validation) to the developer's choice of packages.
4.3

Middleware Chain & the next() Function

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

In Express, a middleware function is any function with the signature (req, res, next). The next parameter is a callback function provided by Express's router. When called as next(), it transfers control to the next middleware in the stack. When called as next(error) with an argument, it skips all remaining regular middleware and jumps directly to the error handling middleware. If a middleware neither calls next() nor sends a response, the request hangs indefinitely — a common source of timeout bugs.

▸ FitoGlobe Request: POST /api/workouts — Full Middleware Journey
Incoming
POST /api/workouts — Browser sends request with JWT token + JSON body
↓
helmet() — adds security headers, calls next()
Security
helmet() → Sets X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, etc. → calls next()
↓
cors() — checks Origin header, adds ACAO header, calls next()
CORS
cors() → Origin: http://localhost:5173 ✓ → Access-Control-Allow-Origin header added → next()
↓
express.json() — reads body stream, parses JSON, sets req.body
Body Parser
express.json() → req.body = {"name":"Chest Day","duration":60} → next()
↓
rateLimit() — checks IP request count, calls next() or returns 429
Rate Limit
rateLimit() → IP has 3 requests this window (limit: 100) → next()
↓
Router matches /api/workouts → workout.routes.js → protect middleware
Auth Middleware
protect() → reads Authorization header → verifies JWT → req.user = {id, email} → next()
↓
Route handler found: POST / → createWorkout controller
Controller
createWorkout() → prisma.workout.create() → res.status(201).json(workout) ← RESPONSE SENT
next() — All Three Behaviors
JS
1// ---- BEHAVIOR 1: next() with no argument → pass to next middleware ----
2const loggerMiddleware = (req, res, next) => {
3 console.log(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] ${req.method} ${req.path}`);
4 next(); // ← Must call this! Otherwise request hangs here forever
5};
6
7// ---- BEHAVIOR 2: next(error) → skip to error handler ----
8const protect = async (req, res, next) => {
9 try {
10 const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
11 if (!token) {
12 // Pass an error object to next() → jumps straight to errorHandler
13 // All remaining regular middleware and route handlers are SKIPPED
14 return next(new Error('No token provided'));
15 }
16 const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
17 // Attach user to request object — downstream middleware and controllers read this
18 req.user = decoded;
19 next(); // Token valid — continue to route handler
20 } catch (err) {
21 next(err); // JWT verification failed — pass error to handler
22 }
23};
24
25// ---- BEHAVIOR 3: neither next() nor response → request hangs ----
26// This is a bug — NEVER forget to call next() or send a response
27const brokenMiddleware = (req, res, next) => {
28 console.log('I run but I stop everything');
29 // ❌ No next() call, no res.send() → request will timeout after 30s
30 // Client just waits... and waits... then gets ECONNRESET
31};
❌ COMMON MISTAKES
  • Calling next() after already sending a response

    Calling res.json() sends the response. Calling next() afterward causes Express to try running the next middleware, which may also try to send a response — resulting in "Cannot set headers after they are sent to the client" error, crashing the request handler.

    Fix

    Always use return next() or return res.json() to prevent execution continuing after branching. The return statement ensures no further code in the function runs.

  • Forgetting to call next() in an async middleware

    In an async middleware, if the code throws an exception inside a try block that you handle by logging and then returning silently — without calling next(err) — the request hangs indefinitely. The error handler never runs, the client never gets a response, and Node.js shows no error.

    Fix

    Always end every code path in middleware with either next(), next(err), or a response call. Consider wrapping route handlers with an asyncHandler utility that automatically calls next(err) on rejection.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Middleware & next()
Explain the full lifecycle of a request in Express from when it arrives to when the response is sent.
When a TCP packet arrives on the configured port, Node.js's HTTP server parses it into a request object (IncomingMessage) and creates a response object (ServerResponse). Express receives these as req and res and begins walking its internal middleware stack. Each middleware function is called with (req, res, next). If it calls next(), Express calls the next registered middleware. If it calls next(err), Express skips to the error handling middleware. If it calls res.json() or res.send(), Express sends the response and the pipeline ends. In FitoGlobe, a POST request to /api/workouts passes through: helmet() → cors() → express.json() (populates req.body) → rateLimit() → the workout router (which matches the path) → protect() middleware (validates JWT, sets req.user) → createWorkout controller (queries Prisma, calls res.status(201).json()). The entire pipeline is synchronous in structure but non-blocking in execution due to Node.js's async I/O.
What is an Express Router and why is it better than defining all routes in server.js?
express.Router() creates a mini Express application — it has its own middleware stack and can define routes, but is mounted onto a parent app at a given path. Without Router, all 30+ routes in FitoGlobe would be in server.js, which would become hundreds of lines of mixed concerns — auth routes next to workout routes next to AI routes, with no logical grouping. With Router, each domain has its own file: workout.routes.js only knows about workout paths (/, /:id), and its middleware applies only to those routes. The prefix (/api/workouts) is applied when mounting in server.js, which means route files don't need to know their own prefix. This enables: file-level organization, domain-specific middleware (e.g., a stricter rate limiter only on /api/ai), independent testing of route groups, and easy prefix changes without touching individual route files.
4.4

MVC Architecture Pattern in FitoGlobe

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

MVC (Model-View-Controller) is a software architectural pattern that separates an application into three interconnected layers: the Model handles data structure and business data logic, the View handles presentation, and the Controller handles the orchestration between the two — receiving input, calling the model, and returning a response. In a REST API (like FitoGlobe's Express backend), the "View" is replaced by JSON responses rather than HTML templates.

M
Model
Defines the data structure and handles all database interactions. In FitoGlobe, the Prisma schema IS the model — it defines what a Workout, User, or DietEntry looks like and what fields they have.
📄 prisma/schema.prisma
📄 config/db.js
📄 (Prisma-generated types)
V
View
In a traditional app, this is HTML templates. In FitoGlobe's API, the "View" is the JSON response object that controllers return — res.json({data}). The React frontend is the actual visual presentation layer.
📄 (React frontend — separate)
📄 res.json() responses
📄 Error response shapes
C
Controller
Receives the HTTP request (via routes), validates input, calls Prisma to query/mutate data, and sends the JSON response. Each controller function handles one specific operation — one function, one job.
📄 controllers/workout.controller.js
📄 controllers/auth.controller.js
📄 controllers/diet.controller.js
▸ MVC Flow — GET /api/workouts in FitoGlobe
HTTP REQUEST
React fetch()
→ GET /api/workouts
+ JWT token
→
ROUTES (URL dispatcher)
workout.routes.js
GET / → protect
→ getWorkouts()
→
CONTROLLER (logic)
getWorkouts()
reads req.user.id
calls prisma query
→
MODEL (Prisma)
prisma.workout
.findMany({where:
{userId}})
→
VIEW (JSON response)
res.json({
data: workouts
})
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — MVC Pattern
Why separate routes from controllers? Why not put the business logic directly in the route handler?
Putting logic directly in route handlers is a common beginner pattern that quickly becomes unmaintainable. When everything is in one place, a single route file for workouts might grow to 500+ lines of mixed routing declarations and business logic. The separation provides concrete benefits: testability — controller functions can be unit-tested independently by calling them directly without making HTTP requests; reusability — a controller function can be called from multiple routes or even from other controllers; readability — the route file becomes a clean map of "what URL calls what function," and the controller file describes "what that function does"; and single responsibility — the route only knows about URLs and which controller to call, the controller only knows about business logic, and the model only knows about data structure. In FitoGlobe's route files, each route handler is a one-liner pointing to a controller function — the route file reads like a table of contents for the API.
4.5

routes/ — Every Route File Explained

ℹ️ FULL API SURFACE — Every Endpoint FitoGlobe Exposes

This is the complete contract between your frontend and backend. Every endpoint you see here has a corresponding service call in the React frontend's services/ folder.

MethodPathAuthControllerPurpose
POST/api/auth/registerPUBLICregister()Create new user account
POST/api/auth/loginPUBLIClogin()Login with email + password → JWT
POST/api/auth/refreshPUBLICrefreshToken()Exchange refresh token for new access token
POST/api/auth/logoutPRIVATElogout()Invalidate refresh token
POST/api/auth/forgot-passwordPUBLICforgotPassword()Send password reset email
POST/api/auth/reset-passwordPUBLICresetPassword()Set new password with reset token
GET/api/auth/googlePUBLICpassport.authenticate()Redirect to Google OAuth
GET/api/auth/google/callbackPUBLICgoogleCallback()Handle OAuth callback → JWT
GET/api/workoutsPRIVATEgetWorkouts()List all workouts for user
GET/api/workouts/:idPRIVATEgetWorkoutById()Get single workout
POST/api/workoutsPRIVATEcreateWorkout()Log a new workout
PATCH/api/workouts/:idPRIVATEupdateWorkout()Update workout details
DELETE/api/workouts/:idPRIVATEdeleteWorkout()Remove a workout
GET/api/dietPRIVATEgetDietEntries()List nutrition entries
POST/api/dietPRIVATElogMeal()Log a meal
DELETE/api/diet/:idPRIVATEdeleteMeal()Remove a meal entry
POST/api/ai/chatPRIVATEaiChat()Chat with AI fitness coach
POST/api/ai/recommendPRIVATEaiRecommend()Get AI workout recommendation
GET/api/user/profilePRIVATEgetProfile()Get user profile
PATCH/api/user/profilePRIVATEupdateProfile()Update profile settings
DELETE/api/user/accountPRIVATEdeleteAccount()Permanently delete account
backend/routes/workout.routes.js — Deep Annotated
JS
1const { Router } = require('express');
2const router = Router(); // Mini Express app — only handles workout paths
3const { protect } = require('../middleware/auth.middleware');
4const ctrl = require('../controllers/workout.controller');
5const { validateWorkout } = require('../middleware/validate.middleware');
6
7// Route parameters: ':id' becomes req.params.id in the controller
8// GET /api/workouts → combined with server.js prefix '/api/workouts' + '/'
9// Middleware array: [protect, ctrl.getWorkouts]
10// protect runs first → if JWT valid → ctrl.getWorkouts runs second
11router.get('/', [protect], ctrl.getWorkouts);
12router.get('/:id', [protect], ctrl.getWorkoutById);
13
14// Multiple middleware: protect first, then validate body, then create
15// If validation fails, validateWorkout calls next(err) — createWorkout never runs
16router.post('/', [protect, validateWorkout], ctrl.createWorkout);
17router.patch('/:id',[protect], ctrl.updateWorkout);
18router.delete('/:id',[protect], ctrl.deleteWorkout);
19
20module.exports = router;
4.6

controllers/ — Business Logic Layer

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Controllers are functions that receive an HTTP request (with parsed body, query parameters, URL parameters, and authenticated user from middleware), execute the appropriate business logic (validation, authorization checks, database operations), and send an HTTP response. Each controller function is the boundary between the HTTP world (requests/responses) and the business domain (workouts, nutrition, user data). A controller should do exactly one thing — a "fat controller" that does too much is a design smell.

backend/controllers/workout.controller.js — Complete Annotated
JS
1const prisma = require('../config/db'); // Singleton Prisma client
2const { ApiError } = require('../utils/apiError'); // Custom error class
3
4// ---- GET /api/workouts ----
5const getWorkouts = async (req, res, next) => {
6 try {
7 // req.user was set by protect middleware — contains decoded JWT payload
8 // userId ensures users only see THEIR workouts (authorization check)
9 const workouts = await prisma.workout.findMany({
10 where: { userId: req.user.id }, // CRITICAL: filter by current user
11 orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }, // Newest first
12 include: { exercises: true }, // Include related exercise records
13 take: 20, // Pagination: max 20 per request
14 skip: parseInt(req.query.skip) || 0 // Offset: ?skip=20 for page 2
15 });
16 // 200 OK with standardised response shape
17 res.status(200).json({ success: true, data: workouts, count: workouts.length });
18 } catch (error) {
19 next(error); // Passes to errorHandler middleware → logs + sends 500
20 }
21};
22
23// ---- POST /api/workouts ----
24const createWorkout = async (req, res, next) => {
25 try {
26 // Destructure validated body (validateWorkout middleware already ran)
27 const { name, duration, exercises, notes } = req.body;
28
29 // Create workout AND its exercises in one atomic operation (transaction)
30 const workout = await prisma.workout.create({
31 data: {
32 name, duration, notes,
33 userId: req.user.id, // Associate workout with logged-in user
34 exercises: {
35 createMany: { data: exercises } // Create all exercises in one query
36 }
37 },
38 include: { exercises: true } // Return exercises in response
39 });
40 // 201 Created — new resource was created
41 res.status(201).json({ success: true, data: workout });
42 } catch (error) {
43 next(error);
44 }
45};
46
47// ---- DELETE /api/workouts/:id ----
48const deleteWorkout = async (req, res, next) => {
49 try {
50 // Step 1: Find the workout first (ownership check)
51 const workout = await prisma.workout.findUnique({
52 where: { id: req.params.id }
53 });
54
55 if (!workout) return next(new ApiError(404, 'Workout not found'));
56
57 // Step 2: Ownership check — cannot delete another user's workout
58 if (workout.userId !== req.user.id) {
59 return next(new ApiError(403, 'Not authorized to delete this workout'));
60 }
61
62 // Step 3: Delete — Prisma cascades to exercises (schema: onDelete: Cascade)
63 await prisma.workout.delete({ where: { id: req.params.id } });
64
65 // 204 No Content — success but nothing to return
66 res.status(204).send();
67 } catch (error) {
68 next(error);
69 }
70};
71
72module.exports = { getWorkouts, createWorkout, deleteWorkout };
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Controllers
What is the "asyncHandler" pattern and why do many Express codebases use it?
Every async controller in FitoGlobe wraps its logic in a try/catch block and passes errors to next(error). Without this, a rejected Promise in an async controller causes an UnhandledPromiseRejection — the error handler never receives it, the client hangs or gets a cryptic error, and Node.js may exit in production. The asyncHandler pattern eliminates this boilerplate: it's a higher-order function that wraps your async controller and automatically catches any thrown error, passing it to next(error). Example: const asyncHandler = fn => (req, res, next) => Promise.resolve(fn(req, res, next)).catch(next);. Then instead of writing try/catch in every controller, you write router.get('/', asyncHandler(getWorkouts)). If getWorkouts throws or rejects, asyncHandler catches it and calls next(error) automatically. This makes controllers cleaner and eliminates an entire class of "silent hang" bugs from forgetting to catch async errors.
4.7

middleware/ — Auth, Validation, and Rate Limiting

backend/middleware/auth.middleware.js — JWT Verification Deep Dive
JS
1const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken');
2const prisma = require('../config/db');
3const { ApiError } = require('../utils/apiError');
4
5// protect: verifies JWT and sets req.user for all downstream handlers
6// Every protected route runs this before its controller
7const protect = async (req, res, next) => {
8 try {
9 // ---- STEP 1: Extract token from header ----
10 // Authorization: "Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..."
11 // Optional chaining ?. prevents crash if header is absent
12 const authHeader = req.headers['authorization'];
13 const token = authHeader?.startsWith('Bearer ') ? authHeader.split(' ')[1] : null;
14
15 if (!token) {
16 return next(new ApiError(401, 'No authentication token provided'));
17 }
18
19 // ---- STEP 2: Verify signature and expiry ----
20 // jwt.verify() does TWO things:
21 // (a) Verifies HMAC signature → token was signed with our secret
22 // (b) Checks exp claim → token hasn't expired
23 // Throws JsonWebTokenError or TokenExpiredError if either fails
24 const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
25 // decoded = { id: "clx8k2...", email: "[email protected]", iat: ..., exp: ... }
26
27 // ---- STEP 3: (Optional) verify user still exists ----
28 // A valid JWT for a deleted account should be rejected
29 // This adds one DB query per request — skip in high-traffic APIs, use for security-critical ones
30 const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({
31 where: { id: decoded.id },
32 select: { id: true, email: true, name: true } // Select only what's needed
33 });
34
35 if (!user) return next(new ApiError(401, 'User no longer exists'));
36
37 // ---- STEP 4: Attach user to request ----
38 // All downstream controllers access this via req.user
39 // This is why controllers can do: prisma.workout.findMany({where:{userId: req.user.id}})
40 req.user = user;
41 next(); // Authentication successful — proceed to controller
42
43 } catch (err) {
44 // jwt.verify() throws specific error types — use them for better messages
45 if (err.name === 'TokenExpiredError') {
46 return next(new ApiError(401, 'Session expired, please log in again'));
47 }
48 if (err.name === 'JsonWebTokenError') {
49 return next(new ApiError(401, 'Invalid token'));
50 }
51 next(err); // Unknown error — pass to general error handler
52 }
53};
54
55module.exports = { protect };
backend/middleware/validate.middleware.js — Input Validation with Zod
JS
1const { z } = require('zod'); // Type-safe schema validation library
2
3// Define the shape of a valid workout request body using Zod schema
4const workoutSchema = z.object({
5 name: z.string().min(1, 'Name is required').max(100),
6 duration: z.number().int().min(1).max(600), // 1 to 600 minutes
7 exercises: z.array(z.object({
8 name: z.string().min(1),
9 sets: z.number().int().positive(),
10 reps: z.number().int().positive().optional(),
11 })).min(1, 'At least one exercise required'),
12 notes: z.string().max(500).optional() // optional field
13});
14
15// Middleware factory: validates req.body against schema
16const validateWorkout = (req, res, next) => {
17 const result = workoutSchema.safeParse(req.body);
18 if (!result.success) {
19 // Zod formats errors as human-readable messages with field paths
20 const errors = result.error.errors.map(e => ({
21 field: e.path.join('.'),
22 message: e.message
23 }));
24 // Return 400 with all validation errors — controller never runs
25 return res.status(400).json({ success: false, errors });
26 }
27 // Replace req.body with parsed/coerced data (Zod converts types automatically)
28 req.body = result.data;
29 next(); // Validation passed — proceed to createWorkout controller
30};
31module.exports = { validateWorkout };
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Auth Middleware
How does req.user get set and why can the controller trust it?
req.user is set by the protect middleware after it successfully verifies the JWT. The trust chain works like this: (1) The JWT was originally signed with the server's secret (JWT_SECRET) when the user logged in. (2) jwt.verify(token, JWT_SECRET) cryptographically confirms the token was signed with the same secret — it cannot be forged without knowing the secret. (3) If verification succeeds, the decoded payload (containing the user's ID) is used to look up the actual user in the database. (4) The real user object is attached to req.user. (5) Since route middleware runs in order and the controller only executes after protect calls next(), by the time controller code reads req.user, it is guaranteed to be a verified, database-confirmed user. The controller doesn't need to re-verify — it can trust that req.user.id belongs to the authenticated user. This is why using req.user.id in database queries is a safe authorization pattern.
Why validate input on the backend even if the frontend already validates it?
Frontend validation is purely a user experience feature — it gives immediate feedback without a server round-trip. It provides zero security. Any user can bypass your React form validation by sending a raw HTTP request using curl, Postman, or a custom script directly to your API endpoint, completely skipping the frontend. Without backend validation, a malicious user could send {"name": "", "duration": -999, "exercises": null} to your API and cause database errors, crashes, or corrupted data. In FitoGlobe, backend validation with Zod serves as the actual security gate: it ensures the data has the correct types, is within valid ranges, and satisfies business rules — regardless of how the request was made. The rule is: never trust client-side data. The frontend validates for UX, the backend validates for security and data integrity.
4.8

config/db.js — Prisma Singleton Pattern

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

The Singleton pattern ensures a class or module is instantiated exactly once and that instance is shared across the entire application. In FitoGlobe, config/db.js creates a single PrismaClient instance and exports it. Every module that needs database access imports this single instance. This is critical because each PrismaClient instance maintains its own connection pool — creating multiple instances wastes database connections, which are a limited resource (PostgreSQL defaults to 100 max connections).

💡 TELEPHONE SWITCHBOARD ANALOGY

A database connection pool is like a telephone switchboard with a limited number of phone lines. The switchboard (PrismaClient) manages all calls — when a query comes in, it picks up an available line (connection), makes the call to the database, and returns the line when done. If you create multiple switchboards (multiple PrismaClient instances), each one reserves its own set of lines — quickly exhausting the database's maximum connection limit and causing "too many connections" errors. One switchboard, shared by everyone, is the correct architecture.

backend/config/db.js — Singleton with Hot Reload Safety
JS
1const { PrismaClient } = require('@prisma/client');
2
3// ---- THE SINGLETON PROBLEM IN DEVELOPMENT ----
4// In dev, nodemon restarts the server when files change
5// BUT Node.js module cache is rebuilt → new PrismaClient created each restart
6// In production this is fine (only one start). In dev, we use globalThis
7
8let prisma;
9
10if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production') {
11 // Production: safe to create new instance (server only starts once)
12 prisma = new PrismaClient({
13 log: ['error', 'warn'], // Only log errors in production
14 errorFormat: 'minimal' // Don't expose detailed errors
15 });
16} else {
17 // Development: store on globalThis to survive hot reloads
18 // globalThis persists across Node module re-evaluations during nodemon restarts
19 if (!globalThis.__prisma) {
20 globalThis.__prisma = new PrismaClient({
21 log: ['query', 'error', 'warn'] // Log all queries in dev for debugging
22 });
23 }
24 prisma = globalThis.__prisma; // Reuse existing instance
25}
26
27// Graceful shutdown: disconnect when process exits
28// Ensures all pending queries complete before connection closes
29process.on('beforeExit', async () => {
30 await prisma.$disconnect();
31});
32
33module.exports = prisma;
34// Usage in any controller: const prisma = require('../config/db');
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Prisma Singleton
What is connection pooling and why does it matter for a web application?
A database connection is an expensive resource — establishing a TCP connection to PostgreSQL, authenticating, and negotiating the protocol takes 20-100ms. Connection pooling maintains a pool of pre-established connections that are reused across multiple requests instead of creating and destroying a connection per request. When a query is needed, the pool lends an available connection; when the query completes, the connection is returned to the pool. Prisma's built-in connection pool (powered by the Prisma Query Engine) maintains a configurable number of persistent connections. Without pooling, a FitoGlobe server handling 100 concurrent requests would try to open 100 database connections simultaneously — hitting PostgreSQL's max_connections limit (default: 100) and causing "too many connections" errors. With pooling, those 100 requests share perhaps 10 connections, queuing behind each other. For Railway's free tier PostgreSQL, which has stricter connection limits, the singleton pattern combined with Prisma's pool is essential for stability.
4.9

config/passport.js — Google OAuth 2.0 Flow

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that allows a user to grant a third-party application (FitoGlobe) limited access to their account on another service (Google) without sharing their password. The Authorization Code Flow is the most secure variant: the user authenticates with Google directly, Google provides a short-lived code to FitoGlobe's server, the server exchanges that code for access and refresh tokens, and uses those tokens to fetch user profile data. Passport.js is authentication middleware that implements various OAuth strategies, abstracting the complex token exchange flow.

▸ Google OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow — FitoGlobe
1
User Clicks "Sign in with Google"

React frontend calls GET /api/auth/google. Passport redirects browser to Google's OAuth consent page: accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth?client_id=...&scope=email+profile&redirect_uri=...

2
User Consents on Google's Page

User sees "FitoGlobe wants to access your Google account" and clicks "Allow." This happens entirely on Google's servers — FitoGlobe never sees the user's Google password.

3
Google Redirects with Authorization Code

Google redirects the browser to /api/auth/google/callback?code=4/0AfQ...&state=.... The code is a short-lived (60s), one-time-use authorization code.

4
Server Exchanges Code for Tokens

Passport's GoogleStrategy sends a server-to-server POST to Google's token endpoint with the code + client secret. Google returns access token + refresh token + ID token. This exchange happens server-side — the tokens never appear in the browser URL or history.

5
Server Fetches User Profile

Using the access token, Passport calls Google's People API to get: id, email, displayName, photo. The verify callback in our config receives these.

6
Find or Create User in Database

The verify callback uses prisma.user.upsert(): if a user with this Google ID exists, return them; if not, create a new account. This enables "login or auto-register" in one step.

7
Issue FitoGlobe JWT

The callback controller receives the user, signs a FitoGlobe JWT, and redirects to the frontend with the token: https://fitoglobe.vercel.app/auth/callback?token=eyJ.... React stores the token and navigates to the dashboard.

backend/config/passport.js — Google Strategy Setup
JS
1const passport = require('passport');
2const { Strategy: GoogleStrategy } = require('passport-google-oauth20');
3const prisma = require('./db');
4
5passport.use(new GoogleStrategy({
6 // These come from Google Cloud Console → Credentials → OAuth 2.0 Client IDs
7 clientID: process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID,
8 clientSecret: process.env.GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET,
9 // Where Google redirects after user grants permission
10 // Must be registered in Google Cloud Console under Authorized Redirect URIs
11 callbackURL: `${process.env.BACKEND_URL}/api/auth/google/callback`,
12 scope: ['profile', 'email'] // Request these profile fields from Google
13},
14// Verify callback: runs after Google returns user's profile
15// accessToken: Google access token (for calling Google APIs)
16// refreshToken: long-lived token (for refreshing access token)
17// profile: user's Google profile data
18async (accessToken, refreshToken, profile, done) => {
19 try {
20 // upsert: find user by googleId OR create them if they don't exist
21 // This implements "find or create" for OAuth — one query handles both cases
22 const user = await prisma.user.upsert({
23 where: { googleId: profile.id },
24 update: {
25 // Update these fields on every login (in case user changed Google profile)
26 name: profile.displayName,
27 avatar: profile.photos[0]?.value
28 },
29 create: {
30 // First time logging in with Google — create full account
31 googleId: profile.id,
32 email: profile.emails[0].value,
33 name: profile.displayName,
34 avatar: profile.photos[0]?.value,
35 // No password field — this is an OAuth-only account
36 authProvider: 'GOOGLE'
37 }
38 });
39 // done(null, user) → Passport sets req.user = user
40 // done(err, false) → Passport calls next(err)
41 done(null, user);
42 } catch (err) {
43 done(err, false);
44 }
45}));
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — OAuth
What is the difference between authentication and OAuth? Why doesn't OAuth mean the user is authenticated?
OAuth 2.0 is fundamentally an authorization framework, not an authentication protocol. It answers "what can this app do on your behalf?" not "who are you?" The confusion arises because developers use OAuth to achieve authentication by requesting profile access. When Google sends back a user profile with an email address and Google ID, we're using this as proof of identity — but OAuth itself doesn't guarantee this. OpenID Connect (OIDC) is the authentication layer built on top of OAuth 2.0 that adds an ID token (a JWT) proving the user's identity in a standardized way. In FitoGlobe's implementation, after receiving the Google profile via Passport's GoogleStrategy (which uses OIDC under the hood), we issue our own FitoGlobe JWT. This JWT is what actually authenticates requests to FitoGlobe's API — not the Google access token. The Google OAuth flow is just the mechanism we use to verify who the user is without them creating a password.
4.10

services/email.service.js — Nodemailer

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Nodemailer is a Node.js module for sending emails via SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) or mail service APIs. It requires a transport — the underlying delivery mechanism — which can be a direct SMTP connection to an email server (Gmail, Outlook) or a third-party email delivery service (SendGrid, Resend, Mailgun) via their API. FitoGlobe uses email for password reset flows — a security-critical feature where implementation errors can be exploited.

backend/services/email.service.js — Nodemailer with Templates
JS
1const nodemailer = require('nodemailer');
2
3// ---- CREATE TRANSPORTER ----
4// Transporter = the "email service" configuration
5// In dev: use Ethereal (fake SMTP — emails captured at ethereal.email, never delivered)
6// In prod: use a real service (Resend, SendGrid, or SMTP)
7const createTransporter = () => {
8 if (process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production') {
9 // Production: Resend API (modern, deliverability-optimized)
10 return nodemailer.createTransport({
11 host: 'smtp.resend.com',
12 port: 465, // Port 465 = SMTP with TLS (secure)
13 secure: true, // Use TLS encryption
14 auth: {
15 user: 'resend',
16 pass: process.env.RESEND_API_KEY
17 }
18 });
19 } else {
20 // Development: Ethereal — fake SMTP server for testing
21 // Emails are "sent" but captured in the Ethereal dashboard, not delivered
22 return nodemailer.createTransport({
23 host: 'smtp.ethereal.email',
24 port: 587,
25 auth: {
26 user: process.env.ETHEREAL_USER,
27 pass: process.env.ETHEREAL_PASS
28 }
29 });
30 }
31};
32
33// ---- SECURE PASSWORD RESET EMAIL ----
34// resetToken: cryptographically random token (not the user's password)
35// token is stored as a HASH in DB (never plain text)
36const sendPasswordResetEmail = async ({ to, resetToken, userName }) => {
37 const transporter = createTransporter();
38 const resetUrl = `${process.env.FRONTEND_URL}/reset-password?token=${resetToken}`;
39
40 const mailOptions = {
41 from: '"FitoGlobe" <[email protected]>',
42 to,
43 subject: 'Reset Your FitoGlobe Password',
44 // Plain text fallback for email clients that don't render HTML
45 text: `Hi ${userName}, reset your password: ${resetUrl} (expires in 1 hour)`,
46 // HTML email template — inline styles required (email clients strip <style> tags)
47 html: `
48 <div style="font-family:sans-serif;max-width:600px">
49 <h1 style="color:#c8f135">FitoGlobe</h1>
50 <p>Hi ${userName},</p>
51 <p>Click the button below to reset your password (expires in 1 hour):</p>
52 <a href="${resetUrl}" style="background:#c8f135;padding:12px 24px;display:inline-block">
53 Reset Password</a>
54 <p>If you didn't request this, ignore this email.</p>
55 </div>`
56 };
57
58 await transporter.sendMail(mailOptions);
59};
60
61module.exports = { sendPasswordResetEmail };
4.11

Error Handling Middleware — Centralized Error Management

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

In Express, an error handling middleware is a special middleware function with exactly four parameters: (err, req, res, next). Express identifies it as an error handler by the four-parameter signature — the first parameter receives the error object passed to next(error). It must be registered after all other middleware and route handlers in server.js, because Express only invokes it when an error has been passed to next() by a preceding middleware or route handler.

⚠️ CRITICAL: ALL 4 PARAMETERS ARE REQUIRED

If you define the error handler with only 3 parameters (err, req, res), Express treats it as a regular middleware, NOT an error handler. Errors will silently pass through it without being handled. The function signature must be exactly (err, req, res, next) — even if you never call next.

backend/middleware/error.middleware.js — Production-Grade Error Handler
JS
1// Custom error class — allows controllers to specify HTTP status codes
2// new ApiError(404, 'Workout not found') vs generic Error
3class ApiError extends Error {
4 constructor(statusCode, message) {
5 super(message); // Sets this.message
6 this.statusCode = statusCode; // Custom property for the HTTP status
7 this.isOperational = true; // Marks as expected error (not a bug)
8 Error.captureStackTrace(this, ApiError);
9 }
10}
11
12// ============================================
13// THE ERROR HANDLER — 4 params = Express knows this is an error handler
14// ============================================
15const errorHandler = (err, req, res, next) => {
16 // ---- STEP 1: Determine the correct HTTP status code ----
17 let statusCode = err.statusCode || 500;
18 let message = err.message || 'Internal server error';
19
20 // ---- STEP 2: Handle specific Prisma error codes ----
21 if (err.code === 'P2002') {
22 // P2002 = Prisma unique constraint violation (e.g., duplicate email)
23 statusCode = 409; // 409 Conflict
24 message = `Duplicate value for field: ${err.meta?.target}`;
25 }
26 if (err.code === 'P2025') {
27 // P2025 = Prisma record not found (e.g., update/delete non-existent record)
28 statusCode = 404;
29 message = 'Record not found';
30 }
31
32 // ---- STEP 3: Handle JWT errors ----
33 if (err.name === 'JsonWebTokenError') { statusCode = 401; message = 'Invalid token'; }
34 if (err.name === 'TokenExpiredError') { statusCode = 401; message = 'Token expired'; }
35
36 // ---- STEP 4: Log the error (ALWAYS log, NEVER send to client) ----
37 if (statusCode >= 500) {
38 // 500s are bugs — log the full stack trace for debugging
39 console.error('💥 SERVER ERROR:', err);
40 // In production, also send to Sentry: Sentry.captureException(err)
41 }
42
43 // ---- STEP 5: Send sanitized error response ----
44 res.status(statusCode).json({
45 success: false,
46 error: message,
47 // Only include stack trace in development — NEVER in production
48 ...(process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' && { stack: err.stack })
49 });
50};
51
52module.exports = { errorHandler, ApiError };
▸ Operational vs Programming Errors — Two Error Categories
✓ OPERATIONAL ERRORS (expected)
User not found (404)
Validation failed (400)
Token expired (401)
Duplicate email (409)
Rate limit exceeded (429)

Handle gracefully: meaningful message to client, don't crash
✗ PROGRAMMING ERRORS (bugs)
TypeError: Cannot read property
Prisma connection lost
Out of memory
Stack overflow
Unhandled promise rejection

Log the full error: generic 500 to client, fix the bug
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Error Handling
Why does the error handling middleware need exactly 4 parameters, and why must it be registered last?
Express uses function arity (the number of declared parameters) to distinguish error handlers from regular middleware. When Express encounters (err, req, res, next), it recognizes the four-parameter signature and registers it as an error handler — a middleware that only runs when next(error) is called with a truthy argument. A three-parameter function (req, res, next) is a regular middleware that runs on every request. If you accidentally define your error handler with three parameters, errors silently pass through it without being caught. It must be last because Express processes middleware in registration order. If you register the error handler before your routes, it can never receive errors from those routes — errors would only reach it if thrown by the middleware registered before it. Registering it after all routes ensures it catches errors from the entire application.
How do you handle unhandled Promise rejections in an Express application?
Unhandled Promise rejections — async errors not caught by try/catch or .catch() — bypass Express's error handling entirely because they occur outside the request cycle. In Node.js 15+, an unhandled rejection crashes the process with a non-zero exit code. There are two layers of defense: (1) Per-controller: always wrap async controllers in try/catch and call next(err), or use an asyncHandler wrapper that does this automatically. (2) Global fallback: register a process.on('unhandledRejection', (reason, promise) => { console.error(reason); process.exit(1); }) handler in server.js. This catches any rejection that slipped through, logs it, and exits cleanly (so process managers like PM2 or Railway's container runtime can restart the process). Letting the process hang with an unhandled rejection in an unknown state is worse than crashing and restarting cleanly.
✓ PART 4 COMPLETE
Backend Deep Dive Complete

You now understand the complete lifecycle of a request through Express, every backend file's purpose, and the patterns that make the backend secure and maintainable.

server.js ✓ Middleware Chain ✓ MVC Pattern ✓ Auth Middleware ✓ Prisma Singleton ✓ Google OAuth ✓ Nodemailer ✓ Error Handling ✓
MERGE INSTRUCTION: Copy the <div class="content-area"> contents from this file and paste after Part 3's last </section> in the master file.
PART 05 / 13

Database Complete

The database is where your data lives forever — or gets lost forever if you misuse it. Every model, every relation, every query, every performance concern, and every safety mechanism in FitoGlobe's PostgreSQL + Prisma stack examined in full depth.

PostgreSQL Prisma ORM ACID Relations Migrations N+1 Problem Transactions Indexing SQL vs NoSQL
5.1

What Is a Database — And Why Not Just Use Files?

📖 DEFINITION
📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

A database is an organized collection of structured data managed by a Database Management System (DBMS) — software that enforces rules (constraints, data types, relationships), provides query language (SQL), manages concurrent access from multiple processes, handles failures gracefully, and optimizes data retrieval via indexing. A DBMS solves problems that plain file storage cannot: concurrent writes, transactional safety, relational integrity, query optimization, and crash recovery.

💡 FILING CABINET vs LIBRARY ANALOGY

Storing data in JSON files is like a filing cabinet — you can put things in drawers and find them if you remember where you put them. But imagine 10 people trying to write to the same file simultaneously — the last writer wins and everyone else's changes are lost. A database is like a fully staffed library with a card catalog (indexes), librarians (query optimizer) who find your book in milliseconds, rules preventing you from checking out a book someone already has (transactions/locking), automatic recovery from power failures (write-ahead log), and multiple readers allowed simultaneously (MVCC). FitoGlobe uses PostgreSQL — one of the most battle-tested "libraries" ever built.

Why Files Are Not Enough

ProblemJSON FilesPostgreSQL
Concurrent writesRace conditions — last write winsMVCC — each transaction sees consistent snapshot
Partial failureHalf-written file = corrupt dataWAL — always fully committed or fully rolled back
Finding recordsRead entire file, filter in memoryB-tree index — O(log n) lookup
RelationshipsManual ID matching, no enforcementForeign keys with cascade rules
Data integrityAny value acceptedNOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK constraints enforced
Query languageWrite your own filtering codeSQL — declarative, optimized automatically
Scale10,000 records = slowMillions of records with proper indexes

ACID Properties — The Four Guarantees

Every database transaction in PostgreSQL obeys ACID — four guarantees that make your data trustworthy even when things go wrong.

A
Atomicity
A transaction is all-or-nothing. In FitoGlobe, creating a workout AND its exercises is one transaction — if creating exercises fails, the workout is also rolled back. You never have a workout with no exercises due to a mid-operation crash.
C
Consistency
The database moves from one valid state to another valid state. Constraints (NOT NULL, UNIQUE, FOREIGN KEY) are checked during every transaction. You cannot save a workout with no userId — the foreign key constraint prevents it.
I
Isolation
Concurrent transactions don't interfere. If two users both submit workouts at the same time, PostgreSQL's MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) ensures each sees a consistent view of the data without one corrupting the other's operation.
D
Durability
Once committed, data survives. PostgreSQL's Write-Ahead Log (WAL) writes changes to disk before confirming the commit. If Railway's server crashes mid-operation, the WAL replays committed transactions on restart — your workout data is never lost.
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Database Fundamentals
Explain ACID properties and why they matter for a fitness application like FitoGlobe.
ACID properties are the four guarantees that make database operations trustworthy. Atomicity means that when FitoGlobe creates a workout with multiple exercises, either all records are saved or none are — if the server crashes halfway through, no partial workout exists in the database. Consistency means constraints are always enforced — you can never have a workout that references a non-existent user, or a diet entry with a negative calorie count if we've defined a CHECK constraint. Isolation means that two users logging workouts simultaneously don't corrupt each other's data — PostgreSQL's MVCC gives each transaction a consistent snapshot. Durability means that once a workout is saved (the API returns 201), it survives even if Railway's server immediately powers off — the Write-Ahead Log ensures the data is persisted to disk before acknowledging the commit. Without ACID, a fitness tracking app would have users randomly losing workout history, corrupted progress data, and impossible-to-debug data inconsistencies.
5.2

SQL vs NoSQL — Deep Comparison

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

SQL (Relational) databases organize data into tables with predefined schemas, enforce relationships via foreign keys, and use Structured Query Language for querying. They excel at complex queries joining multiple tables and maintaining strict data integrity. NoSQL databases use flexible schemas with documents (MongoDB), key-value pairs (Redis), wide columns (Cassandra), or graphs (Neo4j). They excel at storing denormalized, schema-flexible data at massive scale where horizontal scaling and developer speed take priority over strict consistency.

▸ The Same Fitness Data — SQL vs NoSQL Representation
SQL (PostgreSQL) — Normalized Tables
-- users table
id | name | email
1 | Ayush | [email protected]
-- workouts table
id | userId | name | duration
10 | 1 | Chest | 60
-- exercises table
id | workoutId | name | sets
20 | 10 | Bench | 4
21 | 10 | Fly | 3
✓ No data duplication
✓ Complex queries: JOIN users + workouts + exercises
✓ Strict data integrity (FK constraints)
✗ Schema changes require migrations
NoSQL (MongoDB) — Document
// workouts collection
{
_id: ObjectId("..."),
userId: "1",
name: "Chest Day",
duration: 60,
exercises: [
{name:"Bench",sets:4},
{name:"Fly",sets:3}
]
}
✓ One query fetches entire workout
✓ Schema can vary per document
✓ Natural for nested/hierarchical data
✗ Harder to query across documents
✗ Data duplication (user name in every doc)

Which Should FitoGlobe Use — And Why PostgreSQL?

🏋️ WHY POSTGRESQL FOR FITOGLOBE

FitoGlobe's data is highly relational: Users have Workouts, Workouts have Exercises, Users have DietEntries, DietEntries have FoodItems, Users have Progress records. These relationships are fixed and well-defined — exactly what SQL was designed for. Questions like "show me all workouts this user logged in the last 30 days, grouped by muscle group, with their total volume" require JOINs across multiple tables — trivial in SQL, painful in MongoDB. The schema is also stable — a Workout always has name, duration, userId. PostgreSQL's strict constraints (NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY) prevent data corruption that would cause bugs in the React UI. For a data-integrity-critical fitness tracking app, PostgreSQL is the right choice.

CriteriaUse PostgreSQL WhenUse MongoDB When
Data structureStructured, well-defined schemaFlexible/varying schema, nested documents
RelationshipsComplex, multi-table joins neededData can be embedded in documents
ConsistencyACID compliance requiredEventual consistency acceptable
QueriesComplex analytical queriesSimple lookups by ID or indexed field
Scale typeVertical scaling + read replicasHorizontal sharding across many servers
Real examplesBanking, fitness tracking, e-commerceContent platforms, catalogs, real-time apps
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — SQL vs NoSQL
When would you choose MongoDB over PostgreSQL and vice versa?
The choice depends on data structure, query patterns, and consistency requirements — not on which is "newer" or "faster." PostgreSQL excels when your data has clear relationships between entities (users, orders, products), when you need complex analytical queries joining multiple tables, when data integrity is non-negotiable (financial transactions, health data), and when your schema is stable. MongoDB excels when documents naturally embed related data (a blog post with its comments), when the schema varies significantly between records (a product catalog where electronics and clothing have completely different attributes), when you need to scale horizontally across many servers, and when developer speed and schema flexibility outweigh strict consistency. A common pragmatic view: start with PostgreSQL — it handles most use cases, has excellent tooling (Prisma, pgAdmin), and you can always migrate later. The "MongoDB is faster" claim is often a myth — properly indexed PostgreSQL at similar scale often outperforms MongoDB. For FitoGlobe, PostgreSQL is clearly the right choice given the relational nature of fitness tracking data and the need for accurate progress analytics.
What is database normalization and when is denormalization appropriate?
Normalization is the process of organizing a database to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity by dividing data into separate related tables. In FitoGlobe, the user's name is stored once in the users table — not copied into every workout or diet entry. When the user changes their name, one update propagates everywhere via the foreign key relationship. This is the correct approach for most data. Denormalization is the deliberate introduction of redundancy to improve read performance. If querying FitoGlobe's leaderboard page required joining 5 tables on every request, the query might become slow. A denormalized approach might store a precomputed totalWorkouts counter on the user record — updated with every workout creation/deletion — so the leaderboard can read one field instead of counting rows. Denormalization trades write complexity (now two records must be kept in sync) for read performance. Use it surgically on proven bottlenecks after profiling, never as a default approach.
5.3

PostgreSQL — Architecture & Key Features

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

PostgreSQL (often called "Postgres") is a free, open-source, object-relational database management system with over 35 years of active development. It is known for standards compliance (SQL:2016), extensibility (custom data types, functions, operators), robustness (ACID compliant, MVCC), and advanced features (full-text search, JSON support, window functions, CTEs). It runs as a separate server process accepting TCP connections on port 5432.

Key PostgreSQL Concepts for FitoGlobe

  1. 1
    MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) PostgreSQL never overwrites existing data when updating. Instead it creates a new version of the row and marks the old version as expired. Multiple transactions can read consistent snapshots simultaneously without locking each other. This is why reads and writes rarely block each other in PostgreSQL.
  2. 2
    WAL (Write-Ahead Log) Before any data change is applied to the actual data files, PostgreSQL records it in the WAL. On crash recovery, PostgreSQL replays WAL entries to restore a consistent state. This is the mechanism behind Durability in ACID. It also enables streaming replication to read replicas.
  3. 3
    B-Tree Indexes The default index type. PostgreSQL automatically creates a B-tree index on every PRIMARY KEY and UNIQUE column. B-trees keep data sorted, enabling O(log n) lookup — finding one workout among 1,000,000 in ~20 comparisons instead of scanning all 1,000,000.
  4. 4
    JSONB Column Type PostgreSQL supports storing JSON data in a binary JSONB column with full indexing support. FitoGlobe could store workout exercise metadata as JSONB rather than a separate exercises table — useful for highly variable data structures.
  5. 5
    Connection Model Each client connection to PostgreSQL spawns a new backend process. This is why connection limits matter — each process consumes ~5-10MB of memory. PostgreSQL's max_connections defaults to 100. On Railway's free tier this limit is lower, making Prisma's connection pool configuration critical.
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — PostgreSQL
What is MVCC and how does it allow concurrent reads and writes without locking?
MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) is PostgreSQL's mechanism for handling concurrent access. When a transaction updates a row, PostgreSQL does not modify the existing row in place. Instead, it creates a new version of the row with a new transaction ID and marks the old version with an expiry transaction ID. Readers see the version that was current at the start of their transaction — they read a consistent snapshot of the database at a point in time, unaffected by concurrent writes. Writers create new versions without disturbing readers. This means reads never block writes and writes never block reads. The old versions (called "dead tuples") are periodically cleaned up by the VACUUM process. For FitoGlobe, this means that while a user is reading their workout history (a potentially slow query with many joins), another user can simultaneously log a new workout without either being blocked.
5.4

What Is an ORM?

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

An ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) is a software layer that translates between the object-oriented paradigm of your application code (JavaScript objects, TypeScript types) and the relational paradigm of a SQL database (tables, rows, columns). Instead of writing raw SQL strings, you interact with the database using native language constructs — function calls, objects, and type-safe operations — while the ORM generates and executes the corresponding SQL queries.

💡 TRANSLATOR ANALOGY

You speak JavaScript. The database speaks SQL. An ORM is a professional translator sitting between you. You say in JavaScript: prisma.workout.findMany({where: {userId: '123'}}). The ORM translates this to SQL: SELECT * FROM workouts WHERE user_id = '123', sends it to PostgreSQL, receives the rows back, and translates them into JavaScript objects that you can directly work with. You never need to speak SQL directly (though knowing SQL helps you understand what's happening).

ApproachCode ExampleProsCons
Raw SQL (pg) db.query("SELECT * FROM workouts WHERE user_id=$1", [id]) Full control, best performance SQL injection risk, no type safety, verbose
Query Builder (Knex) knex('workouts').where('userId', id) Composable, still flexible No type safety, no auto-migrations
ORM (Prisma) prisma.workout.findMany({where:{userId:id}}) Type safety, auto-migrations, readable Less control, learning curve, some N+1 risk
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — ORM
What are the trade-offs of using an ORM vs writing raw SQL?
ORMs provide significant developer productivity benefits but introduce real trade-offs. The advantages include: type safety (Prisma generates TypeScript types from your schema — accessing workout.nonExistentField is a compile-time error), protection against SQL injection (parameterized queries are automatic), database-agnostic code (switch from PostgreSQL to MySQL with minimal changes), auto-generated migrations (schema changes are tracked), and readable code that maps closely to business concepts. The trade-offs are: performance overhead (the ORM must parse, build, and execute queries — adding ~1-5ms per query), loss of control (complex queries with window functions, CTEs, or lateral joins may require raw SQL via Prisma's $queryRaw), potential for N+1 queries (if developers don't understand how ORM loading works — covered in section 5.11), and the ORM becoming a "magic box" where developers don't understand the generated SQL. The pragmatic answer: use an ORM like Prisma for 90% of operations, drop to raw SQL for complex analytical queries where performance matters.
5.5

Prisma ORM — Schema-First, Type-Safe Database Access

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Prisma is a next-generation ORM for Node.js and TypeScript consisting of three tools: Prisma Schema (a declarative data modeling language in schema.prisma), Prisma Migrate (generates and applies SQL migrations from schema changes), and Prisma Client (an auto-generated, type-safe query builder). Unlike traditional ORMs that define models in application code, Prisma uses a schema file as the single source of truth — generating the client, TypeScript types, and migrations all from it.

▸ Prisma's Three-Layer Architecture
SCHEMA LAYER
prisma/schema.prisma
• Define models
• Define relations
• Single source of truth
→
prisma generate
CLIENT LAYER
@prisma/client
• Type-safe queries
• Auto-completion
• Runtime query engine
→
generates SQL
DATABASE LAYER
PostgreSQL
• Executes SQL
• Returns rows
• Enforces constraints
schema.prisma → npx prisma generate → TypeScript types + PrismaClient → your controllers call prisma.workout.findMany() → Prisma Engine translates to SQL → PostgreSQL executes
Prisma Schema Syntax — Core Concepts
PRISMA
1// datasource: tells Prisma which database and where to connect
2datasource db {
3 provider = "postgresql" // Could also be "mysql", "sqlite", "mongodb"
4 url = env("DATABASE_URL") // Read from .env — never hardcode credentials
5}
6
7// generator: defines what Prisma generates from your schema
8generator client {
9 provider = "prisma-client-js" // Generates JavaScript/TypeScript client
10}
11
12// model: maps to a PostgreSQL table
13// Field syntax: fieldName Type Modifier(s) @decorator(s)
14model User {
15 id String @id @default(cuid()) // Primary key, auto-generated CUID
16 email String @unique // UNIQUE constraint — no duplicate emails
17 name String // NOT NULL by default in Prisma
18 password String? // ? = nullable (OAuth users have no password)
19 createdAt DateTime @default(now()) // Auto-set to current timestamp on insert
20 updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt // Auto-updated on every modification
21
22 // Relation fields — not actual DB columns, just navigation properties
23 workouts Workout[] // One user has many workouts
24 dietEntries DietEntry[]
25}
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Prisma
How does Prisma generate type-safe queries and why does this prevent bugs?
When you run prisma generate, Prisma reads schema.prisma and generates a TypeScript client that exactly mirrors your data model. Every model, field, and relation becomes a typed object. This means: (1) Your IDE auto-completes prisma.workout. and shows only valid operations (findMany, create, update, delete). (2) prisma.workout.findMany({where: {userId: req.user.id}}) — if you typo userId as userID, TypeScript throws a compile-time error before your code ever runs. (3) The returned object is typed — accessing workout.name gives you string, not any. This prevents entire categories of bugs: accessing fields that don't exist, passing the wrong type to a query, misspelling field names. In contrast, raw SQL like db.query("SELECT * FROM workouts WHERE user_id=$1") returns any[] — no type safety, and a typo in the SQL string only fails at runtime when that specific code path is executed.
5.6

FitoGlobe Complete Schema — Every Model Explained

backend/prisma/schema.prisma — Complete Annotated Schema
PRISMA
1datasource db { provider = "postgresql"; url = env("DATABASE_URL") }
2generator client { provider = "prisma-client-js" }
3
4// =============================================
5// USER MODEL — Central entity everything relates to
6// =============================================
7model User {
8 id String @id @default(cuid()) // cuid: collision-resistant ID (safer than UUID v4)
9 email String @unique // Enforces no duplicate accounts
10 name String // Display name — required
11 password String? // Null for Google OAuth users
12 googleId String? @unique // Google's user ID (for OAuth)
13 authProvider AuthProvider @default(EMAIL) // Enum: EMAIL or GOOGLE
14 avatar String? // Profile photo URL
15 weight Float? // kg — for BMI/progress calculations
16 height Float? // cm — for BMI
17 dateOfBirth DateTime? // Age-appropriate fitness advice
18 goal String? // "lose_weight", "build_muscle", etc.
19 resetToken String? @unique // Password reset token (hashed)
20 resetTokenExp DateTime? // Reset token expiry (1 hour)
21 createdAt DateTime @default(now())
22 updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt
23 // Relations — these do NOT create columns, they enable Prisma includes
24 workouts Workout[]
25 dietEntries DietEntry[]
26 progressLogs ProgressLog[]
27 @@map("users") // Prisma model "User" maps to PostgreSQL table "users"
28}
29
30// =============================================
31// WORKOUT MODEL
32// =============================================
33model Workout {
34 id String @id @default(cuid())
35 name String // "Chest Day", "Morning Run"
36 duration Int // Minutes — stored as integer
37 notes String? // Optional workout notes
38 date DateTime @default(now()) // When the workout was performed
39 createdAt DateTime @default(now())
40 updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt
41 // Foreign key — links workout to its owner
42 userId String // Actual FK column in DB
43 user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
44 // onDelete: Cascade — if user is deleted, all their workouts are also deleted
45 exercises Exercise[] // One workout has many exercises
46 @@index([userId]) // Index on userId — speeds up "get all workouts for user X"
47 @@index([userId, date]) // Composite index — "workouts for user X in date range"
48 @@map("workouts")
49}
50
51model Exercise {
52 id String @id @default(cuid())
53 name String // "Bench Press", "Squat"
54 sets Int // Number of sets
55 reps Int? // Reps per set (null for time-based exercises)
56 weight Float? // kg lifted (null for bodyweight)
57 duration Int? // Seconds (for planks, cardio)
58 workoutId String
59 workout Workout @relation(fields: [workoutId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
60 @@map("exercises")
61}
62
63model DietEntry {
64 id String @id @default(cuid())
65 mealType MealType // Enum: BREAKFAST, LUNCH, DINNER, SNACK
66 date DateTime @default(now())
67 calories Int // Total calories for this entry
68 protein Float // Grams
69 carbs Float // Grams
70 fat Float // Grams
71 notes String? // "Homemade", "Restaurant"
72 createdAt DateTime @default(now())
73 userId String
74 user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
75 @@index([userId, date])
76 @@map("diet_entries")
77}
78
79model ProgressLog {
80 id String @id @default(cuid())
81 date DateTime @default(now())
82 weight Float? // Body weight snapshot
83 bodyFat Float? // Body fat % (optional)
84 notes String?
85 photos String[] // Array of photo URLs (progress pics)
86 userId String
87 user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
88 @@map("progress_logs")
89}
90
91// Enums — enforced at DB level, prevents invalid strings
92enum AuthProvider { EMAIL GOOGLE }
93enum MealType { BREAKFAST LUNCH DINNER SNACK }

Visual Schema — Table Relationships at a Glance

▸ FitoGlobe Database — Entity Relationship Overview
👤 users
idStringPK
emailStringUQ
nameStringNOT NULL
passwordString?nullable
googleIdString?UQ
weightFloat?nullable
createdAtDateTime@default(now)
💪 workouts
idStringPK
nameStringNOT NULL
durationIntNOT NULL
notesString?nullable
dateDateTime@default
userIdStringFK→users
[userId]Index@@index
🏋️ exercises
idStringPK
nameStringNOT NULL
setsIntNOT NULL
repsInt?nullable
weightFloat?nullable
workoutIdStringFK→workouts
🥗 diet_entries
idStringPK
mealTypeMealTypeenum
caloriesIntNOT NULL
proteinFloatNOT NULL
carbsFloatNOT NULL
fatFloatNOT NULL
userIdStringFK→users
users1──*workouts(cascade)
workouts1──*exercises(cascade)
users1──*diet_entries(cascade)
users1──*progress_logs(cascade)
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Schema Design
Why use cuid() instead of auto-increment integers for primary keys?
Auto-increment integers (1, 2, 3...) seem simpler but have real problems at scale and in distributed systems. First, security: sequential IDs expose business metrics — if a competitor registers and gets user ID 1000, and a week later gets ID 1050, they know you acquired 50 users that week. They can also enumerate resources: GET /api/workouts/1, /api/workouts/2. CUIDs (Collision-resistant Unique IDs) like clx8k2abc123 are non-enumerable — you can't guess another user's workout ID. Second, distributed systems: if you ever run multiple database instances (sharding), auto-increment requires coordination between instances to avoid duplicate IDs. CUIDs are generated client-side (by Prisma's runtime) and are statistically collision-resistant. UUIDs serve the same purpose but CUIDs are shorter and sort better chronologically. Third, consistency: you can generate a CUID before the database insert, enabling optimistic UI updates — the React component can display the new workout with its ID immediately before the server confirms the save.
What does onDelete: Cascade mean and when is it dangerous?
onDelete: Cascade is a referential action that automatically deletes related records when the parent record is deleted. In FitoGlobe, Workout @relation(onDelete: Cascade) means that when a user deletes their account, all their workouts are automatically deleted by PostgreSQL — no additional code needed. Without cascade, deleting a user would fail with a foreign key constraint violation because workouts still reference the user's ID. Cascade is appropriate when child records have no meaning without their parent (exercises without a workout, workouts without a user). It is dangerous when used incorrectly: if you accidentally cascade on @relation in a shared resource scenario (e.g., a "category" referenced by thousands of products), deleting one category would silently delete all its products. Always think carefully: "If the parent is deleted, should the children also disappear, or should they be set to null, or should the deletion be blocked?" The options are: Cascade (delete children), SetNull (set FK to null — requires nullable FK), Restrict (block deletion if children exist, the default), and NoAction (similar to Restrict but checked at end of transaction).
5.7

Database Relations — One-to-Many & Beyond

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Database relations define how tables are connected to each other. A one-to-many (1:N) relation means one record in table A relates to many records in table B (one User has many Workouts). A one-to-one (1:1) relation means each record in A relates to exactly one in B. A many-to-many (M:N) relation means records in A can relate to multiple records in B and vice versa — requires a junction table. Relations are enforced by foreign keys: a column in the child table that references the parent's primary key.

All Three Relation Types — FitoGlobe Examples
PRISMA
1// ============================================
2// ONE-TO-MANY: User → Workouts (most common)
3// One user has many workouts. Each workout belongs to one user.
4// The FK (userId) lives in the CHILD table (workouts)
5// ============================================
6model User {
7 id String @id @default(cuid())
8 workouts Workout[] // "[] = can have many"
9}
10model Workout {
11 id String @id @default(cuid())
12 userId String // The FK column stored in this table
13 user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
14}
15// SQL generated: ALTER TABLE workouts ADD FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id)
16
17// ============================================
18// ONE-TO-ONE: User → UserPreferences
19// Each user has exactly one preferences record
20// ============================================
21model UserPreferences {
22 id String @id @default(cuid())
23 theme String @default("dark")
24 language String @default("en")
25 units String @default("metric")
26 userId String @unique // @unique enforces the 1-to-1 (one pref per user)
27 user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
28}
29
30// ============================================
31// MANY-TO-MANY: Workout ↔ MuscleGroup
32// One workout targets many muscle groups
33// One muscle group is targeted by many workouts
34// Prisma creates a hidden junction table automatically
35// ============================================
36model Workout {
37 id String @id @default(cuid())
38 muscleGroups MuscleGroup[] // Prisma generates _WorkoutToMuscleGroup junction table
39}
40model MuscleGroup {
41 id String @id @default(cuid())
42 name String @unique // "chest", "back", "legs"
43 workouts Workout[]
44}
45// Generated junction table: _WorkoutToMuscleGroup(A: workoutId, B: muscleGroupId)
5.8

Prisma CRUD Operations — Complete Reference

All Prisma Operations — FitoGlobe Context with Generated SQL
JS
1// ============================================
2// READ OPERATIONS
3// ============================================
4
5// findMany: fetch multiple records with filtering, sorting, pagination
6// SQL: SELECT * FROM workouts WHERE user_id=$1 AND duration>=$2 ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20
7const workouts = await prisma.workout.findMany({
8 where: {
9 userId: req.user.id, // Filter by current user
10 duration: { gte: 30 }, // Greater than or equal to 30 minutes
11 date: { gte: new Date('2024-01-01') } // Only this year
12 },
13 orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }, // Newest first
14 take: 20, // Limit to 20 records
15 skip: 0, // Offset for pagination
16 include: { exercises: true }, // JOIN exercises table (avoids N+1)
17 select: { id: true, name: true, duration: true } // Only fetch these columns
18});
19// Note: include and select are mutually exclusive — use one or the other
20
21// findUnique: find exactly one record by unique field
22// SQL: SELECT * FROM workouts WHERE id=$1 LIMIT 1
23const workout = await prisma.workout.findUnique({
24 where: { id: req.params.id } // Only works on @id or @unique fields
25}); // Returns null if not found — check before using
26
27// findFirst: find first record matching non-unique conditions
28const latest = await prisma.workout.findFirst({
29 where: { userId: req.user.id },
30 orderBy: { date: 'desc' } // Get the most recent workout
31});
32
33// count: count matching records without fetching them
34// SQL: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM workouts WHERE user_id=$1
35const total = await prisma.workout.count({ where: { userId: req.user.id } });
36
37// ============================================
38// WRITE OPERATIONS
39// ============================================
40
41// create: insert a new record + nested creates in one transaction
42const workout = await prisma.workout.create({
43 data: {
44 name: 'Chest Day',
45 duration: 60,
46 userId: req.user.id,
47 exercises: {
48 createMany: { data: [ // Creates multiple exercises in one query
49 { name: 'Bench Press', sets: 4, reps: 8, weight: 80 },
50 { name: 'Chest Fly', sets: 3, reps: 12, weight: 15 }
51 ]}
52 }
53 },
54 include: { exercises: true } // Return the exercises in the response
55});
56
57// update: modify specific fields of an existing record
58// SQL: UPDATE workouts SET name=$1, duration=$2 WHERE id=$3
59const updated = await prisma.workout.update({
60 where: { id: req.params.id },
61 data: { name: 'Updated Name', duration: 75 } // Only these fields change
62});
63
64// upsert: create if not exists, update if exists (used in OAuth)
65const user = await prisma.user.upsert({
66 where: { googleId: profile.id },
67 update: { name: profile.displayName }, // Run if user exists
68 create: { email: profile.emails[0].value, name: profile.displayName, googleId: profile.id }
69});
70
71// delete: remove a record (cascade removes exercises automatically)
72await prisma.workout.delete({ where: { id: req.params.id } });
73
74// deleteMany: delete multiple records at once
75await prisma.workout.deleteMany({ where: { userId: req.user.id } }); // delete all user's workouts
76
77// aggregate: compute stats in the database (faster than JS after fetch)
78// SQL: SELECT SUM(duration), AVG(duration), COUNT(*) FROM workouts WHERE user_id=$1
79const stats = await prisma.workout.aggregate({
80 where: { userId: req.user.id },
81 _sum: { duration: true }, // Total minutes worked out
82 _avg: { duration: true }, // Average workout length
83 _count: true // Total number of workouts
84});
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Prisma CRUD
What is the difference between findUnique and findFirst in Prisma?
findUnique and findFirst both return a single record, but they serve different purposes. findUnique can only be used with @id or @unique decorated fields in the schema. It generates a highly optimized SQL query because PostgreSQL knows the result will be exactly zero or one rows — it uses the primary key or unique index for an O(log n) lookup. Prisma validates at the TypeScript level that you're only using unique fields. findFirst works with any field combination but may need to scan more rows. It's used when you need the first record matching non-unique criteria — for example, finding the most recent workout for a user (non-unique userId + ordering).
