    Express — Middleware Chain Node.js receives the TCP data, HTTP parser builds the req object. Express runs middleware in order: helmet() → cors() checks Origin → express.json() parses body → rateLimit() checks IP count → router matches POST /api/workouts.
  5. 5
    Auth Middleware — JWT Verification protect() extracts the Bearer token from the Authorization header. Calls jwt.verify(token, JWT_SECRET) — recomputes the HMAC signature and verifies expiry. Queries the database to confirm the user still exists. Sets req.user = { id, email, name }. Calls next().
  6. 6
    Validation Middleware — Input Verification validateWorkout() runs the Zod schema against req.body. Verifies name is a non-empty string, duration is an integer 1–600, exercises array has at least one item. If invalid → return res.status(400).json({errors}). If valid → req.body = parsedData; next().
  7. 7
    Controller — Business Logic createWorkout() runs. Calls prisma.workout.create({ data: { ...req.body, userId: req.user.id }, include: { exercises: true } }).
  8. 8
    Prisma ORM — Query Generation Prisma translates the create() call into SQL: BEGIN; INSERT INTO workouts ...; INSERT INTO exercises ...; COMMIT; — all in one transaction. Sends to PostgreSQL via connection pool.
  9. 9
    PostgreSQL — Database Execution PostgreSQL validates constraints (NOT NULL, foreign keys), writes to WAL, executes the INSERT, returns the created rows. Prisma maps them to JavaScript objects.
  10. 10
    Response Sent — Back Through Network Controller calls res.status(201).json({ success: true, data: workout }). Express serializes to JSON, sets Content-Type header, sends HTTP response. Network layer returns it to the browser.
  11. 11
    Axios Response Interceptor Response interceptor runs. Status 201 → passes through. await in the service function resolves with response.data.
  12. 12
    React State Update — UI Re-render handleSubmit() receives the new workout. Calls setWorkouts(prev => [data, ...prev]) and setLoading(false). React schedules a re-render. Virtual DOM diffing identifies the new workout card to add. DOM updates — the user sees their new workout appear instantly.
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Request-Response Cycle
A user clicks "Save Workout" and the UI shows a loading spinner but never resolves. How do you debug this?
A systematic debugging approach by layer eliminates the problem space: Layer 1 — Browser Network tab: Open DevTools → Network → look for the POST request. If it shows "Pending" indefinitely, the request is hanging server-side (middleware not calling next(), no response sent). If it shows an error status code, check the response body for the error message. If there's no request at all, the JavaScript code path that calls axios was never reached — debug the React event handler. Layer 2 — Server logs: Check Railway's log viewer. Does the request arrive? Which middleware logs appear? If you see CORS middleware log but not body parser log, the issue is in cors/body parser. If you see "protect: verifying token" but not "createWorkout called", JWT verification failed. Layer 3 — Prisma logs: Enable query logging in development — does the SQL query appear? If not, the issue is before Prisma. If it appears but hangs, PostgreSQL is the issue. Layer 4 — React state: Add console.logs to verify setLoading(false) is reached. Check if the response interceptor is silently catching and redirecting. The loading spinner that never resolves typically means either: the request times out (check network tab for timeout), the controller doesn't call res.json() in a code path (forgotten else branch), or an unhandled promise rejection leaves the controller without sending a response.
7.6

API Versioning

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

API versioning is the practice of maintaining multiple versions of an API simultaneously so that consumers of an older version are not broken when a newer version introduces breaking changes. A breaking change is any modification that requires existing clients to update their code — removing a field, renaming a field, changing a data type, or changing the behavior of an endpoint.

URI Versioning
/api/v1/workouts
/api/v2/workouts
✓ Most explicit and discoverable
✓ Easy to test in browser/Postman
✓ Can be cached per version
✓ FitoGlobe uses this
Header Versioning
Accept-Version: v2
API-Version: 2024-01-01
✓ Clean URLs, no version in path
✗ Not visible in browser address bar
✗ Harder to test, proxy caching issues
✗ Used by Stripe, GitHub API
Query Param Versioning
/workouts?version=2
/workouts?v=2024-01
✓ Easy to add without route changes
✗ Clutters URLs
✗ Can be forgotten or ignored
✗ Breaks URL semantic meaning
backend/server.js — URI Versioning Setup
JS
1// Version 1 routes — existing clients continue to work
2app.use('/api/v1/auth', require('./routes/v1/auth.routes'));
3app.use('/api/v1/workouts', require('./routes/v1/workout.routes'));
4
5// Version 2 routes — breaking changes, new features
6app.use('/api/v2/auth', require('./routes/v2/auth.routes'));
7app.use('/api/v2/workouts', require('./routes/v2/workout.routes'));
8
9// Current FitoGlobe: no versioning yet (early stage)
10// When to add versioning: before first external consumer OR first breaking change
11app.use('/api/auth', require('./routes/auth.routes'));
12app.use('/api/workouts', require('./routes/workout.routes'));
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Versioning
What constitutes a breaking change in a REST API and how do you introduce one safely?
Breaking changes are modifications that force existing API clients to update their code or break silently. Breaking changes include: removing a field from a response (client code accessing response.duration gets undefined), renaming a field (duration → durationMinutes), changing a field's data type (integer → string), adding a required request field to an existing endpoint, changing an endpoint URL, or changing the meaning of an existing field value. Non-breaking changes include: adding new optional fields to a response, adding new optional request fields, adding new endpoints, and changing error messages (as long as status codes stay the same). The safe process for a breaking change is: (1) Implement the change in a new version (/api/v2/workouts) without removing the v1 endpoint. (2) Update your own frontend to use v2. (3) Notify external consumers (if any) with a deprecation timeline. (4) After the deprecation period, sunset v1. A common mistake is making breaking changes in place without versioning — this breaks every client immediately, including your own React app if the deployment is staggered.
7.7

Pagination — Offset vs Cursor

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Pagination is the technique of dividing large result sets into smaller pages delivered in separate API calls. Without pagination, GET /api/workouts would return all of a user's workouts — potentially thousands of records — in a single response, causing slow queries, large payloads, and degraded performance. The two primary approaches are offset-based (page number/skip value) and cursor-based (opaque pointer to the last seen record).

Offset-Based (FitoGlobe uses this)

Request
GET /api/workouts?skip=0&take=20
GET /api/workouts?skip=20&take=20
GET /api/workouts?skip=40&take=20
✓ Jump to any page instantly
✓ Simple to implement (Prisma skip/take)
✓ User can navigate to "page 5"
✗ "Drifting" — new workouts inserted mid-page
✗ Performance degrades at high offsets

Cursor-Based (for infinite scroll / real-time)

Request + Response
GET /api/workouts?take=20
← { data:[], nextCursor: "clx8k2" }

GET /api/workouts?cursor=clx8k2&take=20
← { data:[], nextCursor: "cly9m3" }
✓ Stable — no drift when items added
✓ O(log n) — fast at any offset
✓ Perfect for infinite scroll
✗ Can't jump to page N
✗ More complex implementation
FitoGlobe Pagination — Both Implementations
JS
1// ---- OFFSET PAGINATION (FitoGlobe workouts list) ----
2const getWorkouts = async (req, res) => {
3 const skip = parseInt(req.query.skip) || 0;
4 const take = parseInt(req.query.take) || 20; // Default page size
5 const where = { userId: req.user.id }; // Always scope to current user
6
7 // Run count and data queries in parallel (not sequential)
8 const [total, workouts] = await Promise.all([
9 prisma.workout.count({ where }),
10 prisma.workout.findMany({ where, skip, take, orderBy: { date: 'desc' } })
11 ]);
12
13 res.json({
14 success: true,
15 data: workouts,
16 count: workouts.length, // Items in this page
17 total, // Total matching — for "Page 2 of 5" UI
18 skip,
19 take,
20 hasMore: skip + workouts.length < total // Are there more pages?
21 });
22};
23
24// ---- CURSOR PAGINATION (for infinite scroll — e.g., AI chat history) ----
25const getChatHistory = async (req, res) => {
26 const { cursor, take = 20 } = req.query;
27
28 const messages = await prisma.chatMessage.findMany({
29 where: { userId: req.user.id },
30 take: parseInt(take) + 1, // Fetch one extra to check if more exist
31 cursor: cursor ? { id: cursor } : undefined,
32 skip: cursor ? 1 : 0, // Skip the cursor item itself
33 orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }
34 });
35
36 const hasMore = messages.length > parseInt(take);
37 const data = hasMore ? messages.slice(0, -1) : messages; // Remove extra item
38 const nextCursor = hasMore ? data[data.length - 1].id : null;
39
40 res.json({ success: true, data, nextCursor, hasMore });
41};
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Pagination
Why does offset pagination degrade in performance at high page numbers?
Offset pagination translates to SQL LIMIT n OFFSET m. PostgreSQL must still read and discard m rows before returning the next n. If you request page 1000 with 20 items per page, PostgreSQL reads and discards 19,980 rows, then returns 20. The more pages you go forward, the more rows are wasted on each query. Even with an index, the database must traverse 20,000 index entries. This is called the "deep pagination" problem. Cursor-based pagination avoids this entirely: it uses a WHERE clause — WHERE id > 'cursor_value' — which hits the B-tree index directly regardless of how far into the dataset you are. The query cost is constant O(log n) regardless of page number. For FitoGlobe, most users have under a few hundred workouts — offset pagination performs fine. For datasets with millions of records (like an activity feed or search results), cursor pagination is essential.
7.8

Postman — Professional API Testing Workflow

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Postman is an API platform for building, testing, and documenting APIs. It allows developers to send HTTP requests with any method, headers, body, and authentication without writing code — enabling backend testing independent of the frontend. Features include: collections (organized groups of requests), environments (variable sets for dev/staging/prod), automatic variable injection, test scripts (JavaScript assertions that run after each response), and mock servers.

Complete FitoGlobe Postman Workflow

1
Create Environment — "FitoGlobe Dev"

In Postman → Environments → Create new. Add variables: baseUrl = http://localhost:5000, token = (empty initially). This lets every request use {{baseUrl}}/api/workouts instead of hardcoding the URL.

2
Create Collection — "FitoGlobe API"

Right-click → Add Collection. Organize with folders: Auth, Workouts, Diet, AI, User. Add collection-level Authentication: Type = Bearer Token, Token = {{token}}. Now every request in the collection automatically uses the stored token.

3
Login Request with Auto-Token Extraction

POST {{baseUrl}}/api/auth/login, Body → raw JSON: {"email":"[email protected]","password":"Password123!"}. In the Tests tab, add this script to automatically save the token:

Postman Tests Script — Auto-Extract JWT Token After Login
JS
1// This script runs AFTER the login response is received
2// It automatically saves the token to the environment variable
3// Every subsequent request in the collection uses {{token}} automatically
4
5// pm.test runs assertions — turns green/red in Postman's test results
6pm.test("Status is 200", () => {
7 pm.response.to.have.status(200);
8});
9
10pm.test("Response has token", () => {
11 const json = pm.response.json();
12 pm.expect(json.token).to.be.a('string');
13 // Save token to environment variable for use in all other requests
14 pm.environment.set("token", json.token);
15 // Also save userId for use in workout requests
16 pm.environment.set("userId", json.user.id);
17});
18
19// Now run "Create Workout" request — it automatically has the Bearer token
20// because the collection-level Authorization uses {{token}}
4
Create Workout Request with Tests

POST {{baseUrl}}/api/workouts. Body: {"name":"Test Workout","duration":60,"exercises":[{"name":"Bench Press","sets":4,"reps":8}]}. Tests tab: assert status 201, response has data.id, save pm.environment.set("workoutId", json.data.id).

5
Run Collection — Automated E2E Test Suite

Collection Runner lets you run all requests in sequence with the test assertions. Every request uses data from previous requests via environment variables. This creates an automated end-to-end test: Register → Login → Create → Read → Update → Delete, all assertions verified.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Postman
Why should you use Postman environments instead of hardcoding values in requests?
Postman environments solve several real development problems. Without environments, switching from development to production requires manually editing the URL in every single request — 20+ requests in a collection means 20+ edits and high chance of missing one. With environments, you change one variable (baseUrl) and every request updates simultaneously. The same applies to tokens: without environments, you'd copy-paste the JWT into each request's Authorization header manually after every login. The auto-extract script (saving the token to pm.environment.set("token")) means you log in once and every subsequent request automatically uses the new token. Environments also enable testing against multiple deployment stages: create "Dev" environment pointing to localhost:5000, "Staging" pointing to the staging server, "Production" pointing to Railway — switch between them with a dropdown. No request modification needed. Environment variables can also store dynamic test data: the workoutId created in one request is automatically used in the subsequent Get/Update/Delete requests, creating a self-contained, reproducible test flow.
7.9

API Error Handling Patterns

ℹ️ ERROR HANDLING IS USER EXPERIENCE

Every API error that reaches a user is a user experience failure. Good error handling gives the user (and the frontend developer consuming your API) enough information to understand what went wrong and what to do next — without revealing internal implementation details that could be exploited.

The Three Layers of Error Handling in FitoGlobe

▸ Error Handling Architecture — From Request to Response
LAYER 1: Validation Middleware (400 errors)
Catches malformed/invalid input BEFORE it reaches business logic. Zod schema validation. Returns structured field-level errors immediately. The controller never runs for invalid input.
LAYER 2: Controller try/catch (Business logic errors)
Catches expected errors: resource not found (404), unauthorized access (403), conflicts (409). Uses next(new ApiError(statusCode, message)) to forward to error handler. The controller decides WHAT the error means.
LAYER 3: Error Handler Middleware (500 catch-all)
Centralized error formatter. Translates Prisma errors, JWT errors, and unexpected exceptions into clean JSON responses. Logs full details server-side. Sends safe, minimal info to client. NEVER exposes stack traces in production.
React — Consuming API Errors Consistently
JSX
1// frontend/src/services/workout.service.js
2const createWorkout = async (data) => {
3 try {
4 const response = await api.post('/workouts', data);
5 return response.data; // { success: true, data: workout }
6 } catch (error) {
7 // Axios wraps HTTP errors in error.response
8 if (error.response) {
9 // Server responded with 4xx or 5xx
10 const { status, data } = error.response;
11 if (status === 400 && data.errors) {
12 // Validation errors → show field-level feedback
13 throw new ValidationError(data.errors);
14 }
15 throw new ApiError(status, data.error);
16 } else if (error.request) {
17 // Request sent but no response (network error, timeout)
18 throw new Error('Network error — check your connection');
19 } else {
20 // Request setup error (code bug)
21 throw error;
22 }
23 }
24};
25
26// React component — clear error UX
27const handleSubmit = async () => {
28 try {
29 await workoutService.createWorkout(formData);
30 toast.success('Workout saved!');
31 } catch (err) {
32 if (err instanceof ValidationError) {
33 setFieldErrors(err.errors); // Show per-field error messages
34 } else {
35 toast.error(err.message || 'Failed to save workout');
36 }
37 }
38};
7.10

Rate Limiting — Algorithms & Implementation

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

Rate limiting is a technique that controls the rate of requests a client can make to an API within a given time window. It protects against denial-of-service attacks, brute force attempts, API abuse, and excessive cost from pay-per-use services (like Groq). When a client exceeds the limit, the server responds with 429 Too Many Requests, optionally including a Retry-After header indicating when the client may try again.

Fixed Window Counter
Count requests in a fixed time window (e.g., 100 req/min). Counter resets at window boundary. Simple but has "burst at boundary" problem — 100 requests at 11:59:59 and 100 at 12:00:00 = 200 in 2 seconds.
Window: 12:00:00 - 12:01:00
Request count: 73/100
↓ reset at 12:01:00
Window: 12:01:00 - 12:02:00
Request count: 0/100
Sliding Window Log
Maintains a log of request timestamps. On each request, removes entries older than the window, counts remaining, rejects if over limit. Precise but memory-intensive at scale.
Now: 12:05:00, Window: 60s
Log: [12:04:05, 12:04:23, ..., 12:04:59]
Remove entries < 12:04:00
Count remaining: 47/100 ✓
Token Bucket
Each client has a "bucket" with N tokens. Each request consumes 1 token. Tokens refill at a fixed rate (e.g., 10/second). Allows bursts up to bucket size while enforcing average rate. Used by AWS, Stripe.
Bucket capacity: 100 tokens
Refill rate: 10 tokens/second
Current: 45 tokens
Request → consume 1 → 44 tokens ✓
10 rapid requests → 35 tokens ✓
101st in 1s → 0 tokens → 429 ✗
Leaky Bucket
Requests enter a queue (bucket) and are processed at a fixed rate, regardless of input rate. Excess requests either queue up or are dropped. Smooths out bursts — output rate is always constant.
Input: 1000 req/second burst
Queue capacity: 100
↓ processes at fixed rate
Output: constant 10 req/second
Queue full → excess dropped → 429
backend/server.js — Tiered Rate Limiting for FitoGlobe
JS
1const rateLimit = require('express-rate-limit');
2
3// ---- Global API limiter ----
4const globalLimiter = rateLimit({
5 windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000, // 15 minutes
6 max: 200, // 200 requests per 15 min per IP
7 standardHeaders: true, // Adds X-RateLimit-* headers to response
8 legacyHeaders: false,
9 message: { error: 'Too many requests. Try again in 15 minutes.' }
10});
11app.use('/api', globalLimiter);
12
13// ---- Auth endpoint limiter — stricter (prevent brute force) ----
14const authLimiter = rateLimit({
15 windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000,
16 max: 5, // Only 5 login attempts per 15 min
17 message: { error: 'Too many login attempts. Please wait 15 minutes.' },
18 skipSuccessfulRequests: true // Don't count successful logins against the limit
19});
20app.use('/api/auth/login', authLimiter);
21app.use('/api/auth/forgot-password', authLimiter);
22
23// ---- AI endpoint limiter — cost protection ----
24// Groq charges per token — unlimited requests = runaway costs
25const aiLimiter = rateLimit({
26 windowMs: 60 * 1000, // 1 minute window
27 max: 10, // 10 AI requests per minute
28 keyGenerator: (req) => req.user?.id || req.ip, // Limit per user, not just IP
29 message: { error: 'AI rate limit reached. Please wait a moment before asking again.' }
30});
31app.use('/api/ai', aiLimiter);
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Rate Limiting
Why should the AI endpoint have stricter rate limiting than other API endpoints?
The AI endpoint (/api/ai/chat) calls the Groq API, which charges per token — every request has a real monetary cost. Without AI-specific rate limiting, several attack scenarios become possible: (1) Cost amplification attacks: a malicious user writes a bot that sends thousands of messages per minute, each requiring Groq API calls — the attacker spends nothing but your Groq bill spikes instantly. (2) Prompt flooding: even legitimate users on free tiers could accidentally exhaust your Groq quota by sending very long conversation histories in rapid succession. (3) Competitive abuse: competitors could systematically query your AI to extract training data or knowledge. The AI rate limiter is configured per-user ID (not just IP) because VPN users could bypass IP-based limits, and the natural usage pattern for AI (one conversation at a time) means 10 requests/minute is generous for legitimate use but prevents scripted abuse. This is also why FitoGlobe uses a stricter keyGenerator on the AI limiter — req.user.id after authentication, falling back to IP only if somehow unauthenticated (which shouldn't happen since AI routes are protected).
7.11

API Keys vs JWT — When to Use Each

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

An API key is a long, random string (e.g., sk-proj-abc123...) that identifies and authenticates a client — typically a server or application, not a human user. API keys are stateful: they are stored in a database and can be revoked instantly. A JWT is a self-contained, signed token identifying a specific user with encoded claims and an expiry. JWTs are stateless — verification requires only the secret, not a database lookup.

AspectAPI KeysJWT
Who uses itServer-to-server (applications, bots)Human users authenticating to a web app
ExpiryNo built-in expiry (permanent until revoked)Built-in expiry claim (exp)
RevocationInstant — delete from DBCannot revoke before expiry (without blocklist)
Carries user dataNo (just an opaque identifier)Yes (user ID, email, roles in payload)
DB lookup requiredYes — always look up API key in DBNo — verify signature in memory
How it's sentHeader (X-API-Key: sk-...) or query paramHeader (Authorization: Bearer eyJ...)
FitoGlobe exampleGroq API key, future partner API accessUser login sessions
Real-world examplesStripe, OpenAI, Google Maps APIAuth0, Firebase Auth, most web apps
🏋️ FITOGLOBE USES BOTH

FitoGlobe as a consumer: The backend uses the Groq API key (GROQ_API_KEY in .env) to call Groq's API from the ai.service.js file. This is a server-to-server API key — never exposed to the browser.

FitoGlobe as a provider: Users authenticate with JWT (login → token → protected routes). If FitoGlobe ever provides a public API for third-party fitness apps, those partners would use API keys issued by FitoGlobe.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — API Keys vs JWT
Why should the Groq API key never be exposed to the browser, and how does FitoGlobe prevent this?
The Groq API key is a secret credential — anyone who possesses it can make API calls billed to your Groq account, with no rate limiting from Groq's perspective (only your own server-side limits apply). If the key were in the React frontend code (as a VITE_GROQ_API_KEY), it would be compiled into the JavaScript bundle and visible to anyone who opens browser DevTools → Sources or downloads the JS file. Malicious users could extract it and use it directly, bypassing FitoGlobe's rate limiting entirely. FitoGlobe prevents this through architecture: the React frontend never communicates with Groq directly. It sends POST /api/ai/chat to the Express backend (with the user's JWT). The Express AI controller reads the Groq key from process.env.GROQ_API_KEY (server-side, never sent to the browser), calls the Groq API on the backend, and returns only the AI response to the frontend. The backend acts as a trusted proxy — it validates the user's JWT (ensuring they're authenticated), enforces the rate limit (10 AI calls/minute), and then calls Groq with the server-side key. This architecture is the correct pattern for any pay-per-use API key.
7.12

Webhooks — Event-Driven API Integration

📖 FORMAL DEFINITION

A webhook is an HTTP callback — a user-defined HTTP endpoint that a service calls when a specific event occurs, rather than requiring the client to repeatedly poll for changes. Webhooks implement the Observer pattern at the HTTP level: you register your URL with a service, and that service sends an HTTP POST to your URL whenever the subscribed event fires. This is sometimes called a "reverse API" — instead of your app calling their API, their system calls yours.

💡 DOORBELL vs CONSTANTLY CHECKING THE DOOR

Polling is like standing at your door and opening it every 5 minutes to check if there's a package. Wasteful — 99% of the time there's nothing there. Webhooks are like a doorbell — you install it once, and it rings (calls your URL) exactly when the delivery person arrives. You only process an event when it actually happens.

▸ Polling vs Webhooks — FitoGlobe Future Use Case (Payment Events)
❌ POLLING (inefficient)
every 30 seconds:
GET stripe.com/payment/status
← "pending"
GET stripe.com/payment/status
← "pending"
GET stripe.com/payment/status
← "pending"
... (50 requests over 25 minutes)
GET stripe.com/payment/status
← "succeeded"
✓ WEBHOOK (efficient)
Register once:
POST stripe.com/webhooks
url: fitoglobe.com/webhooks/stripe
events: ["payment.succeeded"]

... user pays ...

Stripe calls FitoGlobe:
POST fitoglobe.com/webhooks/stripe
{ type: "payment.succeeded", ... }
backend/routes/webhook.routes.js — Secure Stripe Webhook Handler
JS
1const stripe = require('stripe')(process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY);
2
3// ✅ CRITICAL: Use express.raw() for webhooks, NOT express.json()
4// Stripe signature verification requires the RAW request body (before JSON parsing)
5// If express.json() runs first, the body is already parsed and signature fails
6router.post('/stripe', express.raw({ type: 'application/json' }), (req, res) => {
7 const sig = req.headers['stripe-signature'];
8 let event;
9
10 try {
11 // Verify that the webhook came from Stripe, not a random attacker
12 // Stripe signs webhooks with STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET — verify before processing
13 event = stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(req.body, sig, process.env.STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET);
14 } catch (err) {
15 // Invalid signature = forged request — reject immediately
16 return res.status(400).json({ error: 'Invalid webhook signature' });
17 }
18
19 // ✅ IMPORTANT: Always respond 200 quickly before processing
20 // If processing takes too long, Stripe retries the webhook
21 // Acknowledge receipt, then process asynchronously
22 res.status(200).json({ received: true });
23
24 // Handle different event types
25 switch (event.type) {
26 case 'payment_intent.succeeded':
27 handlePaymentSuccess(event.data.object); // Async, don't await here
28 break;
29 case 'customer.subscription.deleted':
30 handleSubscriptionCancel(event.data.object);
31 break;
32 default:
33 console.log(`Unhandled webhook event: ${event.type}`);
34 }
35});
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A
🎤 INTERVIEW QUESTIONS — Webhooks
How do you ensure a webhook is genuinely from the expected source and not from an attacker?
Without verification, any attacker could POST a fake webhook to fitoglobe.com/webhooks/stripe with a fabricated payload like {"type":"payment.succeeded","amount":9999} — triggering FitoGlobe to grant premium access without real payment. Webhook security relies on signature verification. When you register your webhook URL with Stripe, Stripe gives you a webhook signing secret (whsec_abc123...). When Stripe sends a webhook, it computes HMAC_SHA256(rawBody + timestamp, webhookSecret) and includes it in the Stripe-Signature header. Your server recomputes the same HMAC and compares it. If they match, the payload is genuine — only Stripe (which has the same secret) could have produced that signature. This is why the webhook handler must receive the raw body (before JSON parsing) — even a single whitespace change in the body produces a completely different HMAC. Using express.raw() for the webhook route (instead of express.json()) is essential. Always reject any webhook where the signature doesn't match — don't log the event, don't process it, return 400.
What is webhook idempotency and why must your webhook handler implement it?
Webhook delivery is at-least-once, not exactly-once — if your server takes too long to respond (>30 seconds for Stripe), the sending service retries the webhook assuming delivery failed. This means your webhook handler might receive the same event multiple times. Without idempotency protection, a "payment.succeeded" webhook delivered twice would grant premium access twice, or a "subscription.created" would create two subscription records. Idempotency in webhook handling means: processing the same event multiple times produces the same result as processing it once. Implementation: store received event IDs in a database table. Before processing, check if this event ID already exists — if yes, return 200 (acknowledge it) but skip processing. Stripe, GitHub, and most webhook providers include a unique event ID in the payload for exactly this purpose. For FitoGlobe's premium subscription webhooks: prisma.processedWebhook.upsert({ where: {eventId}, create: {eventId, processedAt}, update: {} }) — if the event was already processed, the upsert is a no-op.
✓ PART 7 COMPLETE
APIs — Comprehensive

You now understand APIs from first principles — REST constraints, naming conventions, every FitoGlobe endpoint, the complete request-response cycle, rate limiting algorithms, and webhook security.

REST Principles ✓ API Design ✓ All Endpoints ✓ Request Cycle ✓ Versioning ✓ Pagination ✓ Postman ✓ Rate Limiting ✓ Webhooks ✓
MERGE INSTRUCTION: Copy <div class="content-area"> contents and paste after Part 6's last </section> in the master file.
PART 08 — CLOUD & PLATFORMS

Deploy Everywhere,
Break Nothing

From your laptop to the internet — how Vercel, Railway, Docker, CI/CD, DNS, and cloud pricing actually work. Everything you need to ship FitoGlobe (and any real app) to production confidently.

Vercel Railway Supabase Docker CI/CD DNS AWS Free Tier
8.1

What Is "The Cloud"?

📖 DEFINITION

The cloud is simply someone else's computer that you rent by the hour (or second). Instead of buying a physical server, you pay AWS, Google, Microsoft, or smaller platforms to run your code on their hardware worldwide.

🏠 ANALOGY

Think of it like renting a flat vs. buying a house. You don't own the building but you can move in immediately, scale your rooms as needed, and leave whenever you want — without worrying about fixing the plumbing.

Before the cloud, every startup had to buy physical servers, put them in a data centre, hire people to maintain them, and hope they guessed the right capacity. If you got featured on the news and traffic spiked 100x overnight, your servers would crash. The cloud solved this.

On-Premise vs Cloud — The Shift
BEFORE — ON-PREMISE
🏢 Buy physical servers ($5k–$50k)
🔧 Hire sysadmin team
⏳ 2–4 week setup lead time
📉 Over-provision or under-provision
🔒 Fixed to one location
AFTER — CLOUD
💳 Pay per second / GB used
🤖 Managed by platform
⚡ Deploy in minutes
📈 Auto-scale to demand
🌍 Global edge network

The Three Cloud Service Models

ModelWhat You ManageWhat Provider ManagesExamples
IaaS
Infrastructure as a Service		OS, runtime, apps, dataPhysical servers, networking, storageAWS EC2, Google Compute, Azure VMs
PaaS
Platform as a Service		Your app code & data onlyOS, runtime, scaling, serversRailway, Heroku, Render, Fly.io
BaaS
Backend as a Service		Frontend + business logicAuth, DB, storage, realtime, APIsSupabase, Firebase, Appwrite
SaaS
Software as a Service		Nothing (just use it)EverythingGmail, Notion, Figma, Vercel (for end users)

Most Asked: "What is the difference between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS?"

Use the pizza analogy: IaaS = you cook at home with rented kitchen; PaaS = you order delivery ingredients, they cook; SaaS = you order a full pizza. For FitoGlobe: Vercel (PaaS for frontend), Railway (PaaS for backend), Supabase (BaaS for DB). You wrote zero infrastructure code.

8.2

Hosting Models Explained

Even within "cloud", there are fundamentally different models for how your code runs. Understanding this changes how you think about performance, pricing, and cold starts.

Hosting Model Spectrum — Control vs Convenience
← More Control More Convenience →
🖥️
VPS / IaaS
SSH in, install everything. Full power. EC2, DigitalOcean Droplet
📦
Containers
Docker on Fly.io, Railway, ECS. Reproducible, portable
🚂
PaaS
Push code, platform handles rest. Railway, Render, Heroku
⚡
Serverless
Functions only. Vercel Edge, Cloudflare Workers, AWS Lambda

Serverless Deep Dive — What Actually Happens

Serverless doesn't mean "no server" — it means you don't manage the server. When a request comes in, the platform spins up a container, runs your function, returns the result, and shuts it down. This is called the Function as a Service (FaaS) model.

⚡ COLD STARTS — THE KEY TRADEOFF

If no one has used your function for a while, the platform needs to spin up a new container. This takes 100ms–3000ms depending on the platform and runtime. For a workout tracking app, a 2s delay on the first request of the day is very noticeable. Solutions:

  • Keep-alive pings — hit your own endpoint every 10 minutes (Railway/Render free tier trick)
  • Paid tier — Vercel Pro and Railway paid keep functions warm
  • Edge functions — run closer to the user, faster cold starts (~50ms)
  • Provisioned concurrency — AWS Lambda option to pre-warm functions
🏋️ FITOGLOBE CONTEXT

FitoGlobe uses Vercel for the React frontend (served as static assets via CDN — zero cold starts) and Railway for the Node.js/Express backend (always-on container on paid tier). The database on Railway PostgreSQL is persistent, not serverless, so queries are fast.

Interview: "Explain cold starts and how you'd solve them"

Cold starts happen in FaaS when a function hasn't been invoked recently and the container needs to initialize. Key fix strategies: keep-alive pings for free tier, provisioned concurrency for AWS Lambda in production, edge runtimes for lightweight functions near users. Mention that Node.js has smaller cold starts than Python, which has smaller starts than Java (due to JVM init).

8.3

Vercel Deep Dive

📖 DEFINITION

Vercel is a frontend cloud platform purpose-built for frameworks like React, Next.js, Vue, and Svelte. It gives you automatic deployments on git push, a global CDN, preview URLs for every PR, and serverless functions — all from a single config file (or zero config).

How Vercel Deployment Works — Step by Step

  • 1
    Git Push to GitHubYou push code to main branch. Vercel's GitHub App detects the push via webhook.
  • 2
    Build TriggeredVercel clones your repo, installs dependencies (npm ci), and runs your build command (npm run build → vite build).
  • 3
    Output AnalysisVercel reads the dist/ folder. Static HTML/CSS/JS assets are identified. Any /api folders become serverless functions.
  • 4
    CDN DistributionAssets are pushed to Vercel's Edge Network — 100+ PoPs (Points of Presence) worldwide. A user in Mumbai gets files from the Mumbai edge, not a US server.
  • 5
    Deployment URL GeneratedEvery deployment gets a unique URL: fitoglobe-abc123.vercel.app. Your main domain is aliased to the latest production deployment.
  • 6
    Instant Rollback AvailableEvery previous deployment is kept. One click in dashboard or vercel rollback CLI reverts immediately.

vercel.json — Configuration File

vercel.json — FitoGlobe config
JSON
1{
2 "buildCommand": "npm run build", // overrides auto-detect
3 "outputDirectory": "dist", // where Vite puts built files
4 "framework": "vite", // tells Vercel which preset
5 "rewrites": [
6 { // CRITICAL for React Router SPAs
7 "source": "/((?!api/.*).*)", // all non-/api routes
8 "destination": "/index.html" // always serve index.html
9 } // React Router handles the rest
10 ],
11 "headers": [
12 {
13 "source": "/assets/(.*)", // all Vite-hashed assets
14 "headers": [{ // cache aggressively
15 "key": "Cache-Control",
16 "value": "public, max-age=31536000, immutable"
17 }]
18 }
19 ]
20}
⚠️ THE MOST COMMON VERCEL MISTAKE

Deploying a React SPA without the rewrites rule (line 6–9 above). Without it, navigating to yourapp.com/dashboard directly returns a 404 because Vercel tries to find a physical /dashboard/index.html file, which doesn't exist. React Router handles routing in the browser only. Always add this rewrite.

⚠ Common Mistakes with Vercel
Committing .env files to git

Your API keys end up in your GitHub repo, visible to the world. Vercel reads env vars from its dashboard, not from committed files.

Set env vars in Vercel Dashboard → Settings → Environment Variables

Separate values for Development, Preview, and Production environments. Use VITE_ prefix for frontend-accessible vars.

Using process.env in React code

process.env is a Node.js API — it doesn't exist in the browser. Vite uses import.meta.env.

Use import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL in Vite/React code

And always prefix with VITE_ — unprefixed vars are private (build-time only, not exposed to the browser).

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A — VERCEL
How does Vercel serve a React SPA? What's the difference from a traditional server?
Vercel builds your React app into static HTML/CSS/JS files and distributes them across a global CDN. There's no server computing a response for each request — the CDN just returns files. A traditional server (Express, Django) runs code on every request. This makes Vercel deployments near-instant and infinitely scalable for frontends. The tradeoff is you need a separate backend server for any dynamic logic or database access.
What's a Preview Deployment and why is it useful?
Every PR/branch pushed to GitHub automatically gets its own deployment URL (e.g., fitoglobe-git-feature-login.vercel.app). This means QA testing without touching production, sharing work-in-progress with stakeholders, and automatic cleanup when the branch is deleted. It's one of Vercel's killer features and a strong argument for using it in team environments.
8.4

Railway Deep Dive

📖 DEFINITION

Railway is a platform for deploying backend services, databases, and workers. Unlike Vercel which specialises in frontend, Railway runs persistent long-lived processes — your Node.js/Express API stays running 24/7, waiting for requests. It detects your language/framework and handles infrastructure automatically.

Railway vs Render vs Fly.io vs Heroku

PlatformFree TierCold StartsDB IncludedDXBest For
Railway$5 credit/moNo (paid tier)Yes★★★★★Full-stack backends + DB together
RenderYes (sleeps)Yes (~30s)Yes★★★★☆Free hobby projects
Fly.ioYes (limited)MinimalExtra setup★★★☆☆Docker containers, global edge
HerokuNo (paid only)NoYes★★★★☆Legacy apps, mature ecosystem

Deploying FitoGlobe Backend to Railway

Railway deployment — required files
BASH + JSON
1# package.json — Railway needs a "start" script
2{
3 "scripts": {
4 "start": "node src/server.js", // Railway runs this
5 "dev": "nodemon src/server.js" // local only
6 }
7}
8
9# .nvmrc — pin Node version (optional but recommended)
1020.11.0
11
12# server.js — CRITICAL: use process.env.PORT
13const PORT = process.env.PORT || 5000;
14// Railway assigns a random PORT env var — if you hardcode
15// port 5000, Railway can't route traffic to your server!
16app.listen(PORT, () => {
17 console.log(`Server on port ${PORT}`);
18});
🚨 #1 RAILWAY DEPLOYMENT FAILURE REASON

Hardcoding app.listen(5000) instead of app.listen(process.env.PORT). Railway dynamically assigns a port and sets it as an environment variable. If you ignore it and listen on 5000, Railway's load balancer can't find your service and your deployment reports "service unavailable". This catches nearly every beginner.

Prisma Migrations on Railway

Run migrations automatically on deploy
JSON
1{
2 "scripts": {
3 "start": "prisma migrate deploy && node src/server.js",
4 // prisma migrate deploy applies pending migrations
5 // does NOT create new migrations — safe for production
6 // runs before the server starts on every deploy
7 }
8}
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A — DEPLOYMENT
Walk me through deploying a full-stack app from scratch
1. Push backend to Railway: connect GitHub repo, set env vars (DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET, etc.), ensure process.env.PORT is used.
2. Add Railway PostgreSQL plugin — automatically injects DATABASE_URL.
3. Run prisma migrate deploy on first deploy.
4. Push frontend to Vercel: connect GitHub repo, set VITE_API_URL to Railway backend URL, add SPA rewrite rule.
5. Add custom domain if needed. Total time: ~20 minutes.
8.5

Supabase vs Firebase (BaaS)

Both Supabase and Firebase are Backend as a Service (BaaS) platforms — they give you a full backend (database, auth, storage, realtime) without writing server code. The right choice depends heavily on your data model and philosophy.

FeatureSupabaseFirebase (Firestore)
DatabasePostgreSQL (relational)NoSQL (document-based)
Query LanguageSQL + Supabase JS SDKFirestore API (limited queries)
Open SourceYes — self-hostableNo — Google lock-in
RealtimeYes (Postgres replication)Yes (first-class feature)
AuthBuilt-in (Email, OAuth, OTP)Built-in (many providers)
StorageYes (S3-compatible)Yes (Firebase Storage)
Complex QueriesFull SQL powerLimited (no JOINs)
Free Tier2 free projects, 500MB DBGenerous (Spark plan)
Pricing at ScalePredictableCan spike unexpectedly
Best ForRelational data, traditional appsRealtime-heavy, mobile apps
🏋️ FITOGLOBE DECISION

FitoGlobe uses Railway PostgreSQL + Prisma (not Supabase/Firebase) because we want full SQL control and a custom backend for business logic. However, Supabase would be a valid choice if we wanted to move faster on MVP — use Supabase Auth instead of writing JWT code, Supabase Storage for workout videos, and Supabase Realtime for live workout sessions.

Interview: "Why would you choose Supabase over building your own backend?"

When speed of shipping matters more than control. Supabase gives you auth, file storage, realtime subscriptions, and a PostgreSQL database in 10 minutes. The tradeoff: you're constrained by what Supabase supports, harder to add complex business logic, and Row Level Security (RLS) policies can become complex. For prototypes, MVPs, and CRUD-heavy apps: Supabase wins. For apps with complex server-side logic, payment processing, or microservices: custom backend wins.

8.6

CI/CD Pipelines

📖 DEFINITION

CI (Continuous Integration) = automatically run tests and checks whenever code is pushed. CD (Continuous Deployment) = automatically deploy to production when CI passes. Together they ensure bugs are caught early and shipping is automatic, not manual.

🏭 ANALOGY

CI/CD is like a factory assembly line with quality control stations. Every product (code change) goes through: quality inspection (tests), approval (PR review), and automated packaging/shipping (deployment). No product reaches customers without passing the line.

CI/CD Pipeline — Git Push to Production
👨‍💻 git push origin main
→
GitHub Webhook triggers
→
GitHub Actions runner spins up
↓
Install deps (npm ci)
→
Run linter (eslint)
→
Run tests (jest)
→
Build (vite build)
❌ FAIL → Notify dev
Deployment blocked
✓ PASS → Auto deploy
to Vercel + Railway

GitHub Actions — FitoGlobe Workflow

.github/workflows/ci.yml
YAML
1name: FitoGlobe CI/CD
2
3on:
4 push:
5 branches: [main] # trigger on push to main
6 pull_request:
7 branches: [main] # also run on PR to main
8
9jobs:
10 test-and-build:
11 runs-on: ubuntu-latest # fresh Ubuntu VM each time
12 steps:
13 - uses: actions/checkout@v4 # clone the repo
14 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
15 with:
16 node-version: '20' # pin Node version
17 cache: 'npm' # cache node_modules
18 - run: npm ci # install exact versions from lockfile
19 - run: npm run lint # catch code style issues
20 - run: npm test -- --ci # run tests, exit on fail
21 - run: npm run build # verify build succeeds
22 env:
23 VITE_API_URL: ${{ secrets.VITE_API_URL }} # use GitHub Secrets
ℹ️ npm ci vs npm install

Always use npm ci in CI/CD. It installs exactly what's in package-lock.json, deletes and recreates node_modules, and fails if lockfile is out of sync. npm install resolves and potentially updates versions, which can cause silent breaking changes.

Interview: "What's the difference between CI and CD?"

CI is about integration confidence — every push runs tests, ensuring the codebase always works. CD is about deployment automation — no manual steps to ship to production. They're separate concerns. Some teams do CI without CD (manual deploy gate), some do both. Mention: blue/green deployments, canary releases, and feature flags as advanced CD strategies that reduce deployment risk.

8.7

Environment Variables in Production

Environment variables in production are not the same as your local .env file. In production, you set them through the platform's UI or CLI — they're stored securely, encrypted at rest, and injected into your process at runtime.

FitoGlobe — Complete Env Var Map

VariableWhere SetWho Reads ItWhy Needed
DATABASE_URLRailway (auto-injected)Prisma / backendPostgreSQL connection string
JWT_SECRETRailway dashboardBackend (auth)Sign/verify JWTs — must be 32+ chars
GOOGLE_CLIENT_IDRailway dashboardBackend (OAuth)Google OAuth flow
GROQ_API_KEYRailway dashboardBackend (AI service)AI workout generation — never expose to frontend
VITE_API_URLVercel dashboardFrontend (axios)Backend API base URL
VITE_APP_NAMEVercel dashboardFrontend (UI)Display name, safe to expose
SMTP_PASSWORDRailway dashboardBackend (email)Nodemailer SMTP auth
NODE_ENVAuto-set by RailwayBackend everywhere"production" enables optimisations
🚨 CRITICAL SECURITY RULE

API keys like GROQ_API_KEY, JWT_SECRET, SMTP_PASSWORD must NEVER have the VITE_ prefix. Any variable prefixed with VITE_ is embedded into the compiled JS bundle and visible to anyone who opens DevTools. Use Vite-prefixed vars only for non-sensitive config like API URLs and feature flags.

⚠ Common Env Var Mistakes
Pushing .env to GitHub

Even if you later delete the file, git history retains it permanently. Secrets can be extracted from old commits.

Add .env to .gitignore before first commit

Run git rm --cached .env if it was accidentally tracked. Rotate all exposed secrets immediately.

Same .env for dev and production

Using the same DATABASE_URL in local dev and production means a mistake in dev code could modify real user data.

Always separate dev and production databases

Vercel and Railway both have separate env var scopes for development, preview, and production environments.

8.8

DNS & Custom Domains

📖 DEFINITION

DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (fitoglobe.com) into IP addresses (76.76.21.21) that computers use to find servers. Custom domains require you to point DNS records from your domain registrar to your hosting provider.

Adding a Custom Domain to Vercel — Step by Step

  • 1
    Buy a domainFrom Namecheap, Cloudflare Registrar, or Google Domains. ~$10/year for a .com.
  • 2
    Add domain in VercelProject → Settings → Domains → Add fitoglobe.com and www.fitoglobe.com. Vercel shows you DNS records to set.
  • 3
    Set DNS recordsAt your registrar, add an A record: @ → 76.76.21.21 (Vercel's IP). Add CNAME: www → cname.vercel-dns.com.
  • 4
    Wait for propagationDNS changes take 1–48 hours to propagate worldwide (usually under 30 min). Check with dig fitoglobe.com.
  • 5
    SSL certificate auto-issuedVercel automatically provisions a Let's Encrypt TLS certificate. Your site is now HTTPS without any extra setup.

DNS Record Types You Must Know

RecordPurposeExample
AMaps domain to IPv4 addressfitoglobe.com → 76.76.21.21
AAAAMaps domain to IPv6 addressfitoglobe.com → 2606:4700::1
CNAMEAlias — maps domain to another domainwww → cname.vercel-dns.com
MXMail server routingFor receiving email at @fitoglobe.com
TXTVerification + SPF/DKIM for emailDomain ownership proof for Google, SSL
NSName servers — who controls DNSPoint to Cloudflare for DNS management
✓ PRO TIP — USE CLOUDFLARE FOR FREE

Point your domain's nameservers to Cloudflare (free tier). You get: faster DNS resolution, DDoS protection, free SSL, analytics, and the ability to manage all DNS in one place for all your projects. Cloudflare's DNS resolves in under 11ms vs 50–200ms for registrar DNS.

8.9

Docker Introduction

📖 DEFINITION

Docker packages your application and all its dependencies (Node.js version, npm packages, OS libraries) into a container — a portable, isolated unit that runs the same way on any machine. "It works on my machine" becomes "it works in the container, everywhere."

📦 ANALOGY

A Docker container is like a shipping container. Before shipping containers, every cargo ship was different — goods had to be repacked each time. After, everything fits in a standard container that loads onto any ship, truck, or train without modification. Docker does this for software.

VM vs Container — Key Difference

Virtual Machine vs Docker Container
VIRTUAL MACHINE
App A
Full OS (2GB+)
Hypervisor
Host OS + Hardware

Heavy. Each VM has full OS. Slow boot (minutes).

DOCKER CONTAINER
App A
App B
Docker Engine (shares OS kernel)
Host OS + Hardware

Lightweight. Shares OS kernel. Fast boot (seconds).

Dockerfile for FitoGlobe Backend

backend/Dockerfile — production-ready
DOCKERFILE
1# Stage 1: Dependencies
2FROM node:20-alpine AS deps # alpine = tiny Linux (5MB vs 900MB)
3WORKDIR /app
4COPY package*.json ./ # copy lockfile first (layer cache)
5RUN npm ci --only=production # install prod deps only
6
7# Stage 2: Build (Prisma client generation)
8FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
9WORKDIR /app
10COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
11COPY . .
12RUN npx prisma generate # generate Prisma client
13
14# Stage 3: Production runner
15FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
16WORKDIR /app
17ENV NODE_ENV=production
18COPY --from=builder /app . # copy built app
19EXPOSE 5000 # document which port we use
20CMD ["node", "src/server.js"] # the command to run
ℹ️ WHY MULTI-STAGE BUILDS?

Without multi-stage builds, your final Docker image contains all build tools, dev dependencies, and source files — potentially 800MB+. With multi-stage builds, the final image contains only what's needed to run (Node, prod deps, compiled files) — often under 150MB. Smaller image = faster deployments, less attack surface, lower storage costs.

Interview: "What is Docker and why do developers use it?"

Docker solves the "works on my machine" problem by packaging the app with all its dependencies into a container. Key benefits: environment consistency (dev, staging, prod run identical containers), fast onboarding (new dev runs docker compose up instead of 2 hours of setup), portability (ship to any cloud), and isolation (apps don't interfere with each other). Containers are lighter than VMs because they share the host OS kernel.

8.10

AWS Overview for Full-Stack Devs

AWS has 200+ services. For a full-stack developer, only ~10 are essential to understand. Here's the map:

🖥️
EC2
Elastic Compute Cloud

Virtual servers in the cloud. You pick OS, CPU, RAM. Requires manual setup. Equivalent to a VPS but infinitely configurable and scalable.

IaaSVMsFlexible
⚡
Lambda
Serverless Functions

Run functions without servers. Pay per invocation. Perfect for event-driven tasks: image resizing, cron jobs, webhooks. Cold starts exist.

FaaSServerlessEvents
🪣
S3
Simple Storage Service

Infinite object storage. Store user uploads, static assets, backups. Highly durable (11 nines). Used by virtually every production app for file storage.

StorageCDNFiles
🗄️
RDS
Relational Database Service

Managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, Aurora. Automatic backups, failover, read replicas. More expensive than Railway/Supabase but enterprise-grade reliability.

PostgreSQLManaged DB
🌐
CloudFront
CDN

AWS's global CDN. Cache assets at 400+ edge locations worldwide. Pair with S3 for blazing-fast static site delivery. Vercel uses CloudFront internally.

CDNCacheGlobal
🔒
IAM
Identity & Access Management

Control who can do what in AWS. Create roles, policies, and service accounts. The most complex but most critical AWS service to understand for security.

SecurityRolesPolicies
ℹ️ AWS vs RAILWAY FOR FITOGLOBE

Railway is the right choice for FitoGlobe at startup scale. AWS becomes relevant when: you need multiple regions, fine-grained autoscaling, specific compliance (HIPAA, SOC2), or a team of DevOps engineers to manage infrastructure. Rule of thumb: start PaaS, move to AWS when your team and scale demand it.

8.11

Free Tier Limits — What You Actually Get

Free tiers are real and usable for small projects, but they have limits that will catch you if you don't understand them. Here's exactly what you get and where the gotchas are.

PlatformFree TierLimitsGotchas
Vercel Hobby plan — free forever 100GB bandwidth/mo, 100 deploys/day No team features, no password protection
Railway $5 credit/month ~500 runtime hours, 100GB egress Sleeps after 30 days inactivity on free
Supabase 2 free projects 500MB DB, 1GB storage, 50MB file uploads Pauses after 1 week inactivity
GitHub Actions 2000 minutes/month (public repos: unlimited) Public repos: free. Private: 2000 min macOS runners cost 10× more minutes
Cloudflare Free DNS, CDN, DDoS protection Workers: 100k requests/day Workers free tier has 10ms CPU limit
AWS Free Tier 12 months + always-free services EC2: 750hrs t2.micro, S3: 5GB, Lambda: 1M requests Easy to exceed accidentally — set billing alerts!
🚨 SET AWS BILLING ALERTS — NON-NEGOTIABLE

AWS bills you for overages immediately. Developers regularly get $300–$3000 surprise bills from accidentally leaving a service running, misconfigured S3 buckets, or not cleaning up after tutorials. Always go to AWS Billing → Budgets → Create Budget → set an alert at $1 and $10. This takes 2 minutes and can save you from disaster.

⚠ Common Free Tier Mistakes
Forgetting Supabase project auto-pauses

Supabase free projects pause after 1 week of inactivity. When a user tries to log in, it takes 30+ seconds to wake up.

Set a simple keep-alive cron job

A free service like cron-job.org can ping your Supabase API every 3 days to keep it active.

Public S3 bucket exposing private data

Misconfigured S3 buckets are one of the biggest data breach vectors. S3 is private by default — don't change that unless you know exactly why.

Use pre-signed URLs for file access

Generate temporary (15-minute) pre-signed URLs server-side. The bucket stays private, users get time-limited access to specific files.

8.12

Cost & Scaling Strategy

Understanding when to pay for what is a real skill. Here's how to think about scaling costs at each stage of a product's life:

StageUsersRecommended StackMonthly Cost
🧪 Prototype 0–10 (devs only) Vercel free + Railway free + Supabase free $0
🚀 Early Launch 10–500 Vercel free + Railway Starter ($5) + Supabase free ~$5
📈 Growth 500–5,000 Vercel Pro ($20) + Railway Developer ($20) + Supabase Pro ($25) ~$65
🏢 Scale 5,000–50,000 Vercel Pro + Railway Pro + RDS PostgreSQL + Redis ~$200–500
🌍 Enterprise 50,000+ AWS ECS/Fargate + RDS Multi-AZ + CloudFront + Redis Cluster $1,000+
✓ THE GOLDEN SCALING RULE

Don't prematurely optimise for scale. Start with the simplest, cheapest stack and only upgrade when you hit actual limits. A $5/month Railway instance can handle thousands of requests per minute. Your bottleneck is almost always the database, not compute — optimise queries before upgrading servers.

Interview: "How would you scale a Node.js app from 100 to 100,000 users?"

  • 1. DB first: Add indexes, optimise N+1 queries, enable connection pooling (PgBouncer)
  • 2. Caching: Redis for frequently-read data (user sessions, config, leaderboards)
  • 3. Horizontal scaling: Run multiple Node.js instances behind a load balancer
  • 4. CDN: Serve all static assets (images, JS, CSS) from CDN, not your server
  • 5. Background jobs: Move heavy computation (AI, email, reports) to worker queues
  • 6. Read replicas: Separate read and write DB traffic for high-read workloads

Key interview point: Always mention you'd profile and identify the actual bottleneck first — don't guess.

8.13

Platform Comparison — Decision Guide

Use this as your decision framework when choosing a platform for any project:

ScenarioBest ChoiceWhy
React/Next.js frontendVercelBuilt for it, zero config, preview deploys
Node.js/Express backendRailwayAlways-on, DB included, simple pricing
Fast MVP with auth + DBSupabase + VercelFull backend without writing server code
Mobile app backendFirebase or SupabaseRealtime, offline sync, auth built-in
Static website / portfolioVercel or Cloudflare PagesFree, fast, custom domain, SSL
Containerised app (Docker)Fly.io or RailwayNative Docker support, global deployment
Enterprise / compliance neededAWS or GCPHIPAA, SOC2, fine-grained control
Side project with zero budgetVercel + Supabase free tierBoth have free tiers, full-stack capability
🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A — CLOUD PLATFORMS
What is a CDN and why does it matter?
A CDN (Content Delivery Network) is a distributed network of servers that caches your static files closer to users. Without a CDN, a user in Tokyo hitting your US-based server waits for the round trip (~200ms). With a CDN, files are served from a server in Tokyo (~5ms). This matters for: initial page load time (Core Web Vitals), bandwidth costs (CDN bandwidth is cheaper than origin server bandwidth), and reliability (if your origin goes down, CDN cache still serves files). Vercel and Cloudflare both provide CDN automatically.
What does "self-hosting" mean and when should you do it?
Self-hosting means running your infrastructure on servers you control (your own VPS or bare metal), rather than paying a managed platform. When it makes sense: extreme cost sensitivity at high scale, data sovereignty requirements (EU regulations), or specific hardware needs (GPUs). When it doesn't: early stage startups (the time cost of sysadmin > the money saved), teams without DevOps expertise, projects needing rapid iteration. Most startups should default to PaaS until the platform bill exceeds the cost of hiring a DevOps engineer.
Explain horizontal vs vertical scaling
Vertical scaling (scaling up) = make your one server bigger (more CPU, more RAM). Simple but has limits — you can only get so big, and there's downtime during upgrades. Horizontal scaling (scaling out) = add more servers behind a load balancer. Theoretically infinite, but requires your app to be stateless (sessions in Redis, not in memory). Most modern cloud apps should be designed for horizontal scaling from the start.
⚠ Common Cloud Mistakes to Avoid
No backup strategy for the database

Railway, Supabase, and RDS all offer automated backups — but you need to turn them on and test restoring them. "I have backups" and "I can restore from backups" are very different things.

Enable automated backups + test restore quarterly

Supabase Pro has automatic daily backups with point-in-time recovery. Railway has manual backup via pg_dump. Set a calendar reminder to actually test your restore procedure.

Deploying directly from a local machine

Running git push heroku main or vercel --prod from your laptop bypasses CI/CD and means untested code goes to production.

Only production deploys trigger from CI/CD pipelines

Your laptop should only deploy to staging/preview environments. Production deploys happen automatically after CI passes on the main branch.

PART 09 — TECHNOLOGY LAYERS

The Internet's
Hidden Machinery

OSI model, TCP/IP, DNS internals, TLS handshakes, CDNs, Nginx, load balancers, caching strategies, message queues, WebSockets — the infrastructure every senior dev is expected to know cold.

OSI Model TCP/UDP DNS CDN TLS/HTTPS Nginx WebSockets Caching
9.1

The OSI Model — Simplified

📖 DEFINITION

The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a conceptual framework that describes how data travels from one computer to another over a network, broken into 7 distinct layers. Each layer has a specific job and talks only to the layers directly above and below it.

📬 ANALOGY

Think of sending a physical letter internationally. You write the letter (Application), put it in an envelope (Presentation), label it as urgent or standard (Session), hand it to a courier (Transport), who routes it via the correct postal hub (Network), which loads it on the correct truck (Data Link), which drives on physical roads (Physical). Each "layer" does one job and passes work down or up.

OSI Model — All 7 Layers with FitoGlobe Context
7
Application
HTTP, HTTPS, WebSocket, DNS, SMTP
FitoGlobe REST API calls, login requests, workout saves
6
Presentation
SSL/TLS encryption, JSON encoding, compression
HTTPS encryption of your JWT tokens in transit
5
Session
Connection management, auth sessions
Keeping your WebSocket connection alive during a workout
4
Transport
TCP / UDP — ports, reliability, ordering
TCP ensures every API packet arrives in order, no drops
3
Network
IP addressing, routing between networks
IP routes your request from Mumbai → Railway server in US-East
2
Data Link
MAC addresses, frames, WiFi/Ethernet
Your WiFi router's job — move frames hop-by-hop locally
1
Physical
Cables, radio waves, electrical signals, fiber optics
Actual bits moving as light pulses through undersea fiber cables
ℹ️ THE PRACTICAL SHORTCUT

As a full-stack developer you operate almost entirely at Layer 7 (Application). You use HTTP, HTTPS, WebSockets. Understanding Layers 3–4 (IP + TCP) helps you debug network issues. Layers 1–2 are the domain of network engineers. But you need to know all 7 layers exist and their rough responsibilities — interviewers love this question.

ℹ️ TCP/IP MODEL (THE REAL-WORLD VERSION)

In practice, engineers use the TCP/IP model — a simplified 4-layer version: Network Access (L1+L2), Internet (L3), Transport (L4), Application (L5–L7). It maps directly to how the internet is built. OSI is the theoretical reference; TCP/IP is what actually runs under your code.

Interview: "Explain the OSI model. Where does HTTP live?"

The OSI model has 7 layers — Physical (1) through Application (7). HTTP lives at Layer 7 (Application). The key insight interviewers want: each layer adds a header (encapsulation) going down, and strips it going up. A web request wraps the HTTP data in TCP (L4), then IP (L3), then Ethernet frames (L2), then bits (L1). The receiving end reverses this. Practical developer focus: L7 (what you build), L4 (TCP reliability, ports), L3 (IP routing). Mention that HTTP/3 moves from TCP to QUIC (UDP-based) for speed — that shows you're up to date.

9.2

TCP vs UDP

Both TCP and UDP are Layer 4 transport protocols — they define how data is moved between two machines. The choice between them is one of the most fundamental tradeoffs in networking.

PropertyTCPUDP
Full NameTransmission Control ProtocolUser Datagram Protocol
ConnectionConnection-oriented (3-way handshake)Connectionless — fire and forget
ReliabilityGuaranteed delivery, ACKs for every packetNo guarantee — packets can be lost
OrderingPackets arrive in order, re-ordered if neededNo ordering — packets can arrive out of order
SpeedSlower — overhead of reliability checksFaster — minimal overhead
Use WhenData integrity is criticalSpeed matters more than perfection
ExamplesHTTP/HTTPS, SSH, database connections, emailLive video, online gaming, DNS, VoIP, QUIC

The TCP 3-Way Handshake

Before any HTTP request is made, TCP establishes a connection. This is why HTTPS connections have latency before the first byte — the handshake must complete first.

TCP 3-Way Handshake — Before Every HTTP Request
CLIENT (Browser)
SERVER (Railway)
SYN
"I want to connect.
My seq# is 100"
ACK
"Got it. Ready!"
──────→
←──────
──────→
~1–2 RTT
before data
SYN-ACK
"OK! My seq# is 200.
Your next# is 101"
Connection established! 🎉
HTTP can now flow
🏋️ FITOGLOBE CONTEXT

Every API call from FitoGlobe's React frontend to the Railway backend uses TCP under HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2. HTTP/2 introduced multiplexing — multiple requests can share one TCP connection simultaneously, avoiding the overhead of a new handshake per request. This is a major reason Axios with HTTP/2 is faster than older HTTP/1.1 connections. WebSocket connections (for real-time workout tracking) also use TCP — they start as an HTTP upgrade then maintain a persistent TCP connection.

Interview: "Why is HTTP/3 switching from TCP to UDP?"

HTTP/3 uses QUIC, which runs over UDP. The reason: TCP's "head-of-line blocking" problem. In TCP, if one packet is lost, all subsequent packets wait for the retransmit — even if they're unrelated. QUIC on UDP handles each stream independently, so a lost packet for one resource doesn't block others. Result: HTTP/3 is ~30% faster on lossy connections (mobile networks). QUIC also builds TLS 1.3 into the handshake, reducing round trips from 3 to 1. Mention: Cloudflare and Google already support HTTP/3.

9.3

DNS Deep Dive

📖 DEFINITION

DNS (Domain Name System) is the internet's phone book. When you type fitoglobe.com, your computer queries a hierarchy of DNS servers to find the IP address of the server hosting that domain. The full lookup — from browser to IP — is called a DNS resolution.

The Full DNS Resolution — 8 Steps

  • 1
    Browser Cache CheckBrowser checks if it recently resolved fitoglobe.com. If yes (and TTL not expired) → use cached IP. Typical TTL: 300–86400 seconds.
  • 2
    OS Cache / hosts fileIf not in browser cache, OS checks its own DNS cache and the /etc/hosts file. Developers use this to override DNS locally (127.0.0.1 fitoglobe.test).
  • 3
    Recursive ResolverOS queries a Recursive Resolver (usually your ISP's or 8.8.8.8 for Google, 1.1.1.1 for Cloudflare). This resolver does the heavy lifting on your behalf.
  • 4
    Root NameserversResolver queries a Root Nameserver (there are 13 root server IPs worldwide). Root says: "I don't know fitoglobe.com specifically, but for .com domains, ask the TLD nameserver."
  • 5
    TLD NameserversResolver asks the .com TLD nameserver. It says: "For fitoglobe.com, the authoritative nameserver is ns1.cloudflare.com."
  • 6
    Authoritative NameserverResolver queries Cloudflare's nameserver. This one knows the exact answer: "The A record for fitoglobe.com is 76.76.21.21."
  • 7
    Response CachedResolver caches the IP for the TTL duration and returns it to your browser. All future queries within TTL skip steps 4–6.
  • 8
    TCP Connection to IPBrowser now has the IP. It starts the TCP handshake → TLS handshake → HTTP request. The DNS part is complete.
⏱️ HOW LONG DOES DNS TAKE?

First resolution (cache cold): 20–120ms. Subsequent (cached): <1ms. This is why DNS TTL matters — a low TTL (60s) means every minute your users pay the lookup cost. A high TTL (86400s = 1 day) means faster loads but slow propagation when you change servers. For production: TTL of 300–3600 seconds is the standard balance.

Debugging DNS — Commands You Must Know

DNS debugging tools
BASH
1# Check what IP a domain resolves to
2dig fitoglobe.com
3nslookup fitoglobe.com
4
5# Check a specific DNS record type
6dig fitoglobe.com A # IPv4 address
7dig fitoglobe.com MX # mail servers
8dig fitoglobe.com TXT # TXT records (SPF, DKIM, verify)
9
10# Query a specific DNS server (bypass your ISP's resolver)
11dig @8.8.8.8 fitoglobe.com # ask Google's resolver
12dig @1.1.1.1 fitoglobe.com # ask Cloudflare's resolver
13
14# See the full resolution chain (trace)
15dig +trace fitoglobe.com # shows root → TLD → auth
16
17# Flush DNS cache locally (Windows)
18ipconfig /flushdns
19# macOS
20sudo dscacheutil -flushcache
⚠ Common DNS Mistakes
Setting TTL too low in production

A TTL of 60 seconds means every user making a DNS lookup triggers an authoritative nameserver query every minute. At scale, this adds unnecessary latency and hammers your DNS provider.

Use TTL 300–3600s in production, drop to 60s before planned server migrations

Drop TTL a few hours before switching servers so the change propagates fast, then raise it back afterward.

Expecting instant DNS propagation

After changing a DNS record, developers expect all users to see the new IP immediately. DNS TTL means old records are cached globally for up to 48 hours.

Plan ahead — reduce TTL days before a migration

Use dig @8.8.8.8 and dig @1.1.1.1 to verify what different resolvers around the world are seeing.

9.4

CDN Mechanics

📖 DEFINITION

A CDN (Content Delivery Network) is a globally distributed network of servers (called PoPs — Points of Presence) that cache copies of your static assets close to your users. When a user in Tokyo requests fitoglobe.com/assets/main.js, it comes from a Tokyo edge server, not your US origin server.

CDN Request Flow — Cache Hit vs Cache Miss
✓ CACHE HIT (fast path)
User in Tokyo requests main.js
↓
Tokyo CDN PoP has cached copy
↓
Response in ~5ms ⚡
Origin server never involved
✗ CACHE MISS (first request)
User in Tokyo requests main.js
↓
Tokyo CDN has no cache
↓
Fetches from US origin (~200ms)
↓
Caches + responds. Future = 5ms

Cache-Control Headers — How CDNs Know What to Cache

Express — setting cache headers
JS
1// Static assets (Vite-hashed filenames like main.a3f2b1.js)
2// Hash changes on every build → stale cache never served
3res.setHeader('Cache-Control',
4 'public, max-age=31536000, immutable'
5); // cache 1 year — filename guarantees freshness
6
7// index.html — MUST NOT be cached or stale HTML loads old JS
8res.setHeader('Cache-Control',
9 'no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate'
10); // always fetch fresh index.html from origin
11
12// API responses — cache for 60s on CDN, stale-while-revalidate
13res.setHeader('Cache-Control',
14 'public, max-age=60, stale-while-revalidate=300'
15); // serve stale for 5 min while fetching fresh in background

Interview: "What's the difference between max-age and s-maxage?"

max-age controls caching for both browsers and CDNs. s-maxage controls caching for CDNs (shared caches) only, overriding max-age for CDNs while the browser uses its own max-age. This lets you say: "CDN, cache this for 1 hour (s-maxage=3600), but browser, always revalidate (max-age=0)." Very useful for API responses where you want CDN-level caching but always-fresh data in the user's browser.

9.5

SSL/TLS & HTTPS

📖 DEFINITION

TLS (Transport Layer Security) is the encryption protocol that secures data in transit between client and server. HTTPS = HTTP over TLS. When you see the padlock in your browser, TLS is running. SSL is the old, deprecated predecessor — when people say "SSL certificate" they almost always mean TLS.

The TLS 1.3 Handshake — Simplified

TLS 1.3 Handshake — Establishing an Encrypted Channel
CLIENT
SERVER
ClientHello
TLS version, cipher suites, random nonce, key share
Finished
Encrypted. TLS ready.
──────→ ←────── ──────→
1 RTT
TLS 1.3
ServerHello + Certificate + Finished
Chosen cipher, certificate, key share, encrypted data starts
TLS 1.3 win: Only 1 round trip (vs 2 for TLS 1.2). TLS 1.3 also supports 0-RTT for repeat visitors — data is sent with the first packet, no handshake at all.

What Goes Inside a TLS Certificate?

FieldWhat It ContainsWhy It Matters
Subject / CNfitoglobe.com (or *.fitoglobe.com for wildcard)Proves the cert is for this domain
Public KeyRSA or ECDSA public keyUsed to establish encrypted session key
IssuerLet's Encrypt, DigiCert, Cloudflare, etc.Trusted Certificate Authority signed this
Validity PeriodNot Before / Not After datesLet's Encrypt: 90 days. Auto-renewed by Vercel/Railway.
SANSubject Alternative NamesList of all domains the cert covers
SignatureCA's cryptographic signatureProves the CA verified you own the domain
✓ FREE TLS WITH VERCEL, RAILWAY, CLOUDFLARE

All three platforms auto-provision Let's Encrypt certificates and handle renewal automatically. You never need to manually manage TLS certs for standard deployments. The only case to manage certs yourself: wildcard domains (*.fitoglobe.com), multi-domain certs, or custom CA requirements for enterprise clients.

⚠ Common TLS Mistakes
Mixed content — HTTPS page loading HTTP resources

If your HTTPS site loads any resource (image, script, API call) over plain HTTP, browsers block it with a Mixed Content error. Even one HTTP call breaks the padlock.

Always use HTTPS for all resource URLs, API calls, and CDN assets

In Express, force HTTPS with a redirect middleware. Set VITE_API_URL=https://api.fitoglobe.com — never http://.

Not enforcing HTTPS redirects on the backend

If your Express server accepts both HTTP and HTTPS, users who type http://fitoglobe.com get an unencrypted session. JWT tokens travel in plaintext.

Redirect all HTTP to HTTPS with a 301 permanent redirect

if (req.headers['x-forwarded-proto'] !== 'https') return res.redirect(301, 'https://' + req.headers.host + req.url)

9.6

Reverse Proxies & Nginx

📖 DEFINITION

A reverse proxy sits in front of your backend servers and intercepts all incoming requests. It then forwards requests to the appropriate server. Clients only ever talk to the proxy — they never know your actual server's IP. Nginx is the world's most popular reverse proxy, also functioning as a web server, load balancer, and TLS terminator.

🏨 ANALOGY

A reverse proxy is like a hotel receptionist. Guests (clients) interact only with reception — they don't know which floor or room handles their request. The receptionist routes "Room service?" to the kitchen, "Housekeeping?" to housekeeping, "Checkout?" to billing. Each department (server) is isolated. The guest never needs a direct line.

What a Reverse Proxy Does

Nginx as Reverse Proxy — Multiple Services, One Entry Point
Internet Traffic → fitoglobe.com:443
↓
Nginx Reverse Proxy
TLS termination · Rate limiting · Compression · Logging
↓
/ → React Static Files
:5173
↓
/api → Node.js Backend
:5000
↓
/ws → WebSocket Server
:5001

Nginx Config — FitoGlobe Production Setup

/etc/nginx/sites-available/fitoglobe.conf
NGINX
1# Redirect HTTP → HTTPS
2server {
3 listen 80;
4 server_name fitoglobe.com www.fitoglobe.com;
5 return 301 https://$host$request_uri; # permanent redirect
6}
7
8server {
9 listen 443 ssl http2; # HTTPS + HTTP/2
10 server_name fitoglobe.com;
11 ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/fitoglobe.com/fullchain.pem;
12 ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/fitoglobe.com/privkey.pem;
13
14 # Serve React SPA from dist/
15 location / {
16 root /var/www/fitoglobe/dist;
17 try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html; # SPA fallback
18 }
19
20 # Proxy /api to Node.js backend
21 location /api/ {
22 proxy_pass http://localhost:5000;
23 proxy_set_header Host $host;
24 proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr; # pass real IP
25 proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto https; # tell Express it's HTTPS
26 }
27
28 # Proxy WebSocket connections
29 location /ws/ {
30 proxy_pass http://localhost:5001;
31 proxy_http_version 1.1;
32 proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade; # required for WS
33 proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade"; # required for WS
34 }
35 gzip on; # compress responses
36 gzip_types text/css application/javascript application/json;
37}

Interview: "What's the difference between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?"

A forward proxy sits in front of clients — it forwards outbound requests on behalf of users (e.g., VPNs, corporate proxies that filter which sites employees can visit). The server doesn't know who the real client is. A reverse proxy sits in front of servers — it receives inbound requests and forwards them to the appropriate backend. The client doesn't know which server ultimately responded. Key reverse proxy benefits: TLS termination, load balancing, rate limiting, caching, hiding internal architecture, and centralised logging.

9.7

Load Balancing

📖 DEFINITION

A load balancer distributes incoming network traffic across multiple backend servers so no single server gets overwhelmed. It enables horizontal scaling — handling more traffic by adding more servers, not bigger servers. Load balancers also provide automatic failover: if one server dies, traffic reroutes to healthy servers instantly.

Load Balancing Algorithms

AlgorithmHow It WorksBest ForWeakness
Round Robin Requests distributed sequentially: S1, S2, S3, S1, S2… Identical servers, stateless APIs Ignores server load differences
Weighted Round Robin Stronger servers get more requests proportionally Heterogeneous server hardware Requires manual weight tuning
Least Connections Route to server with fewest active connections Long-lived connections, WebSockets More complex to implement
IP Hash Same client IP always routes to same server Session stickiness needed Uneven distribution if few large clients
Random Random server selection each time Simple stateless services Can cause temporary hot spots
⚠️ STATELESS APPS ARE REQUIRED FOR LOAD BALANCING

If your Node.js server stores user sessions in memory (req.session using in-memory store), load balancing breaks — request 1 hits Server A (session created), request 2 hits Server B (no session found → logged out). Solution: store sessions externally in Redis so every server can access them. This is a classic interview question about horizontal scaling.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A — LOAD BALANCING
How would you make a Node.js app load-balancer-ready?
1. Make it stateless — move sessions to Redis, not in-memory. 2. Use process.env.PORT so multiple instances can run on different ports. 3. Health check endpoint — GET /health returns 200 so the load balancer can detect live vs dead instances. 4. Graceful shutdown — handle SIGTERM signal to finish in-flight requests before closing. 5. Idempotent operations — ensure retried requests (from LB failover) don't duplicate data (add unique request IDs or database unique constraints).
9.8

Caching Layers — The Complete Map

📖 DEFINITION

Caching is storing the result of an expensive operation so future requests return it instantly. There are multiple caching layers in a full-stack app — each with different scope, speed, and purpose. Understanding which layer to use for which data is a key senior dev skill.

🌐

Layer 1 — Browser Cache < 1ms

Browser stores JS, CSS, images locally on disk. Controlled by Cache-Control headers. Fastest possible — no network request at all. FitoGlobe: Vite-hashed assets cached 1 year, index.html never cached. Risk: stale JS if you don't invalidate properly (Vite's content hashing solves this).

🌍

Layer 2 — CDN Cache 5–50ms

CDN edge servers cache responses globally. Serves users from the nearest PoP. Controlled by Cache-Control: s-maxage. FitoGlobe: All static assets served via Vercel's CDN. API responses can also be CDN-cached for public, non-user-specific data (e.g., exercise library, workout templates). Risk: cached stale API responses serving wrong data to users.

⚡

Layer 3 — Application Cache / Redis 0.1–1ms

Your Node.js backend caches expensive DB query results in Redis (in-memory key-value store). FitoGlobe: Cache the full exercise library (GET /exercises) — it never changes, so hit DB once, cache in Redis for 24h. Cache leaderboards for 60s — a bit stale is acceptable. Risk: serving stale data after DB writes. Use cache invalidation on write (delete the key when data changes).

🗄️

Layer 4 — Database Query Cache 1–50ms

PostgreSQL has an internal buffer cache (shared_buffers) — frequently accessed data pages stay in RAM. Well-indexed queries hitting hot data return in <1ms. FitoGlobe: Indexing userId on the Workout table means user's recent workouts are always served from PostgreSQL's buffer, not disk. Risk: cold cache after DB restart; large queries evicting hot data.
