vitest.config.js + package.json — full test setup
JS + JSON
1// frontend/vitest.config.js
2import { defineConfig } from 'vitest/config';
3import react from '@vitejs/plugin-react';
4
5export default defineConfig({
6 plugins: [react()],
7 test: {
8 environment: 'jsdom', // simulates a browser DOM
9 globals: true, // describe/it/expect without imports
10 setupFiles: ['./src/test/setup.js'], // runs before each test file
11 coverage: {
12 provider: 'v8', // use V8's built-in coverage
13 reporter: ['text', 'html', 'lcov'], // terminal + html report + CI
14 thresholds: { lines: 70 }, // fail CI if coverage drops below 70%
15 exclude: ['**/*.config.js', 'src/test/**']
16 }
17 }
18});
19
20// src/test/setup.js — runs before every test file
21import '@testing-library/jest-dom'; // adds expect(el).toBeInTheDocument()
22import { afterEach } from 'vitest';
23import { cleanup } from '@testing-library/react';
24afterEach(cleanup); // clean up DOM between tests
25
26// package.json scripts
27{
28 "test": "vitest run", // CI: run once, exit
29 "test:watch": "vitest", // dev: watch mode, re-run on save
30 "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage" // generate coverage report
31}
12.4

Unit Tests — Deep Dive

📖 DEFINITION

A unit test tests one function or class in complete isolation. All dependencies (database, network, other services) are replaced with mocks or fakes. A unit test should never make network calls, never touch the filesystem, never start a server. Result: extremely fast (milliseconds), extremely reliable (no external state).

Anatomy of a Test — AAA Pattern

src/utils/__tests__/calories.test.js — real unit tests
JS
1import { describe, it, expect } from 'vitest';
2import { calculateCalories, calculateBMR } from '../calories';
3
4describe('calculateCalories', () => {
5 it('returns correct calories for push-ups', () => {
6 // ARRANGE — set up inputs
7 const exercise = { name: 'push-ups', sets: 3, reps: 15, weightKg: 0 };
8 const userWeightKg = 75;
9
10 // ACT — call the function under test
11 const result = calculateCalories(exercise, userWeightKg);
12
13 // ASSERT — verify the output
14 expect(result).toBe(27); // exact value
15 expect(result).toBeGreaterThan(0);
16 expect(result).toBeTypeOf('number');
17 });
18
19 it('returns 0 for zero sets', () => {
20 expect(calculateCalories({ sets: 0, reps: 10 }, 75)).toBe(0);
21 });
22
23 it('throws on negative reps', () => {
24 expect(() =>
25 calculateCalories({ sets: 3, reps: -5 }, 75)
26 ).toThrow('Reps must be positive');
27 });
28});
29
30describe('calculateBMR', () => {
31 // Test multiple inputs with it.each — DRY for table-driven tests
32 it.each([
33 ['male', 75, 175, 28, 1822], // gender, weight, height, age, expected
34 ['female', 60, 165, 25, 1432],
35 ])('%s, %dkg, %dcm, %dyo = %d kcal',
36 (gender, weight, height, age, expected) => {
37 expect(calculateBMR({gender,weight,height,age})).toBe(expected);
38 }
39 );
40});

Essential Vitest/Jest Matchers — Cheat Sheet

MatcherUse WhenExample
.toBe()Primitives — exact equality (===)expect(2+2).toBe(4)
.toEqual()Objects/arrays — deep equalityexpect(user).toEqual({name:'Yash',age:25})
.toBeNull() / .toBeDefined()Checking presenceexpect(result).not.toBeNull()
.toContain()Array includes item / string includes substringexpect(names).toContain('push-ups')
.toThrow()Function throws expected errorexpect(() => fn(-1)).toThrow('positive')
.toHaveBeenCalledWith()Mock was called with specific argsexpect(mockFn).toHaveBeenCalledWith(42)
.resolves / .rejectsAsync functionsawait expect(promise).resolves.toBe('ok')
.toMatchSnapshot()Component renders consistently over timeexpect(rendered).toMatchSnapshot()
.toBeCloseTo()Floating point comparisonsexpect(0.1+0.2).toBeCloseTo(0.3)
12.5

Mocking & Spies

📖 DEFINITION

A mock replaces a real dependency with a fake version you control — so your unit test doesn't hit a real database, send real emails, or call real APIs. A spy wraps a real function to record how it was called without replacing it. Both let you test code in isolation and verify interactions.

The Three Types of Test Doubles

TypeWhat It IsWhen to Use
MockFake replacement that records calls and returns preset valuesReplace DB, email service, AI API — anything external
SpyWraps real function, records calls, can optionally override returnVerify a real function was called with correct args
StubSimple fake that always returns the same valueReplace simple dependencies where call verification isn't needed
Mocking Prisma, email service, and AI — FitoGlobe examples
JS
1import { vi, describe, it, expect, beforeEach } from 'vitest';
2
3// ── Mock an entire module ─────────────────────────────────
4vi.mock('../lib/prisma', () => ({
5 default: {
6 workout: {
7 create: vi.fn(), // fake create — records calls, returns undefined by default
8 findMany: vi.fn(),
9 },
10 user: { findUnique: vi.fn(), create: vi.fn() }
11 }
12}));
13
14import prisma from '../lib/prisma'; // gets the mocked version
15import { logWorkout } from '../controllers/workout.controller';
16
17describe('logWorkout controller', () => {
18 beforeEach(() => {
19 vi.clearAllMocks(); // reset mock call counts between tests
20 });
21
22 it('calls prisma.workout.create with correct data', async () => {
23 // Make the mock return a fake workout object
24 prisma.workout.create.mockResolvedValue({
25 id: 'w1', userId: 'u1', duration: 45
26 });
27
28 const req = { user: { id: 'u1' }, body: { duration: 45, exercises: [] } };
29 const res = { status: vi.fn().mockReturnThis(), json: vi.fn() };
30
31 await logWorkout(req, res);
32
33 expect(prisma.workout.create).toHaveBeenCalledOnce();
34 expect(prisma.workout.create).toHaveBeenCalledWith({
35 data: expect.objectContaining({ userId: 'u1', duration: 45 })
36 });
37 expect(res.status).toHaveBeenCalledWith(201);
38 });
39});
⚠ Common Mocking Mistakes
Mocking too much — testing the mock, not the code

If a unit test mocks every single dependency and just asserts that mocks were called, you're testing mock configuration, not real logic. The test passes even when business logic is completely wrong.

Mock only external I/O — database, network, filesystem, time

Pure logic functions (calculations, transformations, validators) should be tested with real inputs and real outputs — no mocking needed at all.

Not resetting mocks between tests

A mock called in test 1 retains its call count in test 2. Test 2 asserts toHaveBeenCalledOnce() but the mock has been called twice (from test 1). Tests become order-dependent.

Always call vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach

Or set clearMocks: true globally in vitest.config.js to clear automatically between every test.

12.6

React Testing Library

📖 DEFINITION

React Testing Library (RTL) tests React components from the user's perspective — by querying the DOM the way a user finds things (by visible text, label, role) rather than implementation details (component names, state variables). The guiding principle: "The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you."

⚠️ RTL PHILOSOPHY — WHAT NOT TO TEST

Don't test: component state directly, which props a child received, whether a specific CSS class is applied, or internal function calls. Do test: what the user sees, what happens when the user clicks/types, what is displayed after an async operation. If a test breaks during an internal refactor that doesn't change user-visible behaviour, the test was testing implementation details, not behaviour.

WorkoutCard.test.jsx — component behaviour tests
JSX
1import { render, screen, fireEvent, waitFor } from '@testing-library/react';
2import { userEvent } from '@testing-library/user-event'; // prefer over fireEvent
3import WorkoutCard from './WorkoutCard';
4
5const mockWorkout = {
6 id: 'w1', name: 'Push Day', duration: 45,
7 calories: 320, exercises: ['Bench Press', 'Push-ups']
8};
9
10describe('WorkoutCard', () => {
11 it('renders workout name and duration', () => {
12 render(<WorkoutCard workout={mockWorkout} />);
13
14 // Query by text — how a user sees it
15 expect(screen.getByText('Push Day')).toBeInTheDocument();
16 expect(screen.getByText('45 min')).toBeInTheDocument();
17 expect(screen.getByText('320 kcal')).toBeInTheDocument();
18 });
19
20 it('calls onDelete when delete button is clicked', async () => {
21 const onDelete = vi.fn();
22 render(<WorkoutCard workout={mockWorkout} onDelete={onDelete} />);
23
24 // userEvent simulates real user interaction (focus, mousedown, click, mouseup)
25 await userEvent.click(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /delete/i }));
26
27 expect(onDelete).toHaveBeenCalledWith('w1');
28 });
29
30 it('shows loading skeleton while fetching', async () => {
31 render(<WorkoutCard workout={null} loading={true} />);
32 expect(screen.getByTestId('skeleton')).toBeInTheDocument();
33 expect(screen.queryByText('Push Day')).not.toBeInTheDocument();
34 });
35});

RTL Query Priority — Always Use in This Order

PriorityQueryWhy
1st (best)getByRole('button', {name:/submit/i})Mimics how screen readers find elements. Tests accessibility too.
2ndgetByLabelText('Email')Form fields — tests label is properly associated.
3rdgetByPlaceholderText('Enter email')When no label exists.
4thgetByText('Submit')Finds by visible text — good for buttons, headings.
5thgetByDisplayValue('user@test.com')Current value of input/select.
Last resortgetByTestId('submit-btn')Only when no semantic query works. Requires adding data-testid.
12.7

Integration Tests

📖 DEFINITION

Integration tests verify that multiple units work together correctly. Unlike unit tests that mock everything, integration tests use real implementations of the modules being integrated — but may still mock external services. For backend, the most valuable integration test is: hit a real route → run through real middleware → save to a real test database → assert the response.

🏋️ WHAT INTEGRATION TESTS CATCH THAT UNIT TESTS MISS

A unit test for logWorkout controller can pass perfectly — but the integration test fails because the JWT middleware was misconfigured and strips req.user before it reaches the controller. Each unit works fine in isolation; they fail together. Integration tests are the only way to catch this entire category of bugs.

Integration Test Setup — Isolated Test Database

backend/src/test/helpers.js — test DB setup
JS
1import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
2import { beforeAll, afterAll, beforeEach } from 'vitest';
3
4// Use a separate test database — never the dev/prod database
5// .env.test: DATABASE_URL="postgresql://...@localhost:5432/fitoglobe_test"
6export const prismaTest = new PrismaClient({
7 datasources: { db: { url: process.env.TEST_DATABASE_URL } }
8});
9
10export function setupTestDB() {
11 beforeAll(async () => {
12 await prismaTest.$connect();
13 });
14 afterAll(async () => {
15 await prismaTest.$disconnect();
16 });
17 beforeEach(async () => {
18 // Wipe tables before each test — ensures isolation
19 await prismaTest.$executeRaw(`TRUNCATE TABLE workouts, users CASCADE`);
20 });
21}
🚨 NEVER RUN INTEGRATION TESTS AGAINST YOUR DEV DATABASE

Every test run truncates tables. Running against your dev database means losing all your development data every time you run tests. Always use a separate fitoglobe_test database with its own TEST_DATABASE_URL environment variable. In CI, spin up a PostgreSQL service container (see Part 11 GitHub Actions config).

12.8

API Testing with Supertest

📖 DEFINITION

Supertest is a library that lets you make HTTP requests to your Express app in tests — without starting a real server. It binds directly to the Express app instance. Combined with a real test database, this gives you complete route-to-database integration tests with no network overhead.

workouts.integration.test.js — full route tests
JS
1import request from 'supertest';
2import { describe, it, expect } from 'vitest';
3import app from '../app'; // Express app (not app.listen())
4import { setupTestDB, createTestUser, getAuthToken } from './helpers';
5
6setupTestDB(); // connect, disconnect, truncate between tests
7
8describe('POST /api/workouts', () => {
9 it('creates a workout and returns 201', async () => {
10 const user = await createTestUser();
11 const token = getAuthToken(user.id); // generates real JWT
12
13 const res = await request(app)
14 .post('/api/workouts')
15 .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`)
16 .send({ name: 'Push Day', duration: 45, exercises: [] });
17
18 expect(res.status).toBe(201);
19 expect(res.body.workout.name).toBe('Push Day');
20 expect(res.body.workout.userId).toBe(user.id); // ownership enforced
21 expect(res.body.workout.id).toBeDefined(); // DB assigned id
22 });
23
24 it('returns 401 without auth token', async () => {
25 const res = await request(app)
26 .post('/api/workouts')
27 .send({ name: 'Push Day' }); // no auth header
28
29 expect(res.status).toBe(401);
30 expect(res.body.error).toMatch(/unauthorized/i);
31 });
32
33 it('returns 400 with missing required fields', async () => {
34 const token = getAuthToken('user1');
35 const res = await request(app)
36 .post('/api/workouts')
37 .set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`)
38 .send({}); // empty body — missing required name/duration
39 expect(res.status).toBe(400);
40 });
41});

Interview: "What's the difference between unit and integration tests? Which do you prefer?"

Unit tests isolate one function — all dependencies mocked, runs in microseconds, tells you exactly which function broke. Integration tests run real code paths together — catches the gaps between units, slower but higher confidence. The honest answer: they serve different roles and you need both. Unit tests for logic-heavy functions (calculators, validators, transformers). Integration tests for routes, middleware chains, and database operations. The biggest mistake teams make is having only unit tests — everything passes but the app still breaks in production because the units don't fit together.

12.9

E2E Testing with Playwright

📖 DEFINITION

Playwright is Microsoft's E2E testing framework. It controls a real browser (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) and simulates real user interactions — clicking, typing, navigating, waiting for network requests. E2E tests verify complete user journeys work from start to finish, exactly as a user would experience them.

Why Playwright Over Cypress

FeaturePlaywrightCypress
Multiple browsersChromium, Firefox, WebKitChromium only (Firefox experimental)
Multiple tabs/windowsFull supportNot supported
SpeedParallel by defaultSerial by default (paid for parallel)
Auto-waitWaits for elements automaticallyAlso auto-waits
Network mockingBuilt-in route interceptionBuilt-in
DX / DebuggingTrace viewer, codegen, UI modeTime-travel debugger
PricingFully open sourceOpen source, paid cloud

Critical E2E Tests — FitoGlobe

e2e/auth.spec.js — signup + login E2E flow
JS
1import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';
2
3test.describe('Authentication flow', () => {
4 test('user can sign up and land on dashboard', async ({ page }) => {
5 await page.goto('http://localhost:5173/signup');
6
7 // Fill the signup form — exactly how a user would
8 await page.getByLabel('Email').fill('yash@test.com');
9 await page.getByLabel('Password').fill('TestPass123!');
10 await page.getByLabel('Full Name').fill('Yash Sharma');
11 await page.getByRole('button', { name: /create account/i }).click();
12
13 // Playwright auto-waits for navigation and elements
14 await expect(page).toHaveURL(/\/dashboard/);
15 await expect(page.getByText('Welcome, Yash')).toBeVisible();
16 });
17
18 test('shows error on invalid credentials', async ({ page }) => {
19 await page.goto('http://localhost:5173/login');
20 await page.getByLabel('Email').fill('nobody@test.com');
21 await page.getByLabel('Password').fill('wrongpassword');
22 await page.getByRole('button', { name: /log in/i }).click();
23 await expect(page.getByRole('alert')).toContainText(/invalid credentials/i);
24 await expect(page).toHaveURL(/\/login/); // stayed on login page
25 });
26});
✓ PLAYWRIGHT CODEGEN — FREE TEST AUTHORING

Run npx playwright codegen http://localhost:5173 and Playwright opens a browser with a recorder. You click through your app normally and it generates the test code automatically. Use it for bootstrapping E2E tests — then refine the generated code. Cuts E2E test writing time by 70% for complex flows.

⚠ Common E2E Mistakes
Flaky tests — using fixed timeouts instead of waiting for conditions

await page.waitForTimeout(3000) — waiting 3 seconds hoping the element appears. On a slow CI machine it still times out. On a fast machine it wastes time.

Use Playwright's auto-waiting locators — no manual timeouts needed

await expect(page.getByText('Dashboard')).toBeVisible() waits up to the configured timeout (default 30s) and retries automatically. Playwright polls until the assertion passes or times out.

Writing too many E2E tests — slow CI, fragile suite

50 E2E tests × 10s each = 8 minutes of CI just for E2E. Every time the UI changes slightly, all 50 break.

E2E tests for critical paths only — 10–15 tests maximum

Signup, login, create first workout, view progress, complete checkout. Everything else is covered by unit and integration tests.

12.10

Code Coverage

📖 DEFINITION

Code coverage measures what percentage of your code was executed during tests. Four metrics: Statements (% of code statements executed), Branches (% of if/else branches taken), Functions (% of functions called), Lines (% of lines executed). Branch coverage is the most meaningful — you can have 100% line coverage but 50% branch coverage if you never test the else path.

FitoGlobe Coverage Goals — Realistic Targets

Auth logic
95%
Controllers
85%
Utils / helpers
90%
React components
70%
Config / boilerplate
30%
⚠️ THE 100% COVERAGE TRAP

100% coverage doesn't mean zero bugs. It means every line was executed — not that the logic is correct. You can have 100% coverage with tests that never assert anything meaningful. Chasing 100% leads to pointless tests of trivial code. The real goal is: high coverage on code that matters (business logic, auth, payments) and lower coverage on boilerplate. Aim for 70–80% overall, 90%+ on critical paths.

Interview: "What is code coverage and what's a good target?"

Coverage measures what percentage of code was executed during tests. The four metrics are statements, branches, functions, and lines — branch coverage is the most meaningful because it tells you if you tested both sides of every decision. A good target depends on the code: aim for 90%+ on critical business logic and auth, 70–80% overall. Key insight: 100% coverage isn't the goal and can be harmful — it leads to testing trivial code and gives false confidence. Two tests that exercise the most important paths in 60 lines of code are worth more than 100 tests that all test the same happy path. Coverage is a tool to find untested code, not a metric to optimise for its own sake.

12.11

TDD — Test-Driven Development

📖 DEFINITION

TDD is a development practice where you write the test before you write the code. The Red-Green-Refactor cycle: write a failing test (Red), write the minimum code to make it pass (Green), then clean up the code (Refactor). The test defines the expected behaviour — then you implement it.

TDD — Red, Green, Refactor Cycle
🔴
RED
Write a failing test that describes the desired behaviour
→
🟢
GREEN
Write the minimum code to make the test pass
→
🔵
REFACTOR
Clean up code while keeping tests green
↩

TDD in Practice — FitoGlobe Calorie Calculator

TDD cycle — write test first, then implement
JS
1// STEP 1 — RED: Write the test first. calculateBMR doesn't exist yet.
2it('calculateBMR: male 75kg 175cm 28yo = 1822 kcal', () => {
3 expect(calculateBMR({gender:'male',weight:75,height:175,age:28})).toBe(1822);
4});
5// ❌ Test fails: "calculateBMR is not defined"
6
7// STEP 2 — GREEN: Write the minimum code to pass the test.
8export function calculateBMR({ gender, weight, height, age }) {
9 // Mifflin-St Jeor Equation
10 const base = 10 * weight + 6.25 * height - 5 * age;
11 return Math.round(gender === 'male' ? base + 5 : base - 161);
12}
13// ✓ Test passes. Now add the female test — same cycle.
14
15// STEP 3 — REFACTOR: Code looks clean. Add edge case tests.
16it('throws on invalid gender', () => {
17 expect(() => calculateBMR({gender:'unknown',...})).toThrow();
18}); // ❌ Red → add validation → ✓ Green → clean up → Refactor
ℹ️ WHEN TO USE TDD

TDD shines for: pure logic functions (calculators, validators, algorithms) where the expected output is crystal clear before coding, bug fixes (write a test that reproduces the bug, then fix it — the test becomes a permanent regression guard), and refactoring (write tests first, refactor freely, tests verify nothing broke). TDD is less natural for: UI components (hard to specify DOM output upfront), exploratory features where you're still figuring out the API, and third-party integrations where you're discovering the API as you go.

12.12

FitoGlobe Complete Test Strategy

Here's the complete, prioritised testing plan for FitoGlobe — what to test, what type of test to use, and why:

FeatureTest TypeToolWhy This Priority
🔐 JWT middleware Unit + Integration Vitest + Supertest CRITICAL — security. Wrong logic = auth bypass
🔐 Password hashing Unit Vitest CRITICAL — plaintext passwords = data breach
🧮 Calorie calculator Unit Vitest HIGH — wrong numbers erode user trust
📡 POST /api/workouts Integration Supertest HIGH — core app functionality
📡 GET /api/workouts Integration Supertest HIGH — verify ownership filtering works
🖥️ Login form E2E Playwright HIGH — most common user entry point
🖥️ Workout creation UI Component + E2E RTL + Playwright HIGH — core user journey
💅 UI component library Unit / Component RTL MEDIUM — rendering, props, interactions
🤖 AI workout generation Integration Supertest (mocked AI) MEDIUM — mock AI, test queue and response handling
📧 Email service Unit Vitest (mocked SMTP) MEDIUM — verify correct email is sent
✓ THE MINIMUM VIABLE TEST SUITE

If you're starting from zero, this order gives maximum protection fastest: 1. Unit tests for all utility functions and calculations (fast, no setup). 2. Integration tests for auth routes — register, login, JWT validation, protected route access. 3. Integration tests for core CRUD routes — create/read workout. 4. One E2E smoke test: can a new user sign up and log their first workout? This set of ~30 tests takes a week to write and protects 80% of your critical paths permanently.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A — TESTING
Our codebase has no tests. How would you start adding them?
Step 1 — Don't try to test everything at once. That leads to analysis paralysis and zero tests shipped. Step 2 — Set up the test runner first. Just getting Vitest installed and one passing test is the first win. Step 3 — Write tests for the next bug fix. Every time a bug is found, write a test that reproduces it before fixing. Now you have tests for real failure modes. Step 4 — Test new code. Every new feature gets at least a unit test for business logic. Step 5 — Gradually backfill critical paths. Auth, payments, data integrity — work backwards from most critical. Key insight: the goal isn't 100% coverage overnight. It's making the codebase slightly safer every week. A suite of 50 good tests is vastly better than 0 tests.
What makes a good test? What makes a bad test?
A good test: is fast (milliseconds for unit, seconds for integration), deterministic (same result every run), tests one behaviour (single logical assertion), has a clear name that says what it tests and what it expects, fails with a useful message, and breaks only when behaviour changes (not on refactors).

A bad test: is flaky (sometimes passes, sometimes fails), tests implementation details (breaks when you rename a variable), has multiple unrelated assertions, uses waitForTimeout instead of proper waiting, tests the same thing as three other tests, and passes trivially even when code is wrong. The single most common bad test pattern: asserting expect(fn()).not.toBeUndefined() — this passes even if the function returns garbage.
Explain the difference between a mock, a stub, and a spy
A stub is a simple fake that returns preset values — it doesn't care how many times it's called. const getUser = () => ({id: 1, name: 'Yash'}). A mock replaces a dependency, records all calls, and can be configured to return different values — you assert on how it was called. vi.fn().mockResolvedValue(data). A spy wraps a real function, lets the real implementation run, but records calls — use when you want real behaviour plus call verification. vi.spyOn(emailService, 'send'). Rule of thumb: use stubs for simple dependencies, mocks for external services, spies when you need to verify that your code correctly delegates to a real function.
PART 13 — TYPESCRIPT

JavaScript With
Superpowers

Types, interfaces, generics, utility types, narrowing, TypeScript in React and Express, Prisma integration, tsconfig mastery, migration guide, and the patterns that make TypeScript genuinely worth the investment — full chapter, zero fluff.

Types Interfaces Generics Utility Types Narrowing React + TS Express + TS Migration
13.1

Why TypeScript?

📖 DEFINITION

TypeScript is a statically typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JavaScript. You add type annotations to variables, functions, and objects — and the TypeScript compiler (tsc) catches type errors before your code ever runs. Every valid JavaScript file is also a valid TypeScript file.

🔬 ANALOGY

JavaScript is like writing a recipe in plain English — flexible but easy to mix up teaspoons with tablespoons. TypeScript adds units of measurement: 2 tsp vs 2 tbsp vs 2 cups. The recipe still works the same, but you can no longer accidentally pass a cup where a teaspoon was expected. The compiler catches it before the dish is ruined.

The Concrete Benefits — With FitoGlobe Examples

JS vs TS — Same Code, Different Feedback
JavaScript — fails silently at runtime
JS ❌
1function calculateBMI(weight, height) {
2 return weight / (height * height);
3}
4
5// Passed string instead of number
6calculateBMI("75", 1.75);
7// Returns NaN — no error thrown!
8// User sees "BMI: NaN" in production 💥
TypeScript — caught at compile time
TS ✓
1function calculateBMI(
2 weight: number,
3 height: number
4): number {
5 return weight / (height * height);
6}
7
8calculateBMI("75", 1.75);
9// ❌ Error: Argument of type 'string'
10// is not assignable to type 'number'
11// Caught before deploy ✓
BenefitHow It Helps FitoGlobe
Catch bugs at compile timeWrong API response shape, missing property — caught before deployment
IDE autocompleteType workout. and VS Code lists every valid property — no docs needed
Safe refactoringRename userId to user_id everywhere — TS shows every place that breaks
Self-documenting codeFunction signatures are readable contracts: (user: User, plan: WorkoutPlan) => Promise<Session>
Team confidenceNew devs understand data shapes immediately — no runtime surprises
Prisma integrationPrisma generates TypeScript types from your schema — DB and app are always in sync

Interview: "Why would you use TypeScript over JavaScript?"

TypeScript catches an entire class of bugs at compile time — wrong types, missing properties, undefined access — that in JavaScript only surface at runtime, sometimes only in production with real user data. The ROI compounds: initial slowdown writing types is recovered many times over in debugging time saved. Critical point: TypeScript is especially valuable for: teams (shared type contracts), large codebases (fearless refactoring), and APIs (enforce response shapes). It's less necessary for tiny solo scripts. Also mention: TypeScript has become the default for new projects at most major companies — it's no longer a niche choice.

13.2

Types & Interfaces

Both type and interface describe the shape of an object. They're 90% interchangeable — the differences matter in specific cases.

types/fitoglobe.ts — core domain types
TS
1// ── PRIMITIVE TYPES ──────────────────────────────────────
2let name: string = 'Yash';
3let age: number = 28;
4let isActive: boolean = true;
5let data: unknown; // safer than any — must narrow before use
6let nothing: null = null;
7let ids: string[] = []; // array of strings
8let pair: [string, number] = ['kg', 75]; // tuple — fixed structure
9
10// ── INTERFACE — preferred for objects / extensible ───────
11interface User {
12 id: string;
13 email: string;
14 name: string;
15 avatarUrl?: string; // ? = optional property
16 readonly createdAt: Date; // readonly = can't be reassigned
17}
18
19// Interface extends another interface
20interface AdminUser extends User {
21 permissions: string[];
22 superAdmin: boolean;
23}
24
25// ── TYPE ALIAS — preferred for unions / computed types ────
26type FitnessGoal = 'muscle_gain' | 'weight_loss' | 'endurance';
27// ^ Union type — only these 3 strings are valid
28
29type WorkoutStatus = 'planned' | 'in_progress' | 'completed' | 'skipped';
30
31type ApiResponse<T> = { // Generic type — T filled in at use-site
32 success: boolean;
33 data: T;
34 message?: string;
35};
36// Usage: ApiResponse<User[]> or ApiResponse<WorkoutPlan>

type vs interface — The Clear Decision Rule

Use interface when…Use type when…
Describing the shape of an object that may be extended Creating union types: A | B | C
Working with classes (implements Interface) Creating intersection types: A & B
Public library APIs (declaration merging possible) Mapped types, conditional types, template literals
Describing React component props (convention) Tuple types: [string, number]
When unsure: use interface for objects, type for everything else. The TypeScript team's own preference.
⚠ The Biggest TS Beginner Mistake
Using any to silence errors

const data: any = response.data — this turns off all type checking for that variable. You've paid the cost of TypeScript (writing types) without getting the benefit (catching errors). any is contagious — it spreads through your codebase and renders your types useless.

Use unknown instead of any — then narrow it properly

unknown forces you to check the type before using it. Use Zod to validate and parse external data: const user = UserSchema.parse(data) — now user is a properly typed User object.

13.3

Advanced Types

Advanced type patterns — FitoGlobe examples
TS
1// ── UNION TYPES — value can be one of several types ──────
2type ID = string | number;
3type MuscleGroup = 'chest' | 'back' | 'legs' | 'shoulders' | 'arms' | 'core';
4
5// ── INTERSECTION TYPES — combine multiple types ───────────
6type WithTimestamps = { createdAt: Date; updatedAt: Date };
7type WorkoutWithMeta = Workout & WithTimestamps; // has ALL fields of both
8
9// ── LITERAL TYPES — exact value, not just the type ───────
10type HttpMethod = 'GET' | 'POST' | 'PUT' | 'DELETE' | 'PATCH';
11type DifficultyLevel = 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5; // only integers 1-5
12
13// ── ENUM — named constants (use sparingly, prefer union) ──
14enum Role {
15 USER = 'user',
16 ADMIN = 'admin',
17 COACH = 'coach'
18}
19// Most TS devs prefer: type Role = 'user' | 'admin' | 'coach'
20
21// ── DISCRIMINATED UNIONS — the "pattern matching" of TS ──
22type ApiResult<T> =
23 | { status: 'success'; data: T }
24 | { status: 'error'; error: string };
25
26function handleResult(result: ApiResult<User>) {
27 if (result.status === 'success') {
28 console.log(result.data.name); // TS knows data exists here
29 } else {
30 console.log(result.error); // TS knows error exists here
31 }
32}
✓ DISCRIMINATED UNIONS — THE MOST POWERFUL TS PATTERN

Discriminated unions with a shared literal tag field (status, type, kind) are the most practical advanced TypeScript pattern. They let TypeScript narrow the exact variant in if/switch blocks, giving you full type safety in each branch. Use them for API responses (success vs error), event handlers (different event types), and state machines (loading vs loaded vs error).

13.4

Generics

📖 DEFINITION

Generics let you write type-safe code that works with any type, rather than writing separate functions for each type. A generic is a type parameter — a placeholder that gets filled in when the function/class is used. Think of it as a type-level variable.

📦 ANALOGY

A generic is like a typed container. A Box<T> is a box that can hold anything — but once you put something in it, TypeScript knows what type it contains. A Box<string> holds a string, a Box<User[]> holds an array of users. The box structure is reused, but the type safety is preserved for whatever's inside.

Generics — from simple to FitoGlobe real-world usage
TS
1// BASIC — generic function
2function first<T>(arr: T[]): T | undefined {
3 return arr[0];
4}
5first([1, 2, 3]) // → inferred as number | undefined
6first(['a', 'b']) // → inferred as string | undefined
7
8// CONSTRAINED — T must be something with an 'id'
9function findById<T extends { id: string }>(
10 items: T[],
11 id: string
12): T | undefined {
13 return items.find(item => item.id === id);
14}
15// Works for User[], Workout[], Exercise[] — any array with id
16
17// REAL-WORLD — typed API response wrapper
18async function apiFetch<T>(url: string): Promise<T> {
19 const res = await fetch(url);
20 const data = await res.json();
21 return data as T;
22}
23
24// Usage — caller specifies what they expect back
25const workouts = await apiFetch<Workout[]>('/api/workouts');
26// workouts is typed as Workout[] — full autocomplete available
27
28// GENERIC REACT HOOK — reusable across any data type
29function useLocalStorage<T>(key: string, initial: T): [T, (v: T) => void] {
30 const [value, setValue] = useState<T>(() => {
31 const stored = localStorage.getItem(key);
32 return stored ? JSON.parse(stored) : initial;
33 });
34 const set = (v: T) => { setValue(v); localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify(v)); };
35 return [value, set];
36}
37// const [theme, setTheme] = useLocalStorage<'dark' | 'light'>('theme', 'dark');

Interview: "Explain generics in TypeScript with a real example"

Generics let you write code that is type-safe but reusable across types. Without generics, you'd either use any (loses type safety) or duplicate functions for each type. Classic example: Array<T> — the same array implementation works for numbers, strings, objects, while keeping full type safety. Real-world example: a generic ApiResponse<T> type wraps any data shape — you write the envelope once and the data type changes at the call site. Constraints with extends let you require the generic to have specific properties while still being flexible — T extends { id: string } allows any object with an id.

13.5

Utility Types

TypeScript ships with built-in utility types that transform existing types into new ones. These eliminate the need to manually duplicate type definitions and are used constantly in real codebases.

Partial<T>

Makes all properties optional. Perfect for PATCH/update operations where you only send changed fields.

type UpdateUser = Partial<User> // { id?: string; email?: string; name?: string }

Required<T>

Makes all optional properties required. Opposite of Partial — ensures nothing is missing.

type CompleteUser = Required<User> // All ? removed — every field mandatory

Readonly<T>

Makes all properties read-only. Excellent for freezing config objects or immutable state.

const config: Readonly<AppConfig> = { ... } // config.apiKey = 'x' → ❌ Error

Pick<T, K>

Creates a new type with only the specified keys. Build focused views of large types.

type UserPreview = Pick<User, 'id' | 'name' | 'avatarUrl'> // Only those 3 fields

Omit<T, K>

Opposite of Pick — creates a type excluding specified keys. Common for stripping sensitive fields.

type PublicUser = Omit<User, 'password' | 'secret'> // Everything except those keys

Record<K, V>

Creates a type with specified keys and value type. Perfect for maps and dictionaries.

type ExerciseMap = Record<MuscleGroup, Exercise[]> // { chest: Exercise[]; back: Exercise[]; ... }

ReturnType<T>

Extracts the return type of a function. Useful when you need the type of what a function returns without duplicating it.

type WorkoutResult = ReturnType<typeof getWorkout> // Whatever getWorkout() returns

NonNullable<T>

Removes null and undefined from a type. Use after runtime null checks.

type DefiniteUser = NonNullable<User | null | undefined> // Just User

Parameters<T>

Extracts parameter types of a function as a tuple.

type LoginParams = Parameters<typeof login> // [email: string, password: string]
Utility types in FitoGlobe — practical examples
TS
1interface Workout {
2 id: string; userId: string; name: string;
3 duration: number; calories: number;
4 exercises: Exercise[]; status: WorkoutStatus;
5}
6
7// Create workout — no id yet (DB generates it)
8type CreateWorkoutDto = Omit<Workout, 'id' | 'userId'>;
9
10// Update workout — any combination of fields
11type UpdateWorkoutDto = Partial<Omit<Workout, 'id' | 'userId'>>;
12// { name?: string; duration?: number; calories?: number; ... }
13
14// Workout list item — just what the card needs
15type WorkoutListItem = Pick<Workout, 'id' | 'name' | 'duration' | 'calories'>;
16
17// Group exercises by muscle group
18type ExerciseLibrary = Record<MuscleGroup, Exercise[]>;
19// { chest: [...], back: [...], legs: [...], ... }
13.6

Type Narrowing & Type Guards

📖 DEFINITION

Type narrowing is TypeScript's ability to refine a broader type into a more specific one within a conditional block. When you check typeof x === 'string', TypeScript knows that inside the if-block, x is definitely a string. Type guards are functions that perform these runtime checks with a special x is Type return type.

Type narrowing techniques — all patterns
TS
1// 1. typeof guard — primitives
2function formatValue(val: string | number) {
3 if (typeof val === 'string') {
4 return val.toUpperCase(); // TS knows: val is string here
5 }
6 return val.toFixed(2); // TS knows: val is number here
7}
8
9// 2. instanceof guard — class instances
10function handleError(err: unknown) {
11 if (err instanceof Error) {
12 console.error(err.message); // TS knows: err.message exists
13 }
14}
15
16// 3. 'in' guard — check if property exists
17type GoogleUser = { googleId: string; email: string };
18type LocalUser = { password: string; email: string };
19
20function handleLogin(user: GoogleUser | LocalUser) {
21 if ('googleId' in user) {
22 handleGoogleOAuth(user.googleId); // TS knows: GoogleUser here
23 } else {
24 verifyPassword(user.password); // TS knows: LocalUser here
25 }
26}
27
28// 4. Custom type guard — 'is' return type
29function isWorkout(obj: unknown): obj is Workout {
30 return (
31 typeof obj === 'object' &&
32 obj !== null &&
33 'id' in obj &&
34 'userId' in obj
35 );
36}
37// After: if (isWorkout(data)) { data.userId → fully typed }
13.7

TypeScript in React

React + TypeScript — complete FitoGlobe component patterns
TSX
1import { useState, useEffect, FC, ReactNode, ChangeEvent } from 'react';
2
3// ── COMPONENT PROPS INTERFACE ────────────────────────────
4interface WorkoutCardProps {
5 workout: Workout;
6 onDelete: (id: string) => void; // callback with specific signature
7 onEdit?: (workout: Workout) => void; // optional callback
8 className?: string;
9 children?: ReactNode; // ReactNode = anything renderable
10}
11
12// FC = FunctionComponent — automatically types 'children'
13const WorkoutCard: FC<WorkoutCardProps> = ({ workout, onDelete, onEdit }) => {
14 return (
15 <div>
16 <h3>{workout.name}</h3>
17 <button onClick={() => onDelete(workout.id)}>Delete</button>
18 </div>
19 );
20};
21
22// ── TYPED useState ───────────────────────────────────────
23const [workouts, setWorkouts] = useState<Workout[]>([]); // array
24const [user, setUser] = useState<User | null>(null); // nullable
25const [goal, setGoal] = useState<FitnessGoal>('muscle_gain');
26
27// ── TYPED EVENT HANDLERS ─────────────────────────────────
28const handleEmailChange = (e: ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) => {
29 setEmail(e.target.value); // e.target.value is typed as string
30};
31
32// ── TYPED useRef ─────────────────────────────────────────
33const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null);
34// inputRef.current?.focus() — TS knows it might be null
35
36// ── TYPED CONTEXT ─────────────────────────────────────────
37interface AuthContextType {
38 user: User | null;
39 login: (email: string, password: string) => Promise<void>;
40 logout: () => void;
41}
42const AuthContext = createContext<AuthContextType | undefined>(undefined);
13.8

TypeScript in Express

Express + TypeScript — typed routes, middleware, req augmentation
TS
1import { Request, Response, NextFunction, RequestHandler } from 'express';
2
3// ── EXTEND Request to add custom properties ───────────────
4// Express's req.user is undefined by default — add it via declaration merging
5declare global {
6 namespace Express {
7 interface Request {
8 user?: { id: string; email: string; role: Role };
9 }
10 }
11}
12// Now req.user is typed everywhere — no more (req as any).user
13
14// ── TYPED MIDDLEWARE ─────────────────────────────────────
15export const authenticate: RequestHandler = (req, res, next) => {
16 const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
17 if (!token) {
18 res.status(401).json({ error: 'Unauthorized' });
19 return; // return void — don't call next()
20 }
21 try {
22 const payload = verifyToken(token);
23 req.user = payload; // typed — no more casting
24 next();
25 } catch {
26 res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid token' });
27 }
28};
29
30// ── TYPED CONTROLLER — generic request body ───────────────
31export const createWorkout = async (
32 req: Request<{}, {}, CreateWorkoutDto>, // 3rd generic = body type
33 res: Response
34) => {
35 const { name, duration, exercises } = req.body; // fully typed
36 const userId = req.user!.id; // ! = post-auth, user exists
37 const workout = await prisma.workout.create({ data: {userId,name,duration} });
38 res.status(201).json({ workout });
39};
ℹ️ Request<Params, ResBody, ReqBody, Query> — all 4 Generics

Express's Request type accepts 4 generics: 1. Route params ({id: string} for /workouts/:id), 2. Response body type, 3. Request body type (most commonly used), 4. Query string type ({page: string, limit: string}). Pass only the ones you need — use {} for unused positions.

13.9

TypeScript + Prisma

The Prisma + TypeScript combination is one of the strongest type-safety stories in backend development. Prisma generates TypeScript types directly from your schema — meaning your database shape and your application types are always in sync.

Using Prisma-generated types in FitoGlobe
TS
1// Prisma generates these from schema.prisma — never write them manually
2import { Prisma, User, Workout, Exercise } from '@prisma/client';
3
4// The generated User type matches schema exactly — no drift possible
5// type User = { id: string; email: string; name: string; ... }
6
7// Prisma namespace has generated Input types for create/update
8type WorkoutCreateInput = Prisma.WorkoutCreateInput;
9type WorkoutUpdateInput = Prisma.WorkoutUpdateInput;
10type WorkoutWhereInput = Prisma.WorkoutWhereInput;
11
12// Get the type of a Prisma query result with relations included
13type WorkoutWithExercises = Prisma.WorkoutGetPayload<{
14 include: { exercises: true }
15}>;
16// ^ Workout & { exercises: Exercise[] } — exact type of what prisma returns
17
18// Type-safe query — wrong field names are caught at compile time
19const workouts = await prisma.workout.findMany({
20 where: { userId: 'u1' },
21 include: { exercises: true }, // return type expands to include exercises
22 orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }
23});
24// workouts is typed as WorkoutWithExercises[] automatically
✓ THE PRISMA + TS SUPERPOWER

When you change a field in schema.prisma and run prisma generate, TypeScript immediately shows you every place in your codebase that uses the changed field. Rename user.displayName to user.name — TS highlights all 47 files that need updating. Without TS, you'd find this bug in production when the field is undefined. This alone is worth the TypeScript investment for any serious application.

13.10

tsconfig.json Deep Dive

tsconfig.json — FitoGlobe fully annotated config
JSON
1{
2 "compilerOptions": {
3 // ── TARGET & MODULE ──────────────────────────────────
4 "target": "ES2022", // compile to ES2022 — supports optional chaining
5 "module": "NodeNext", // use Node.js ESM resolution
6 "moduleResolution": "NodeNext",
7 "lib": ["ES2022", "DOM"], // include DOM types for browser code
8
9 // ── STRICTNESS ───────────────────────────────────────
10 "strict": true, // enables ALL strict checks below (recommended)
11 // strict = true enables these:
12 // strictNullChecks: null/undefined are not assignable to any type
13 // strictFunctionTypes: stricter function parameter checking
14 // noImplicitAny: can't use variables without typing them
15 "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true, // arr[0] could be undefined
16 "noUnusedLocals": true, // error on unused variables
17 "noUnusedParameters": true, // error on unused function params
18 "noImplicitReturns": true, // all code paths must return a value
19
20 // ── OUTPUT ───────────────────────────────────────────
21 "outDir": "./dist", // compiled JS goes here
22 "rootDir": "./src", // source files are here
23 "sourceMap": true, // enable source maps for debugging
24 "declaration": true, // generate .d.ts files (for libraries)
25
26 // ── PATHS (import aliases) ────────────────────────────
27 "baseUrl": ".",
28 "paths": {
29 "@/*": ["src/*"], // @/utils/date = src/utils/date
30 "@types/*": ["src/types/*"] // @types/workout = src/types/workout
31 }
32 },
33 "include": ["src/**/*"],
34 "exclude": ["node_modules", "dist"]
35}
⚠️ ALWAYS ENABLE strict: true

Without strict: true, TypeScript is much weaker. The two most impactful options it enables: strictNullChecks (catches the most bugs — null is no longer assignable everywhere) and noImplicitAny (every variable must have a type or be inferable). Teams that start without strict: true and try to enable it later face hundreds of errors. Start strict from day one.

13.11

JS → TS Migration Guide

Migrating an existing JavaScript codebase to TypeScript doesn't have to be done all at once. The incremental approach lets you migrate file by file without breaking anything.

  • 1
    Install TypeScript and typesnpm install typescript @types/node @types/express --save-dev. Generate initial config: npx tsc --init.
  • 2
    Set allowJs: true in tsconfigThis lets TypeScript process .js files — your existing code compiles without changes. Also set "checkJs": false initially to avoid errors in JS files.
  • 3
    Rename files one at a timeStart with leaf files (utilities, helpers — no dependencies). Rename utils/calories.js → utils/calories.ts. Fix the errors that appear.
  • 4
    Add types to the renamed filesAdd function parameter types and return types. Start with explicit : any where needed, then replace them with real types over time.
  • 5
    Work inward — core modules nextAfter utilities, migrate models/types, then services, then routes. Each migration creates a stable typed foundation for the next layer.
  • 6
    Enable strict mode graduallyFirst enable strictNullChecks alone — fix errors. Then enable the rest of strict. This spreads the pain across sprints rather than all at once.
  • 7
    Remove allowJs and checkJsOnce all files are .ts, disable JS support. You're fully TypeScript. Set up tsc --noEmit in CI to catch type errors before deploy.
✓ THE PRACTICAL MIGRATION TIMELINE

For FitoGlobe-sized projects (~50 files): a realistic migration takes 2–4 sprints when done incrementally. Week 1: setup + utilities. Week 2: models and DB types. Week 3: services and controllers. Week 4: routes, middleware, final cleanup. Never try to do it in one weekend — you'll introduce bugs and your team will hate TypeScript forever.

Interview: "How would you migrate a JavaScript codebase to TypeScript?"

Key approach: incrementally, never all at once. Enable allowJs: true so JS and TS files coexist. Migrate leaf files first (utilities with no dependencies), then work inward to core modules and routes. Use // @ts-ignore or any sparingly as temporary scaffolding — track them as tech debt to fix. Enable strict checks gradually, one per sprint. The most important thing: keep the CI green throughout — add tsc --noEmit to CI from day one and fix errors before merging. Mention that you'd also write the new domain types file (User, Workout, etc.) early — this gives the whole team a shared vocabulary from the start.

13.12

TS Patterns & Anti-patterns

Patterns That Make TypeScript Worth It

Advanced patterns — exhaustive checks, branded types, const assertions
TS
1// ── EXHAUSTIVE SWITCH — never miss a union case ───────────
2type WorkoutStatus = 'planned' | 'completed' | 'skipped';
3
4function assertNever(x: never): never {
5 throw new Error(`Unhandled case: ${x}`);
6}
7
8function getStatusLabel(status: WorkoutStatus) {
9 switch (status) {
10 case 'planned': return 'Scheduled';
11 case 'completed': return 'Done ✓';
12 case 'skipped': return 'Skipped';
13 default: return assertNever(status);
14 // If you add 'in_progress' to WorkoutStatus and forget to add
15 // a case here, TypeScript will error at compile time. 🎯
16 }
17}
18
19// ── BRANDED TYPES — prevent mixing semantically different IDs ─
20type UserId = string & { readonly brand: 'UserId' };
21type WorkoutId = string & { readonly brand: 'WorkoutId' };
22
23function deleteWorkout(id: WorkoutId) { ... }
24const userId = 'u1' as UserId;
25const workoutId = 'w1' as WorkoutId;
26deleteWorkout(userId); // ❌ Error: UserId not assignable to WorkoutId
27deleteWorkout(workoutId); // ✓ Correct
28
29// ── CONST ASSERTION — narrow literal types ────────────────
30const ROUTES = {
31 home: '/',
32 dashboard: '/dashboard',
33 workouts: '/workouts'
34} as const; // makes all values readonly literals
35type Route = typeof ROUTES[keyof typeof ROUTES];
36// Route = '/' | '/dashboard' | '/workouts'
⚠ TypeScript Anti-patterns
Type assertions everywhere: data as User

Type assertions (as) tell TypeScript "trust me, I know what this is." They bypass type checking entirely. If the data isn't actually that type at runtime, you get mysterious errors with no TS safety net.

Use Zod to parse and validate external data, not type assertions

const user = UserSchema.parse(data) — Zod validates at runtime AND gives you a properly typed result. Type assertion = lie to the compiler. Zod = verified truth.

Non-null assertions everywhere: user!.id

The ! post-fix assertion tells TS "this is definitely not null/undefined." Use it when you've already checked, but many devs use it to silence errors without understanding why they appeared.

Handle null properly with optional chaining and guards

user?.id ?? 'unknown' handles null gracefully. Reserve ! for cases where you've already verified non-null in the surrounding code — like after an auth middleware.

Duplicating Prisma types manually

Writing interface User { id: string; email: string; ... } manually when Prisma already generated that type. Now you have two sources of truth that will drift apart.

Import and extend Prisma-generated types

import { User } from '@prisma/client' and build on top with type UserWithWorkouts = User & { workouts: Workout[] }. One source of truth: the schema.

🎤 INTERVIEW Q&A — TYPESCRIPT
What's the difference between unknown and any?
any opts out of type checking entirely — you can call methods on it, assign it anywhere, do anything with it. TypeScript ignores it completely. unknown is a type-safe version of any — you can assign anything to it, but you must narrow it before using it (check with typeof, instanceof, or a type guard). Use unknown for values whose type you genuinely don't know at compile time (API responses, error objects in catch blocks), then validate and narrow. any is almost always a mistake unless you're intentionally bypassing the type system for a specific reason.
When would you use type vs interface?
In practice they're interchangeable for object shapes. The key differences: interface supports declaration merging (can be extended after definition — useful in libraries) and is generally preferred for object shapes and class contracts. type is required for unions (A | B), intersections (A & B), mapped types, conditional types, and tuple types. Simple rule: use interface for objects/classes, type for everything else. When in doubt, either works — consistency within a codebase matters more than which you choose.
How does TypeScript help with API type safety between frontend and backend?
The key technique is sharing types between frontend and backend. In a monorepo, put types in a shared packages/types package — both the React app and Express server import from it. When the API response shape changes, update the shared type once and TypeScript highlights every place in the frontend that now breaks. More advanced: tools like tRPC or Zod let you define the API contract once on the server and have the client automatically get fully typed versions of every endpoint — zero manual type duplication. This is one of TypeScript's highest-value use cases in full-stack development.
PART 14 — DOCKER & CONTAINERS

Your App, Packaged
Perfectly

Images, containers, layers, Dockerfiles, multi-stage builds, Docker Compose, networking, volumes, a complete FitoGlobe stack definition, production deployment patterns, Kubernetes intro, and container security — the complete containerisation chapter.

Docker Dockerfile Compose Networking Volumes Multi-Stage Kubernetes Security
14.1

Why Docker?

📖 DEFINITION

Docker is a platform that packages your application and all its dependencies — runtime, libraries, config — into a portable unit called a container. Containers run identically on any machine that has Docker installed: your laptop, a teammate's Windows PC, GitHub Actions CI, Railway, AWS. "It works on my machine" is no longer a problem.

📦 ANALOGY

Before shipping containers, loading cargo was chaotic — every ship, truck, and warehouse had different systems. After standardisation, one 20ft container could be transferred between ship, truck, and train without repacking anything. Docker does exactly this for software: one standard container format that runs on any infrastructure without repacking.

The Problems Docker Solves

Before Docker vs After Docker
BEFORE DOCKER
😤 "Works on my machine, breaks in CI" — different Node.js versions, OS libs
⏱️ 2-hour new dev setup — install Node, PostgreSQL, Redis, configure everything
😱 "Works in staging, crashes in production" — different OS, lib versions
💸 Each service needs its own VM — expensive, wasteful
AFTER DOCKER
✓ Same container runs identically everywhere — laptop, CI, prod
⚡ New dev runs docker compose up — entire stack ready in 2 minutes
🎯 What you test is what you deploy — same image, same environment
💡 Multiple containers on one host — efficient resource sharing
🏋️ FITOGLOBE WITHOUT DOCKER

A new developer joining FitoGlobe has to: install Node.js 20 (not 18, not 21), install PostgreSQL 15, create the database, run migrations, install Redis, set up all env vars, run the frontend and backend. That's a half-day of setup — and it still breaks because their macOS PostgreSQL behaves differently from the Ubuntu server in production. With Docker: git clone, docker compose up, done in 3 minutes. Every time. On any OS.

Interview: "What problem does Docker solve and why do companies use it?"

Docker solves three fundamental problems: environment consistency (the same image runs identically in dev, CI, and production — no more "works on my machine"), dependency isolation (each service carries its own runtime, preventing version conflicts between services on the same host), and deployment portability (a Docker image runs on any cloud, any OS, without code changes). Companies adopt it because it dramatically reduces ops overhead: infrastructure becomes code (Dockerfile and docker-compose.yml), rollbacks are trivial (re-deploy the previous image), and scaling is just running more container instances.

14.2

Images, Containers & Layers

The Three Core Concepts

ConceptWhat It IsAnalogyFitoGlobe Example
Image Read-only blueprint — snapshot of filesystem + config A recipe / class definition fitoglobe-backend:1.0.0 — Node.js 20 + your code + deps
Container Running instance of an image — writable, isolated process A running oven following the recipe / an object instance The live Express server process serving API requests
Registry Remote store for images — push/pull like git GitHub for images Docker Hub, GitHub Container Registry, AWS ECR

Image Layers — How Docker Stores Files

Docker images are built in layers — each instruction in a Dockerfile creates a new layer. Layers are cached and reused across builds. This is why layer order matters dramatically for build speed.

Docker Image Layer Stack — FitoGlobe Backend
COPY src/ . ~50KB · YOUR CODE · changes every commit
RUN npx prisma generate ~8MB · changes with schema
RUN npm ci --only=production ~80MB · changes with package.json
COPY package*.json . ~2KB · changes with deps update
WORKDIR /app ~0B · almost never changes
FROM node:20-alpine ~7MB · base OS · cached across all Node projects
Cache rule: When a layer changes, all layers above it must be rebuilt. That's why package.json is copied BEFORE src/ — npm install only reruns when deps change, not on every code change. This saves ~30s per build.
ℹ️ IMAGES ARE IMMUTABLE — CONTAINERS ADD A WRITABLE LAYER

When you start a container from an image, Docker adds a thin writable layer on top. All changes (file writes, logs, temp files) go into this writable layer. When the container stops and is removed, the writable layer is deleted — the underlying image is untouched. This is why you must use volumes for any data you need to persist (databases, user uploads).

14.3

Dockerfile Deep Dive

📖 DEFINITION

A Dockerfile is a text file of instructions that tells Docker how to build an image. Each instruction creates a layer. The result is a reproducible, versioned image that can be built identically anywhere.

Every Dockerfile Instruction Explained

Dockerfile — every instruction with deep annotations
DOCKERFILE
1# FROM — base image to build on top of
2# node:20-alpine = Node.js 20 on Alpine Linux (5MB vs 900MB for full Ubuntu)
3FROM node:20-alpine
4
5# WORKDIR — set working directory for all subsequent commands
6# Creates /app if it doesn't exist. Better than RUN mkdir + cd
7WORKDIR /app
8
9# COPY — copy files from host into the image
10# Copy package files FIRST — separate layer, cached when src changes
11COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
12
13# RUN — execute a command during build (creates a layer)
14# npm ci = exact install from lockfile (reproducible + faster than npm install)
15RUN npm ci --only=production
16
17# Copy Prisma schema before generating client
18COPY prisma ./prisma
19RUN npx prisma generate # generate typed Prisma client
20
21# Copy source code LAST — most frequently changing layer
22COPY src ./src
23
24# ENV — set environment variables inside the image
25ENV NODE_ENV=production
26
27# EXPOSE — DOCUMENT which port the container listens on
28# Does NOT actually publish the port — that's done with -p at runtime
29EXPOSE 5000
30
31# USER — run as non-root for security
32# node image includes a 'node' user — always use it
33USER node
34
35# CMD vs ENTRYPOINT
36# CMD = default command, overridable at runtime
37# ENTRYPOINT = always runs, CMD appended as args (use for scripts)
38# JSON array form (exec form) is preferred over shell form
39CMD ["node", "src/server.js"]

.dockerignore — What to Exclude

.dockerignore — always create this file
TEXT
1node_modules # CRITICAL — never copy, reinstall inside container
2.git # git history not needed at runtime
3.env # secrets never go in images
4.env.local
5dist # built output — will be rebuilt inside
6coverage # test coverage reports
7*.test.js # tests not needed in prod image
8*.spec.js
9README.md
10Dockerfile # no need to include the Dockerfile itself
11docker-compose*.yml
12.github
13logs/
🚨 NEVER COPY node_modules INTO AN IMAGE

Always reinstall inside the container with RUN npm ci. Copying node_modules from your host causes: wrong platform binaries (compiled for macOS, running on Linux), massive image size, inconsistent behaviour. The .dockerignore file must exclude node_modules — this is non-negotiable.

⚠ Common Dockerfile Mistakes
Copying source before installing dependencies

COPY . . then RUN npm ci — every code change invalidates the npm install cache. A 30-second npm install runs on every single rebuild.

Copy package files first, install, then copy source

Layer order: COPY package*.json ./ → RUN npm ci → COPY src ./src. Now npm install only reruns when package.json changes.

Using latest tag on base images

FROM node:latest — the image changes with every Node.js release. Your container that worked last week might fail today when Node 22 becomes latest.

Pin exact versions: FROM node:20.11.0-alpine3.19

Use specific version + distro tags. This makes your builds reproducible and prevents surprise breakage from upstream updates.

Running as root inside containers

Default Docker containers run as root. If your container is compromised, the attacker has root access to everything the container can reach.

Always add USER node before CMD

The official Node.js image includes a non-root node user. One line massively reduces your attack surface.

14.4

Multi-Stage Builds

📖 DEFINITION

Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM statements in one Dockerfile. Each stage can build on the previous one, and you can selectively copy only the artefacts you need from each stage into the final image. Result: tiny production images that contain only what's needed to run, not what's needed to build.

FitoGlobe Frontend — Multi-Stage Build

frontend/Dockerfile — build React, serve with Nginx
DOCKERFILE
1# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
2# STAGE 1: Build the React app
3# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
4FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
5WORKDIR /app
6
7# Install ALL deps (including devDeps like vite)
8COPY package*.json ./
9RUN npm ci # includes devDependencies for build
10
11COPY . .
12
13# Build args for env vars (e.g. VITE_API_URL)
14ARG VITE_API_URL
15ENV VITE_API_URL=$VITE_API_URL
16
17RUN npm run build # outputs to /app/dist
18
19# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
20# STAGE 2: Serve with Nginx
21# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
22FROM nginx:1.25-alpine AS runner
23
24# Copy ONLY the built dist/ — not node_modules, not source
25COPY --from=builder /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html
26
27# Copy nginx config for SPA (React Router fallback)
28COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
29
30EXPOSE 80
31CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
32
33# Final image size: ~25MB (vs ~650MB single-stage with all node_modules!)
nginx.conf — SPA fallback for React Router
NGINX
1server {
2 listen 80;
3 root /usr/share/nginx/html;
4 index index.html;
5
6 # SPA fallback — unknown paths serve index.html
7 # React Router handles routing in the browser
8 location / {
9 try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html;
10 }
11
12 # Cache hashed assets aggressively
13 location /assets/ {
14 expires 1y;
15 add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable";
16 }
17}
✓ IMAGE SIZE COMPARISON

Single-stage React build (Node.js + all node_modules): ~650MB. Multi-stage (Nginx + built dist only): ~25MB. That's a 26× size reduction. Smaller images mean: faster CI build times, faster deploys (less data to transfer), reduced attack surface, lower registry storage costs.

14.5

Essential Docker Commands

Docker CLI — complete command reference
BASH
1# ── BUILDING IMAGES ──────────────────────────────────────
2docker build -t fitoglobe-backend:1.0.0 . # build + tag
3docker build --no-cache -t fitoglobe-backend . # force full rebuild
4docker build --build-arg VITE_API_URL=https://api.fitoglobe.com .
5
6# ── RUNNING CONTAINERS ───────────────────────────────────
7docker run fitoglobe-backend # run (attach mode)
8docker run -d fitoglobe-backend # -d = detached (background)
9docker run -d -p 5000:5000 fitoglobe-backend # -p host:container port
10docker run -d --name backend fitoglobe-backend # --name for easy reference
11docker run --rm fitoglobe-backend # --rm = delete on stop
12docker run -e NODE_ENV=production fitoglobe-backend # -e = env var
13
14# ── INSPECTING CONTAINERS ────────────────────────────────
15docker ps # list running containers
16docker ps -a # all containers (incl. stopped)
17docker logs backend # see container stdout/stderr
18docker logs -f backend # -f = follow (live tail)
19docker inspect backend # full JSON metadata
20docker exec -it backend sh # shell into running container
21docker stats # live CPU/memory usage
22
23# ── MANAGING ─────────────────────────────────────────────
24docker stop backend # graceful stop (SIGTERM)
25docker kill backend # force stop (SIGKILL)
26docker rm backend # delete stopped container
27docker rmi fitoglobe-backend # delete image
28docker system prune # delete all unused resources
29docker system prune -a # also removes unused images
30
31# ── REGISTRY ─────────────────────────────────────────────
32docker tag fitoglobe-backend:1.0.0 ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:1.0.0
33docker push ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:1.0.0
34docker pull ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:1.0.0

Interview: "How would you debug a container that keeps crashing on startup?"

1. Check exit code: docker ps -a shows the exit code. Non-zero = error. 2. Read logs: docker logs <container_id> — even crashed containers retain their logs. 3. Override the CMD: docker run --entrypoint sh fitoglobe-backend to start a shell instead of the app — explore the filesystem, check file permissions. 4. Check env vars: missing DATABASE_URL is the most common crash cause. 5. Run locally with verbose output: docker run -e DEBUG=* fitoglobe-backend. Key insight: the container usually tells you exactly what went wrong in its logs — most developers don't check them first.

14.6

Docker Compose

📖 DEFINITION

Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container applications with a single YAML file. Instead of running 3 separate docker run commands with different flags for backend, frontend, and database — you define everything in docker-compose.yml and start the entire stack with docker compose up.

FitoGlobe Development Compose File

docker-compose.yml — full FitoGlobe dev stack
YAML
1version: '3.9'
2
3services:
4
5 # ── PostgreSQL Database ──────────────────────────────────
6 db:
7 image: postgres:15-alpine # official Postgres image
8 environment:
9 POSTGRES_DB: fitoglobe_dev
10 POSTGRES_USER: postgres
11 POSTGRES_PASSWORD: devpassword # dev only — not production!
12 ports:
13 - "5432:5432" # expose to host for local tools
14 volumes:
15 - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data # persist DB data
16 healthcheck: # wait until DB is ready
17 test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
18 interval: 5s
19 retries: 5
20
21 # ── Redis Cache ──────────────────────────────────────────
22 redis:
23 image: redis:7-alpine
24 ports:
25 - "6379:6379"
26 command: redis-server --appendonly yes # enable persistence
27 volumes:
28 - redis_data:/data
29
30 # ── Node.js Backend ──────────────────────────────────────
31 backend:
32 build:
33 context: ./backend # build from this directory
34 dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev # dev-specific Dockerfile
35 ports:
36 - "5000:5000"
37 environment:
38 # 'db' resolves to the db container IP on the internal network
39 DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:devpassword@db:5432/fitoglobe_dev
40 REDIS_URL: redis://redis:6379
41 JWT_SECRET: dev_secret_at_least_32_chars_long
42 volumes:
43 - ./backend/src:/app/src # bind mount for hot reload
44 - /app/node_modules # anonymous volume — preserve container's node_modules
45 depends_on:
46 db:
47 condition: service_healthy # wait for healthcheck to pass
48 restart: unless-stopped # auto-restart on crash
49
50 # ── React Frontend ───────────────────────────────────────
51 frontend:
52 build:
53 context: ./frontend
54 dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
55 ports:
56 - "5173:5173"
57 volumes:
58 - ./frontend/src:/app/src # Vite HMR works through this
59 - /app/node_modules
60 depends_on: [backend]
61
62# Named volumes — data persists across container restarts
63volumes:
64 postgres_data:
65 redis_data:

The Essential Compose Commands

docker compose — daily workflow commands
BASH
1docker compose up # start all services (foreground)
2docker compose up -d # start in background
3docker compose up --build # rebuild images then start
4docker compose up backend # start only the backend service
5
6docker compose down # stop + remove containers
7docker compose down -v # also delete volumes (wipes DB data!)
8
9docker compose logs -f backend # follow logs for one service
10docker compose ps # status of all services
11docker compose exec backend sh # shell into running container
12
13# Run one-off commands inside a service
14docker compose run --rm backend npx prisma migrate dev
15docker compose run --rm backend npx prisma studio
16docker compose run --rm backend npm test
17
18# Override compose files (dev vs prod settings)
19docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up
⚠ Common Docker Compose Mistakes
Using depends_on without healthcheck

depends_on: [db] only waits for the container to start, not for the database inside to be ready to accept connections. Backend starts, tries to connect, PostgreSQL is still initialising — connection refused crash.

Use condition: service_healthy with a proper healthcheck

Add a healthcheck to the db service (pg_isready), then use depends_on: db: condition: service_healthy. Now backend genuinely waits for DB readiness.

Not using a named volume for node_modules

Bind mounting ./backend:/app without excluding /app/node_modules causes the host's node_modules (macOS binaries) to overwrite the container's node_modules (Linux binaries). Native modules break.

Always add an anonymous volume for node_modules

Add - /app/node_modules as a separate volume entry. This creates an anonymous volume that Docker manages — it shadows the bind mount's node_modules without overwriting the container's version.

14.7

Docker Networking

📖 DEFINITION

Docker networking isolates containers into virtual networks. Containers on the same network can communicate using service names as hostnames — no IP addresses needed. Docker Compose automatically creates a network for all services in a compose file, which is why DATABASE_URL: postgresql://db:5432 works — db resolves to the database container's IP.

Docker Network — FitoGlobe Container Communication
fitoglobe_default network (internal)
🖥️
frontend
:5173 (exposed)
⚙️
backend
:5000 (exposed)
🗄️
db
:5432 (internal only)
⚡
redis
:6379 (internal only)
backend → db:5432 ✓  |  backend → redis:6379 ✓  |  frontend → backend:5000 ✓
internet → db:5432 ✗ (not exposed)  |  internet → redis:6379 ✗

Network Types

DriverUse CaseExample
bridge (default)Container-to-container communication on same hostFitoGlobe compose stack — all services on one host
hostContainer uses host's network directly — no isolationPerformance-critical apps, Linux only
noneComplete network isolation — no networkingSecurity-sensitive batch jobs
overlayMulti-host networking for Docker Swarm / KubernetesDistributed container orchestration
✓ SECURITY: NEVER EXPOSE DATABASE PORTS IN PRODUCTION

In development, you might expose 5432:5432 so tools like TablePlus can connect. In production, remove the ports mapping for db and redis entirely. They only need to be accessible to the backend container on the internal network — not to the public internet. Exposing database ports publicly is a critical security vulnerability.

14.8

Volumes & Data Persistence

📖 DEFINITION

Container filesystems are ephemeral — when a container is removed, all data it wrote is gone. Volumes solve this by mounting persistent storage from outside the container. Three types exist: named volumes (managed by Docker), bind mounts (link host directory), and tmpfs mounts (in-memory, temporary).

TypeDefinitionUse CaseFitoGlobe Example
Named Volume Docker-managed storage, persists across container restarts and removals Database data, anything that must survive container lifecycle postgres_data — PostgreSQL files survive docker compose down
Bind Mount Maps a host directory into the container — changes on host reflect instantly Development hot-reload — source code changes appear in container live ./backend/src:/app/src — save a file, nodemon restarts immediately
Anonymous Volume Docker-managed, no name — deleted when container removed Exclude specific paths from bind mounts (the node_modules trick) /app/node_modules — protects container's node_modules from bind mount
tmpfs Mount In-memory only — not written to disk Sensitive data (secrets, tokens) that must never touch disk JWT private keys in a security-sensitive deployment
Volume management commands
BASH
1# List all named volumes
2docker volume ls
3
4# Inspect a volume (find where data lives on host)
5docker volume inspect fitoglobe_postgres_data
6
7# Backup a named volume (dump PostgreSQL data)
8docker exec db pg_dump -U postgres fitoglobe_dev > backup.sql
9
10# DANGER: Delete a volume (destroys all data)
11docker volume rm fitoglobe_postgres_data
12
13# Clean up unused volumes (reclaim disk space)
14docker volume prune
14.9

Full FitoGlobe Docker Stack

Putting everything together — the complete, production-ready container architecture for FitoGlobe.

FitoGlobe Complete Container Architecture
🌍 Internet / Load Balancer
↓
Nginx Reverse Proxy (Port 80/443) — TLS termination, rate limiting, compression
↙
↘
🖥️
frontend
Nginx + React dist
Port 80 internal
⚙️
backend × 2
Node.js / Express
Port 5000 internal
↓↓
🗄️
PostgreSQL
Named volume
Internal only
⚡
Redis
Sessions + cache
Internal only

docker-compose.prod.yml — Production Overrides

docker-compose.prod.yml — production-specific overrides
YAML
1# Merged with docker-compose.yml at deploy time:
2# docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d
3
4services:
5 backend:
6 image: ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:${TAG:-latest} # use pre-built image
7 restart: always # always restart on crash
8 deploy:
9 replicas: 2 # run 2 backend instances
10 resources:
11 limits:
12 cpus: '0.5' # half a CPU per instance
13 memory: 512M # 512MB RAM limit
14 environment:
15 DATABASE_URL: ${DATABASE_URL} # from .env or env vars
16 JWT_SECRET: ${JWT_SECRET}
17 volumes: [] # remove dev bind mounts in prod
18 db:
19 ports: [] # DO NOT expose DB port in production
14.10

Docker in Production

CI/CD — Build, Push, Deploy Pipeline

.github/workflows/docker-deploy.yml — full CI/CD pipeline
YAML
1name: Build & Deploy Docker
2on:
3 push:
4 branches: [main]
5
6jobs:
7 build-and-push:
8 runs-on: ubuntu-latest
9 steps:
10 - uses: actions/checkout@v4
11
12 # Log in to GitHub Container Registry
13 - uses: docker/login-action@v3
14 with:
15 registry: ghcr.io
16 username: ${{ github.actor }}
17 password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} # auto-provided by Actions
18
19 # Build and push with build cache from registry
20 - uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
21 with:
22 context: ./backend
23 push: true
24 tags: |
25 ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:latest
26 ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:${{ github.sha }} # exact commit tag
27 cache-from: type=registry,ref=ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:cache
28 cache-to: type=registry,ref=ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:cache,mode=max
29
30 # SSH into server and pull + redeploy
31 - name: Deploy to server
32 uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
33 with:
34 host: ${{ secrets.SERVER_HOST }}
35 key: ${{ secrets.SSH_PRIVATE_KEY }}
36 script: |
37 cd /opt/fitoglobe
38 docker compose pull
39 docker compose up -d --no-deps backend # rolling update
40 docker image prune -f # clean old images
ℹ️ ALWAYS TAG IMAGES WITH COMMIT SHA

Tagging with :latest only means you can't roll back to a specific version — "latest" always points to the most recent push. Always also tag with the git commit SHA (:abc1234) or a semantic version (:1.4.2). Rolling back to a specific deploy is then: docker compose up -d --no-deps backend=ghcr.io/yash/fitoglobe-backend:abc1234. Takes 30 seconds, saves hours of debugging.

14.11

Kubernetes — The 10-Minute Intro

📖 DEFINITION

Kubernetes (K8s) is a container orchestration system — it automates deployment, scaling, and management of containerised applications across a cluster of machines. Where Docker Compose manages containers on one machine, Kubernetes manages containers across many machines simultaneously, with automatic failover, rolling updates, and scaling.

🚢 ANALOGY

Docker is like a single ship that carries containers. Kubernetes is the entire shipping company — the fleet manager that decides which ship carries which containers, reroutes shipments when a ship has problems, adds more ships when demand increases, and ensures cargo always arrives at the right port. The containers themselves (Docker images) stay the same — it's the orchestration that scales.

Key Kubernetes Concepts

ConceptWhat It IsDocker Compose Equivalent
PodSmallest deployable unit — one or more containers that share network/storageA single service
DeploymentManages a set of identical Pods — handles rolling updates, rollbacks, scalingservices + replicas
ServiceStable DNS name + load balancing across Pods (Pods come and go, Service is permanent)Service name in network
IngressHTTP routing rules — route /api to backend, / to frontendNginx reverse proxy
ConfigMapNon-secret configuration dataEnvironment variables
SecretEncrypted sensitive data (DB passwords, API keys).env file
PersistentVolumeLong-lived storage claim that survives Pod restartsNamed volumes
NodeA physical/virtual machine in the clusterYour laptop / server
🏋️ WHEN DOES FITOGLOBE NEED KUBERNETES?

FitoGlobe does not need Kubernetes now. Docker Compose on a single VPS handles thousands of users comfortably. Kubernetes becomes worth the operational complexity when: you need to scale specific services independently (more backend instances, not more database instances), you're running across multiple geographic regions, your team has a dedicated DevOps/platform engineering function, or you're hitting the limits of vertical scaling on a single machine. Rule: add Kubernetes when the pain of not having it exceeds the pain of managing it.

Interview: "What's the difference between Docker and Kubernetes?"

Docker creates and runs containers. Kubernetes orchestrates containers at scale across a cluster of machines. They work together: Kubernetes uses Docker (or containerd) to run individual containers, then manages how they're distributed, scaled, and healed across many nodes. Concrete difference: Docker Compose starts 3 containers on 1 machine. Kubernetes starts 3 Pods, might place them on 3 different machines, restarts them if they fail, and can scale to 30 Pods automatically if traffic increases. Key Kubernetes value props: self-healing (failed Pods are automatically replaced), horizontal auto-scaling (scale based on CPU/memory metrics), and zero-downtime rolling updates.

14.12

Docker Security Best Practices

Container security is an active responsibility — the defaults are not secure enough for production. Here's the complete security checklist:

PracticeWhy It MattersHow to Implement
Run as non-root Root in container → potential root on host via escape USER node in Dockerfile; securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true in K8s
Read-only filesystem Prevents attackers writing malware if they get in docker run --read-only; use tmpfs for /tmp
Pin base image digests Tags are mutable — :latest can change to a malicious image FROM node:20-alpine@sha256:abc123...
